topics.title: Strategy of Experimentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT727 - DESIGN OF EXPERIMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Strategy of Experimentation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strategy of Experimentation** refers to the systematic, pre-planned sequence of actions and statistical principles adopted by an experimenter to efficiently investigate the effect of one or more input variables (factors) on one or more output responses, in order to draw valid, reliable, and objective conclusions with minimum consumption of resources (time, money, materials).

In the formal KTU context (per *Design and Analysis of Experiments* by Montgomery, the standard text followed across Indian universities), the strategy of experimentation is built upon **three foundational pillars**: **Randomization**, **Replication**, and **Blocking**, often augmented by a fourth modern principle, **Factorial Design**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine you are a **chef** trying to perfect a new recipe for *Kerala Fish Curry*. You want to know how three things affect the taste:
- **Factor A:** Amount of coconut oil (50 ml vs 100 ml)
- **Factor B:** Cooking time (20 min vs 40 min)
- **Factor C:** Spiciness (mild vs hot)

**Bad Strategy (Classical / OFAT):** You first test oil amount by fixing the other two, then test cooking time by fixing the others, then test spice. You finish with **8 separate batches** but **miss all the interactions** (e.g., maybe spice matters more only with longer cooking). You have also wasted ingredients.

**Good Strategy (Factorial):** You plan all **8 combinations at once**, in a randomized order, repeated twice (**16 total runs**). You now know the individual effects *and* the interactions — with greater precision per run.

That switch from "test one thing at a time" to "test a smart pre-planned combination" is the **Strategy of Experimentation**.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU High-Yield Vocabulary)

> [!NOTE]
> These terms are *guaranteed* to appear in KTU Part A questions (2-mark/3-mark).

| Term | Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Factor** | An independent input variable controlled by the experimenter. |
| **Level** | A specific setting or value of a factor. |
| **Response** | The dependent output variable being measured. |
| **Treatment** | A specific combination of factor levels applied in a run. |
| **Run** | A single experimental trial. |
| **Replication** | Repetition of an experimental run under identical conditions. |
| **Interaction** | A situation where the effect of one factor depends on the level of another. |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Major Strategies Compared

### 2.1.1 Classical Strategy — One Factor At A Time (OFAT)

This is the traditional, intuitive approach used before formal statistical design was adopted. The experimenter:
1. Selects a **baseline** (reference) set of conditions.
2. Varies **only one factor** while holding all others fixed.
3. Observes the response, then "resets" and varies the next factor.

**Critical Flaw of OFAT:** It is *mathematically incapable* of detecting **interactions** between factors. Furthermore, it produces a narrow conclusion valid only at the fixed baseline conditions — a problem known as **lack of generalizability**.

### 2.1.2 Modern Strategy — Statistical Design of Experiments (DOE)

The modern strategy, championed by **Sir Ronald A. Fisher** (1920s, Rothamsted Agricultural Station, UK), involves:
1. Varying **all factors simultaneously** across a structured design matrix.
2. **Randomizing** the order of runs to neutralize lurking variables.
3. **Replicating** runs to estimate experimental error.
4. **Blocking** to control nuisance variability.
5. Using **ANOVA** to formally test significance.

## 2.2 The Three (or Four) Foundational Principles

### Principle 1 — **Randomization**
- **Why:** Protects against *lurking variables* (unknown, uncontrolled factors such as ambient temperature drift, machine warm-up, operator fatigue).
- **How:** The order of all experimental runs is determined using a **random number table** or generator, *after* the design has been laid out.
- **Key Insight:** Randomization is the experimenter's insurance policy against bias. It does *not* eliminate the variability; it converts it into a random, unbiased noise component.

### Principle 2 — **Replication**
- **Why:** Allows estimation of the **experimental error** (pure error) and increases the **precision** of treatment mean estimates.
- **How:** Repeat each treatment combination multiple times (denoted $r$, where $r \geq 2$).
- **Statistical effect:** The standard error of a mean shrinks by a factor of $\sqrt{r}$. So 4 replicates give 2× the precision of 1 replicate.

### Principle 3 — **Blocking**
- **Why:** Removes the effect of a *known* nuisance factor from the error term, increasing sensitivity.
- **How:** Group similar experimental units into *blocks* (e.g., same day, same operator, same batch of raw material); randomization occurs *within* each block.

### Principle 4 — **Factorial Design (Modern Inclusion)**
- **Why:** Efficiently estimates main effects *and* all interactions.
- **How:** A **$k$-factor, 2-level full factorial** requires $2^k$ runs; with $r$ replicates, the total is $r \cdot 2^k$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize these formulas — they recur across Part A, Part B, and university exam problems.

| # | Concept | Formula | Meaning of Symbols |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Total runs in $2^k$ factorial | $N = r \cdot 2^k$ | $r$ = replicates, $k$ = factors |
| 2 | Standard error of a mean | $\sigma_{\bar{y}} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ | $\sigma$ = population SD, $n$ = sample size |
| 3 | Standard error of difference of means | $\sigma_{\bar{y}_1 - \bar{y}_2} = \sigma\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n_1} + \dfrac{1}{n_2}}$ | Used in 2-sample comparisons |
| 4 | Replication gain in precision | $\text{Precision gain} \propto \sqrt{r}$ | Doubling $r$ reduces SE by factor $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$ |
| 5 | Coefficient of variation | $\text{CV} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\mu}\times 100\%$ | Normalized measure of variability |
| 6 | Total Sum of Squares (SST) | $\text{SST} = \sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \bar{y})^2$ | $N$ = total observations |
| 7 | Number of degrees of freedom (Total) | $df_{\text{Total}} = N - 1$ | $N$ = total runs |
| 8 | Interaction effect (2-factor) | $(AB)_{ij} = y_{ij} - y_{i\cdot} - y_{\cdot j} + y_{\cdot\cdot}$ | Deviation form of interaction |
| 9 | Blocking factor reduction | $\text{SS}_{\text{Block}}$ is removed from $\text{SS}_{\text{Error}}$ | Increases $F$-ratio power |
| 10 | Randomization assumption | Runs $\sim$ i.i.d. Normal($\mu,\sigma^2$) | Independent, identically distributed, normal |

> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** This framework is used in:
> - **Mechanical:** Optimizing CNC machining parameters (speed, feed, depth) for surface roughness.
> - **Civil:** Concrete mix design — studying cement, water, aggregate ratios.
> - **Electronics:** Yield optimization in semiconductor wafer fabrication.
> - **Biotechnology:** Optimization of fermentation yield (pH, temperature, agitation).
> - **Chemical:** Reactor yield maximization in process industries.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why $\sigma_{\bar{y}}$ Decreases as $\sqrt{n}$

> This is one of the most important foundational results in experimental statistics and is *frequently* tested in KTU Part B questions.

### Step 1 — Setup
Suppose we have $n$ independent observations $y_1, y_2, \dots, y_n$ drawn from a population with **mean** $\mu$ and **variance** $\sigma^2$. The sample mean is defined as:

$$
\bar{y} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i
$$

### Step 2 — Variance of the Sample Mean

By the rules of variance for a sum of independent random variables, $\text{Var}(aX + bY) = a^2\text{Var}(X) + b^2\text{Var}(Y)$. Applying this:

$$
\text{Var}(\bar{y}) = \text{Var}\!\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i\right)
$$

### Step 3 — Pull Out the Constant

Since $\frac{1}{n}$ is a constant, it can be pulled out of the variance operator as a squared term:

$$
\text{Var}(\bar{y}) = \left(\frac{1}{n}\right)^2 \text{Var}\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i\right)
$$

### Step 4 — Apply Independence Assumption

Because the $y_i$ are *independent*, the variance of the sum equals the sum of the variances, and each $\text{Var}(y_i) = \sigma^2$:

$$
\text{Var}(\bar{y}) = \frac{1}{n^2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Var}(y_i) = \frac{1}{n^2} \cdot n\sigma^2
$$

### Step 5 — Simplify and Take Square Root

$$
\text{Var}(\bar{y}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{n}
$$

Taking the square root gives the standard error of the mean:

$$
\sigma_{\bar{y}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The precision of $\bar{y}$ improves only with the *square root* of $n$. To halve the standard error, you must **quadruple** the sample size — a fundamental trade-off in experimental budgeting.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: OFAT vs Factorial Strategy — Side-by-Side

| Evaluation Criterion | OFAT (Classical) | Factorial (Modern) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Number of runs (no replicates)** | $k + 1$ (approx.) | $2^k$ |
| **Detects main effects?** | Yes | Yes |
| **Detects interactions?** | **No** | **Yes** |
| **Optimal region coverage?** | Poor (only baseline region) | Excellent (corners of design space) |
| **Resource efficiency** | Low | High (per-run information density is greater) |
| **Requires statistical analysis?** | No (only descriptive) | Yes (ANOVA mandatory) |
| **Risk of biased conclusions** | High | Low (with randomization + replication) |
| **Recommended by KTU/Montgomery?** | Only for preliminary screening | **Yes — preferred** |

## 3.3 Worked Example: Choosing Between Strategies

**Problem (KTU Pattern):** An engineer wants to study the effect of 3 factors (Temperature, Pressure, Catalyst) on the yield of a chemical reaction. Each factor has 2 levels. Compare the number of runs required by:
(i) OFAT strategy, and
(ii) $2^3$ Full Factorial strategy (with 2 replicates).

### Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1 (OFAT runs):** Fix two factors at a baseline, vary the third across 2 levels. Do this for each of 3 factors. This requires:
- 1 baseline run + 3 × 2 factor-changes = **7 runs** (with no replication).

With 2 replicates: $7 \times 2 = $ **14 runs**.

**Step 2 (Factorial runs):** Use the formula $N = r \cdot 2^k$:
$$
N = r \cdot 2^k = 2 \times 2^3 = 2 \times 8 = 16 \text{ runs}
$$

**Step 3 (Comparison):**
- OFAT: 14 runs, but **misses all 2-factor and 3-factor interactions**.
- Factorial: 16 runs, but estimates **3 main effects + 3 two-factor interactions + 1 three-factor interaction** with formal statistical testing.

**Step 4 (Conclusion):** The Factorial strategy uses only **2 additional runs** but provides vastly more information — the overwhelming KTU-recommended choice.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: The Strategy of Experimentation Decision Tree

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram maps the **decision logic** an experimenter follows when choosing an experimental strategy. This is a typical "board exam favorite" question pattern.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Research Objective] --> B[Identify Response Variable y]
    B --> C[Identify k Factors and their Levels]
    C --> D{Is the Study Exploratory?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Use OFAT or Screening Design like 2 k-1 Fractional Factorial]
    D -- No --> F{Is Interaction Information Needed?}
    F -- No --> G[Use One-Factor Randomized Design]
    F -- Yes --> H[Use Full Factorial 2 to the power k Design]
    H --> I[Apply the Three Principles]
    I --> J[Principle 1: Replication r times]
    I --> K[Principle 2: Randomization of Run Order]
    I --> L[Principle 3: Blocking of Nuisance Factors]
    J --> M[Compute Total Runs N equals r times 2 to the power k]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Execute Runs in Random Order]
    N --> O[Collect Response Data]
    O --> P[Perform ANOVA Analysis]
    P --> Q{Significant Effects Found?}
    Q -- Yes --> R[Draw Conclusions and Optimize]
    Q -- No --> S[Refine Design or Add Factors]
    S --> C
    R --> T[End: Report Results]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Mapping OFAT vs Factorial Run Structure

> This diagram shows the difference in experimental *coverage* of the design space for a 2-factor, 2-level problem.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OFAT[OFAT Strategy 5 Runs]
        A1[Baseline Run] --> A2[Factor A Low]
        A2 --> A3[Factor A High]
        A3 --> A4[Factor B Low]
        A4 --> A5[Factor B High]
    end

    subgraph FACT[Factorial Strategy 4 Runs in Randomized Order]
        B1[Run 1: A High B High]
        B2[Run 2: A Low B High]
        B3[Run 3: A Low B Low]
        B4[Run 4: A High B Low]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation from the diagram:**
> - OFAT visits **5 points** but leaves the corners `A-Low/B-High` and `A-High/B-Low` untested as a *combination*.
> - Factorial visits all **4 corners** of the design space, allowing estimation of the interaction term $AB$.

## 4.3 Block Architecture: Information Flow in a Designed Experiment

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Planning Phase] --> P2[Select Response y]
    P2 --> P3[Select k Factors]
    P3 --> P4[Choose Design Strategy]
    P4 --> P5[Choose Replication r]
    P5 --> P6[Randomize Run Order]
    P6 --> P7[Execute N runs equals r times 2 to the power k]
    P7 --> P8[Record Data]
    P8 --> P9[Compute ANOVA Table]
    P9 --> P10{Compute F ratios}
    P10 --> P11[Compare F with F critical]
    P11 -- F greater --> P12[Reject H0: Factor is Significant]
    P11 -- F less --> P13[Accept H0: Factor Not Significant]
    P12 --> P14[Optimization and Confirmation]
    P13 --> P14
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define the strategy of experimentation. List its basic principles.** *[KTU University Exam – May 2024]*

**Model Answer:**
The strategy of experimentation is a pre-planned, statistical approach to conducting experiments that ensures valid, efficient, and reliable conclusions about the relationship between input factors and output responses. The **basic principles** are:
1. **Randomization** — randomizing run order to neutralize lurking variables.
2. **Replication** — repeating runs to estimate experimental error.
3. **Blocking** — grouping similar experimental units to control nuisance variability.

*(Full 3 marks awarded for naming all three principles with one-line definitions.)*

---

### **Question 2: Distinguish between OFAT and factorial design strategy.** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | OFAT | Factorial |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Factors varied | One at a time | All simultaneously |
| Runs required | $k + 1$ approx. | $2^k$ |
| Detects interactions | No | Yes |
| Efficiency | Low | High |
| Statistical analysis | Not required | ANOVA required |

The factorial strategy is the modern, KTU-preferred approach due to its efficiency and ability to detect interactions.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A: Explain the basic principles of experimental strategy in detail with suitable examples.** *(14 Marks)* *[KTU University Exam – May 2023]*

**Model Solution Outline:**

**(a) Explain the three basic principles with examples — 7 marks**

**Principle 1 — Replication:**
Replication is the repetition of an experimental run under identical conditions. Its purpose is twofold:
- It allows estimation of the **experimental error** (inherent variability of the response).
- It improves the **precision** of estimated treatment means: the standard error of the mean is $\sigma_{\bar{y}} = \sigma / \sqrt{n}$, so precision increases with the square root of $n$.

*Example:* If a chemical yield run yields 75.2 g, a single observation cannot distinguish a true effect from noise. With 3 replicates yielding 75.2, 74.8, 75.5, the **mean $\bar{y} = 75.17$** and the variability gives confidence to the estimate.

**Principle 2 — Randomization:**
Randomization is the process of allocating experimental material and ordering the runs in a completely random fashion. It provides protection against the effect of **lurking variables** — unknown or uncontrollable external influences such as temperature drift, equipment warm-up, or operator fatigue.

*Example:* In a 4-run experiment, instead of running treatments 1, 2, 3, 4 in order, the experimenter uses a random number table and runs them in order say 3, 1, 4, 2. Any drift in conditions is now *spread equally* across all treatments, bias-free.

**Principle 3 — Blocking:**
Blocking is the arrangement of experimental units into groups (blocks) that are similar to one another. Blocking removes the effect of a known nuisance factor from the error term, increasing the sensitivity of the experiment.

*Example:* In testing the strength of concrete cubes, three batches are mixed on Monday, Wednesday, and Friday. To remove "day-of-week" variability, each day's batch forms a **block**, and within each block, the formulations are randomly assigned.

**(b) Explain the modern factorial strategy and its advantages — 7 marks**

The **factorial strategy** involves varying *all* factors simultaneously across a structured set of treatment combinations. For $k$ two-level factors, the **full factorial design** uses $2^k$ runs. With $r$ replicates, the total is $r \cdot 2^k$.

**Key Advantages:**
1. **Efficiency:** Estimates all main effects and interactions using the same experimental runs.
2. **Interaction detection:** Unlike OFAT, it can quantify how factors influence each other.
3. **Generality:** Conclusions hold over the entire experimental region, not just one baseline.
4. **Optimization-ready:** Can be extended to **Response Surface Methodology (RSM)** for finding optimal operating conditions.

**Illustration for $k=3$:** A $2^3$ factorial with 2 replicates uses $N = 2 \times 8 = 16$ runs, estimating 3 main effects + 3 two-factor interactions + 1 three-factor interaction + the error term with $df_{\text{Error}} = 16 - 8 = 8$.

---

### **Question B: What are the limitations of the classical (OFAT) strategy of experimentation? Justify the need for a statistical design strategy.** *(14 Marks)* *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]*

**Model Solution Outline:**

**(a) Limitations of OFAT — 7 marks**

1. **Cannot detect interactions:** OFAT changes one factor while holding others constant. Any two-factor interaction (e.g., temperature × pressure) is *completely invisible* to this approach. Yet in real engineering systems, interactions are *common*.

2. **Result valid only at the baseline:** Suppose the experimenter finds "temperature = 80 °C gives best yield" by holding pressure fixed at 1 atm. The conclusion may *not* hold at pressure = 2 atm. The result lacks **generalizability**.

3. **Inefficient use of resources:** For $k$ factors, OFAT requires $\sim k+1$ runs *per factor*, leading to many runs without cross-information.

4. **No formal error estimate:** Without replication, OFAT provides no statistical measure of uncertainty. The experimenter cannot test hypotheses.

5. **Cannot handle lurking variables:** Without randomization, systematic drift or unknown external factors bias the results.

**(b) Justification for statistical design — 7 marks**

The statistical design strategy addresses every OFAT limitation:
- It uses **factorial structure** to detect interactions.
- It uses **replication** to estimate experimental error.
- It uses **randomization** to defend against lurking variables.
- It uses **blocking** to control known nuisance factors.
- It enables formal **hypothesis testing** via ANOVA ($F$-test).
- It provides **efficient information per run**, reducing experimental cost in the long run.
- It produces conclusions that are **valid across the entire design region**, enabling **optimization** and **response surface modeling**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not** write OFAT as "One Factor At Time" *without* first defining what an interaction is. Examiners deduct **2 marks** for vague answers.
> 2. **Do not** confuse *blocking* with *replication*. Replication = repeat the run identically. Blocking = group similar units but treatments differ within a block.
> 3. **Do not** skip the formula $N = r \cdot 2^k$ when discussing factorial designs — even if the question does not ask for it, including it shows depth.
> 4. **Do not** write replication as "increasing accuracy" — the correct term is "increasing precision." Accuracy and precision are *not* synonyms.
> 5. Always pair **Randomization** with the word **"lurking variables"** in your answer; this is a KTU-evaluator cue phrase.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Summary — read 3 times before exam.**

- ✅ **Strategy of Experimentation** = the pre-planned plan governing how an experiment is conducted to obtain maximum information with minimum cost.
- ✅ **Three classical principles:** Replication, Randomization, Blocking.
- ✅ **Modern addition:** Factorial design (vary all factors simultaneously).
- ✅ **OFAT = One Factor At a Time** — old, inefficient, cannot detect interactions.
- ✅ **Full Factorial $2^k$** with $r$ replicates = $N = r \cdot 2^k$ total runs.
- ✅ **Replication improves precision** as $\sigma_{\bar{y}} = \sigma / \sqrt{n}$.
- ✅ **Randomization defends against lurking variables** — does not eliminate them, only neutralizes them.
- ✅ **Blocking removes the effect of known nuisance factors** from the error term.
- ✅ **Interaction** = the effect of Factor A on response *depends on* the level of Factor B.
- ✅ **Fisher** is the historical father of modern DOE (1920s, Rothamsted).
- ✅ **ANOVA** is the standard statistical tool for analyzing designed experiments.
- ✅ **KTU preference:** Always recommend the factorial strategy over OFAT in design questions.
- ✅ **Key numbers to memorize:** $2^3 = 8$, $2^4 = 16$, $2^5 = 32$.
- ✅ **Degrees of freedom in $2^k$ factorial with $r$ replicates:** $df_{\text{Total}} = r \cdot 2^k - 1$.
- ✅ **Common engineering applications:** CNC optimization, concrete mix design, semiconductor yield, bioreactor yield, chemical reactor tuning.
- ✅ **Mnemonic for 3 principles:** **"R-R-B"** = Replication, Randomization, Blocking (in any order).
- ✅ **Mnemonic for Factorial formula:** **"N equals r two-to-the-k"**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Typical Applications of Experimental Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT727 - DESIGN OF EXPERIMENTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Typical Applications of Experimental Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Design of Experiments (DOE)** is a systematic, statistically rigorous methodology for planning, conducting, analyzing, and interpreting controlled tests to evaluate the effects of one or more independent variables (factors) on a dependent variable (response). As per the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, the study of DOE begins with understanding its **typical applications** across engineering, science, and industrial domains.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**  
> "Experimental Design is a statistical framework that enables the experimenter to extract maximum information with minimum experimental effort by varying multiple factors simultaneously and analyzing the data using Analysis of Variance (ANOVA) techniques."

The phrase **"Typical Applications of Experimental Design"** refers to the standard, widely-recognized real-world use-cases where DOE techniques (such as Completely Randomized Design, Randomized Block Design, Factorial Designs, etc.) are routinely deployed to solve engineering, manufacturing, and research problems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are a **chef in a master kitchen** trying to perfect a signature dish (your *response*). You have multiple ingredients like salt, sugar, chili powder, and cooking time (your *factors*). If you change only one ingredient at a time (the *one-factor-at-a-time* approach), you might never discover that adding sugar AND reducing chili together creates a magical taste — a phenomenon called the **interaction effect**.

**Experimental Design is like a smart recipe optimization system** that:
- Tries multiple ingredient combinations **simultaneously** (factor combinations)
- Records the taste score (response data)
- Uses statistical analysis (ANOVA) to pinpoint which ingredients matter most
- Saves time and money by avoiding unnecessary cooking trials

This same principle is applied in industries — instead of changing one machine parameter at a time (which is slow, costly, and misses interactions), engineers use DOE to **systematically vary multiple parameters at once** and identify the optimal process conditions efficiently.

## 1.3 Why Study Typical Applications?

> [!NOTE]
> Understanding applications BEFORE learning statistical theory is crucial because:
> 1. It builds **engineering motivation** for the mathematical rigor that follows
> 2. It helps students **map KTU exam problems to real-world industries**
> 3. It demonstrates the **economic value** of DOE — often saving lakhs of rupees in manufacturing
> 4. It equips students with **placement-ready knowledge** for roles in Six Sigma, Quality Engineering, R&D, and Process Optimization

## 1.4 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning | Engineering Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Factor** | Independent variable controlled by experimenter | Temperature, Pressure, Speed |
| **Level** | Specific value of a factor | 100°C, 200°C, 300°C |
| **Response** | Dependent variable measured | Yield, Strength, Hardness |
| **Treatment** | A specific combination of factor levels | (Temp=200°C, Pressure=5 bar) |
| **Replication** | Repetition of an experiment under identical conditions | Running the same trial 3 times |
| **Interaction** | Effect of one factor depends on another | Temperature effect varies by pressure |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Typical Applications

The typical applications of Experimental Design can be classified into **five major domains** as recognized by KTU examiners and the broader statistical engineering community.

### Domain A: Industrial Process Optimization

This is the **most frequently tested application** in KTU examinations.

- **Manufacturing Process Tuning:** Optimizing CNC machining parameters (cutting speed, feed rate, depth of cut) to minimize surface roughness.
- **Chemical Process Yield Improvement:** Maximizing product yield in reactors by controlling temperature, pressure, catalyst concentration.
- **Heat Treatment Optimization:** Determining optimal quenching time and temperature for achieving desired hardness.
- **Welding Parameter Selection:** Optimizing current, voltage, and travel speed for maximum weld strength.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Metrics Used:** Yield (%), Tensile Strength (MPa), Surface Roughness (µm - **micrometers**), Hardness (HRC - **Rockwell Hardness C Scale**), Cycle Time (seconds).

### Domain B: Quality Improvement & Six Sigma

- **Reducing Process Variability:** DOE helps identify which factors cause **defects** (DPMO - **Defects Per Million Opportunities**).
- **Robust Design (Taguchi Methods):** Designing products/processes insensitive to noise factors.
- **Six Sigma DMAIC:** DOE forms the **Improve (I)** phase of the DMAIC framework.

### Domain C: Research & Development (R&D)

- **Comparative Studies:** Comparing 3 different catalysts to determine which gives best conversion.
- **Screening Experiments:** Using fractional factorial designs to identify critical factors from a large pool (e.g., 15 factors → 3 critical factors).
- **Response Surface Methodology (RSM):** Building empirical models to predict optimal conditions.
- **New Product Formulation:** Optimizing drug formulations, food products, polymer blends.

### Domain D: Agricultural & Biological Sciences

- **Crop Yield Studies:** Comparing fertilizers, irrigation levels, and seed varieties.
- **Animal Feed Trials:** Randomized Block Designs to test feed formulations.
- **Medical/Clinical Trials:** Block designs to control patient variability (age, gender blocks).

### Domain E: Marketing, Economics & Social Sciences

- **A/B Testing:** Comparing two webpage designs to maximize click-through rates.
- **Pricing Experiments:** Testing price points across regions.
- **Consumer Preference Studies:** Factorial designs to study product features.

## 2.2 The 7-Step DOE Application Roadmap (KTU High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> **Every KTU exam question on "applications of DOE" expects you to mention this 7-step procedure:**

1. **Problem Definition** — Clearly state the objective (e.g., minimize defects, maximize yield).
2. **Response Variable Selection** — Choose the measurable outcome (Y).
3. **Factor & Level Identification** — List independent variables and their test levels.
4. **Choice of Experimental Design** — Select CRD, RBD, LSD, Factorial, etc.
5. **Conducting the Experiment** — Execute trials in randomized order.
6. **Statistical Analysis** — Apply ANOVA, regression, or RSM.
7. **Conclusions & Recommendations** — Identify optimal settings and confirm with confirmation runs.

## 2.3 Mapping Applications to Specific DOE Techniques

| Application Domain | Most Suitable DOE Technique | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| Comparing 4 fertilizers on 5 plots | **CRD** (Completely Randomized Design) | Homogeneous experimental units |
| Testing 3 catalysts in 3 batches | **RBD** (Randomized Block Design) | Batch acts as nuisance factor (block) |
| Studying 4 factors at 2 levels | **2⁴ Factorial Design** | Detects main effects + interactions |
| Optimizing 2 continuous factors | **RSM (CCD/BBD)** | Builds quadratic response surface |
| Screening 8 factors | **2^(8-3) Fractional Factorial** | Reduces runs from 256 to 32 |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are the **statistical backbone** underpinning all DOE applications. Memorize these for guaranteed marks in numerical questions.

$$
\text{Mean of Response} = \bar{Y} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} Y_i}{N}
$$

$$
\text{Total Sum of Squares (SST)} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (Y_i - \bar{Y})^2
$$

$$
\text{Sum of Squares Between Treatments (SSTR)} = \sum_{j=1}^{k} n_j (\bar{Y}_{.j} - \bar{Y}_{..})^2
$$

$$
\text{Sum of Squares Within Treatments (SSE)} = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \sum_{i=1}^{n_j} (Y_{ij} - \bar{Y}_{.j})^2
$$

$$
\text{F-statistic} = F_0 = \frac{\text{MS}_{\text{Treatment}}}{\text{MS}_{\text{Error}}}
$$

$$
\text{Coefficient of Variation (CV)} = \frac{\sqrt{\text{MS}_E}}{\bar{Y}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Treatment Mean Square (MSTR)} = \frac{\text{SSTR}}{k-1}
$$

$$
\text{Error Mean Square (MSE)} = \frac{\text{SSE}}{N-k}
$$

$$
\text{Critical Difference (CD)} = t_{\alpha/2, \, df_E} \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot \text{MSE}}{r}}
$$

$$
\text{Power of Test} = 1 - \beta = P(\text{Reject } H_0 \mid H_1 \text{ is true})
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always state the **null hypothesis** $H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2 = ... = \mu_k$ and **alternative hypothesis** $H_1:$ at least one mean differs. This guarantees full marks in the "definition" component.

### Boundary Conditions & Decision Rules

| Condition | Decision |
|---|---|
| $F_0 > F_{\alpha, \, k-1, \, N-k}$ | Reject $H_0$ → treatments differ significantly |
| $F_0 \leq F_{\alpha, \, k-1, \, N-k}$ | Fail to reject $H_0$ → no significant difference |
| $p\text{-value} < \alpha$ | Reject $H_0$ |
| $p\text{-value} \geq \alpha$ | Fail to reject $H_0$ |

Standard significance level: $\alpha = 0.05$ (i.e., **5% risk**).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The applications of DOE are not academic exercises — they translate directly into **engineering value creation**:

- **Cost Reduction:** DOE has saved industries **millions of rupees** by reducing rejection rates from 15% to under 2%.
- **Time Savings:** Factorial designs compress 16 single-factor experiments into 1 efficient experiment.
- **Quality Enhancement:** Taguchi's Robust Design philosophy has been adopted by Toyota, Ford, Boeing, and Bosch.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** Pharmaceutical companies (regulated by US FDA - **Food and Drug Administration**) must use DOE for process validation.
- **Sustainability:** Optimized processes consume less energy and raw materials, supporting **green manufacturing** goals aligned with KTU's NEP 2020 vision.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the F-Statistic for One-Way ANOVA

This derivation is **frequently asked in KTU Part B questions (14 marks)**. The objective is to show that the F-statistic is the ratio of two independent variance estimates.

### Step 1: Define the Linear Statistical Model

For a one-way ANOVA with $k$ treatments and $n$ observations per treatment:

$$
Y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \epsilon_{ij}
$$

Where:
- $Y_{ij}$ = response from the $j$-th observation of the $i$-th treatment
- $\mu$ = overall (grand) mean
- $\tau_i$ = effect of the $i$-th treatment (deviation from $\mu$)
- $\epsilon_{ij}$ = random error, assumed $\sim N(0, \sigma^2)$

### Step 2: State the Hypotheses

$$
H_0: \tau_1 = \tau_2 = ... = \tau_k = 0 \quad \text{(all treatment means are equal)}
$$

$$
H_1: \tau_i \neq 0 \text{ for at least one } i
$$

### Step 3: Decompose the Total Variation

The fundamental identity of ANOVA (partitioning the total sum of squares):

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{j=1}^{n} (Y_{ij} - \bar{Y}_{..})^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{j=1}^{n} (\bar{Y}_{i.} - \bar{Y}_{..})^2 + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{j=1}^{n} (Y_{ij} - \bar{Y}_{i.})^2
$$

$$
\text{SST} = \text{SSTR} + \text{SSE}
$$

**Explanation:** Total variation in data = Variation BETWEEN treatments + Variation WITHIN treatments.

### Step 4: Expand Each Sum of Squares (Algebraic Form, Computationally Simpler)

$$
\text{SST} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{j=1}^{n} Y_{ij}^2 - \frac{T_{..}^2}{N}
$$

$$
\text{SSTR} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{T_i^2}{n} - \frac{T_{..}^2}{N}
$$

$$
\text{SSE} = \text{SST} - \text{SSTR}
$$

Where:
- $T_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} Y_{ij}$ = total of $i$-th treatment column
- $T_{..} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{j=1}^{n} Y_{ij}$ = grand total of all observations
- $N = k \cdot n$ = total number of observations
- CF (Correction Factor) = $\dfrac{T_{..}^2}{N}$

### Step 5: Compute Mean Squares

$$
\text{MSTR} = \frac{\text{SSTR}}{k - 1}
$$

$$
\text{MSE} = \frac{\text{SSE}}{N - k}
$$

**Degrees of Freedom Logic:**
- SSTR has $k - 1$ df because treatments sum to zero under $H_0$
- SSE has $N - k$ df because each treatment consumes 1 df for its mean
- Total df = $(k-1) + (N-k) = N - 1$ ✓

### Step 6: Construct the F-Statistic

$$
F_0 = \frac{\text{MSTR}}{\text{MSE}} = \frac{\text{MS}_{\text{Between}}}{\text{MS}_{\text{Within}}}
$$

**Logical Justification:**
- Under $H_0$: Both numerator and denominator estimate $\sigma^2$, so $F_0 \approx 1$.
- Under $H_1$: Numerator estimates $\sigma^2 + \dfrac{\sum n_i \tau_i^2}{k-1}$, which is larger.
- Therefore, a **large value of $F_0$** is evidence **against** $H_0$.

### Step 7: Decision Rule

Compare $F_0$ with the critical value $F_{\alpha, k-1, N-k}$ from F-tables:
- If $F_0 > F_{\alpha, k-1, N-k}$ → **Reject $H_0$** (significant difference)
- If $F_0 \leq F_{\alpha, k-1, N-k}$ → **Fail to reject $H_0$**

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (CRD Application)

**KTU Typical Question:** A textile engineer tests 4 different fabric finishes (A, B, C, D) for tensile strength. Each finish is applied to 5 fabric samples. Test at $\alpha = 0.05$ whether mean tensile strength differs among finishes.

### Step 1: Tabulate Hypothetical Data

| Finish A | Finish B | Finish C | Finish D |
|---|---|---|---|
| 35 | 42 | 38 | 31 |
| 37 | 41 | 36 | 33 |
| 34 | 44 | 39 | 30 |
| 36 | 43 | 37 | 32 |
| 38 | 40 | 35 | 34 |

### Step 2: Compute Column Totals

$$
T_A = 35+37+34+36+38 = 180
$$

$$
T_B = 42+41+44+43+40 = 210
$$

$$
T_C = 38+36+39+37+35 = 185
$$

$$
T_D = 31+33+30+32+34 = 160
$$

$$
T_{..} = 180 + 210 + 185 + 160 = 735
$$

$$
N = 4 \times 5 = 20
$$

### Step 3: Correction Factor

$$
\text{CF} = \frac{T_{..}^2}{N} = \frac{735^2}{20} = \frac{540225}{20} = 27011.25
$$

### Step 4: Compute SST (Total Sum of Squares)

Sum of squares of all observations:

$$
\sum Y^2 = (35^2 + 37^2 + ... + 38^2) + (42^2 + ... + 40^2) + ... + (31^2 + ... + 34^2)
$$

For column A: $35^2 + 37^2 + 34^2 + 36^2 + 38^2 = 1225 + 1369 + 1156 + 1296 + 1444 = 6490$
For column B: $42^2 + 41^2 + 44^2 + 43^2 + 40^2 = 1764 + 1681 + 1936 + 1849 + 1600 = 8830$
For column C: $38^2 + 36^2 + 39^2 + 37^2 + 35^2 = 1444 + 1296 + 1521 + 1369 + 1225 = 6855$
For column D: $31^2 + 33^2 + 30^2 + 32^2 + 34^2 = 961 + 1089 + 900 + 1024 + 1156 = 5130$

$$
\sum Y^2 = 6490 + 8830 + 6855 + 5130 = 27305
$$

$$
\text{SST} = \sum Y^2 - \text{CF} = 27305 - 27011.25 = 293.75
$$

### Step 5: Compute SSTR (Between-Treatments Sum of Squares)

$$
\text{SSTR} = \frac{T_A^2 + T_B^2 + T_C^2 + T_D^2}{n} - \text{CF}
$$

$$
\text{SSTR} = \frac{180^2 + 210^2 + 185^2 + 160^2}{5} - 27011.25
$$

$$
= \frac{32400 + 44100 + 34225 + 25600}{5} - 27011.25
$$

$$
= \frac{136325}{5} - 27011.25 = 27265 - 27011.25 = 253.75
$$

### Step 6: Compute SSE (Within-Treatments Sum of Squares)

$$
\text{SSE} = \text{SST} - \text{SSTR} = 293.75 - 253.75 = 40.00
$$

### Step 7: ANOVA Table (Analytical Matrix)

| Source of Variation | Sum of Squares (SS) | Degrees of Freedom (df) | Mean Square (MS) | F-Statistic |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Between Treatments (Finishes)** | 253.75 | $k - 1 = 3$ | $253.75/3 = 84.58$ | $84.58 / 2.50 = 33.83$ |
| **Within Treatments (Error)** | 40.00 | $N - k = 16$ | $40.00/16 = 2.50$ | — |
| **Total** | 293.75 | $N - 1 = 19$ | — | — |

### Step 8: Decision

Critical value: $F_{0.05, 3, 16} = 3.24$ (from F-distribution tables).

Since $F_0 = 33.83 > 3.24$, **we reject $H_0$**.

**Engineering Conclusion:** The four fabric finishes produce **significantly different** tensile strengths. Finish B (mean = 42) is the strongest performer.

## 3.3 Summary of Computational Logic

| Step | Formula | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | CF = $T_{..}^2 / N$ | Remove baseline variation |
| 2 | SST = $\sum Y^2 - CF$ | Total variability in data |
| 3 | SSTR = $\sum T_i^2 / n - CF$ | Variability due to treatments |
| 4 | SSE = SST - SSTR | Residual (error) variability |
| 5 | MS = SS / df | Convert to variances |
| 6 | $F_0 = MS_{TR} / MS_E$ | Test statistic |
| 7 | Compare with $F_{\alpha}$ | Hypothesis decision |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Universal DOE Application Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

This block diagram illustrates the **complete workflow** of any experimental design application — from identifying the engineering problem to confirming optimal solutions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Engineering Problem] --> B[Define Response Variable Y]
    B --> C[List Factors and Levels]
    C --> D[Select Appropriate DOE Method]
    D --> E{DOE Method Choice}
    E -->|Homogeneous Units| F[CRD Completely Randomized Design]
    E -->|Nuisance Variable| G[RBD Randomized Block Design]
    E -->|Multiple Factors| H[Factorial Design 2k or 3k]
    E -->|Optimization Goal| I[RSM Response Surface Methodology]
    F --> J[Conduct Trials in Random Order]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Collect Response Data]
    K --> L[Perform ANOVA Analysis]
    L --> M{F Test Significant?}
    M -->|Yes| N[Identify Optimal Treatment]
    M -->|No| O[Conclude No Difference Among Treatments]
    N --> P[Run Confirmation Experiments]
    O --> Q[Report Findings]
    P --> Q
    Q --> R[Implement in Production]
```

## 4.2 Application Domain Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[Typical Applications of DOE] --> Indus[Industrial Process Optimization]
    Root --> Quality[Quality Improvement Six Sigma]
    Root --> RnD[Research and Development]
    Root --> Agri[Agricultural and Biological]
    Root --> Mkt[Marketing and Social Sciences]

    Indus --> I1[CNC Machining Parameter Tuning]
    Indus --> I2[Chemical Reactor Yield Maximization]
    Indus --> I3[Heat Treatment Process Design]
    Indus --> I4[Weld Strength Optimization]

    Quality --> Q1[Defect Reduction DPMO Control]
    Quality --> Q2[Taguchi Robust Design]
    Quality --> Q3[DMAIC Improve Phase]

    RnD --> R1[Comparative Catalyst Studies]
    RnD --> R2[Screening 8 Plus Factors]
    RnD --> R3[RSM for Process Modeling]
    RnD --> R4[New Product Formulation]

    Agri --> A1[Crop Yield Fertilizer Trials]
    Agri --> A2[Animal Feed Comparison]
    Agri --> A3[Clinical Trial Block Designs]

    Mkt --> M1[AB Testing Web Design]
    Mkt --> M2[Price Point Experiments]
    Mkt --> M3[Consumer Preference Studies]
```

## 4.3 ANOVA Conceptual Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SST[Total Sum of Squares SST] --> SSTR[Between Treatments SSTR]
    SST --> SSE[Within Treatments SSE Error]

    SSTR --> MSTR[Mean Square MSTR]
    SSE --> MSE[Mean Square MSE]

    MSTR --> FRatio[F Statistic F0 equals MSTR divided by MSE]
    MSE --> FRatio

    FRatio --> Comp{Compare with F critical}
    Comp -->|F0 greater than F alpha| Reject[Reject H0 Treatments Differ]
    Comp -->|F0 less than or equal to F alpha| Accept[Fail to Reject H0 No Significant Difference]
```

## 4.4 Mapping Application to Statistical Test

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    App[Industrial Application] --> NumReplications[Equal Replications]
    NumReplications --> Yes1{Yes}
    NumReplications --> No1{No}
    Yes1 --> NormCheck{Data Normal}
    NormCheck -->|Yes| ANOVA1[One Way ANOVA]
    NormCheck -->|No| Kruskal1[Kruskal Wallis Non Parametric]
    No1 --> Welch1[Welch ANOVA Unequal Variances]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: List any six typical applications of Design of Experiments in engineering and industry.**

**Model Answer:**

The six typical applications of DOE are:

1. **Process Optimization in Manufacturing** — Optimizing CNC turning parameters (speed, feed, depth of cut) to minimize surface roughness.
2. **Chemical Process Yield Improvement** — Maximizing product yield by varying temperature, pressure, and catalyst concentration.
3. **Quality Control and Six Sigma** — Reducing process variability and defects using Taguchi's Robust Design philosophy.
4. **Comparative Studies in R&D** — Comparing multiple catalysts, materials, or formulations using ANOVA.
5. **Response Surface Methodology (RSM)** — Building empirical mathematical models to find optimal process settings.
6. **Agricultural Field Trials** — Testing fertilizers, irrigation levels, and crop varieties using Randomized Block Designs.

*(Any 6 relevant applications — full marks)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Q: Why is Design of Experiments preferred over the One-Factor-at-a-Time (OFAT) approach in industrial applications?**

**Model Answer:**

DOE is preferred over the OFAT approach for the following reasons:

| Aspect | OFAT Approach | DOE Approach |
|---|---|---|
| **Interaction Effects** | Cannot detect factor interactions | Detects interactions (e.g., A×B) |
| **Number of Trials** | Requires many experiments | Economical — fewer runs |
| **Cost & Time** | Expensive and slow | Saves cost and time |
| **Optimization** | Sub-optimal results | Global optimum identified |
| **Statistical Validity** | No formal inference | ANOVA-based inference possible |
| **Real-World Relevance** | Single-factor changes are rare | Matches industrial realities |

Hence, DOE is more **efficient, economical, and informative** than OFAT.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**  
> In Part B questions on "applications of DOE", students commonly lose marks by:
> 1. Forgetting to state the **null and alternative hypotheses explicitly** (-2 marks)
> 2. Not mentioning the **degrees of freedom** in the ANOVA table (-1 mark)
> 3. Confusing **SS, MS, and df** notation (-1 mark)
> 4. Skipping the **engineering interpretation** of the statistical result (-2 marks)
> 5. Not drawing conclusions in terms of the **original problem context** (-1 mark)
>
> **Always end with a clear conclusion sentence linking the statistical decision to the engineering application!**

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain in detail any **six typical applications of Design of Experiments** in industrial and engineering scenarios. For each application, mention the **response variable** and the **factors** involved. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An experiment was conducted to compare the **compressive strength (in MPa)** of concrete prepared using **4 different mix designs (M1, M2, M3, M4)**. Each mix was tested using 5 sample cubes. The data are:

| M1 | M2 | M3 | M4 |
|---|---|---|---|
| 32 | 38 | 36 | 30 |
| 34 | 40 | 35 | 31 |
| 33 | 39 | 37 | 29 |
| 35 | 41 | 36 | 32 |
| 31 | 37 | 38 | 30 |

Perform a **one-way ANOVA** at $\alpha = 0.05$ and conclude whether the mix designs differ significantly in compressive strength. Given $F_{0.05, 3, 16} = 3.24$. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Model Answer

The six typical applications of Design of Experiments are:

1. **CNC Machining Optimization**  
   - *Factors:* Cutting speed, feed rate, depth of cut.  
   - *Response:* Surface roughness (µm), Material Removal Rate (mm³/s).  
   - *Application:* Optimize parameters for minimum roughness.

2. **Chemical Reactor Yield Improvement**  
   - *Factors:* Temperature (°C), Pressure (bar), Catalyst concentration (mol%).  
   - *Response:* Percentage yield (%).  
   - *Application:* Maximize product yield with minimum cost.

3. **Heat Treatment Process Design**  
   - *Factors:* Quenching temperature (°C), holding time (hours).  
   - *Response:* Hardness (HRC).  
   - *Application:* Achieve desired hardness in steel components.

4. **Weld Joint Strength Optimization**  
   - *Factors:* Welding current (A), voltage (V), travel speed (mm/min).  
   - *Response:* Tensile strength of weld (MPa).  
   - *Application:* Optimize welding parameters for stronger joints.

5. **Pharmaceutical Tablet Formulation (R&D)**  
   - *Factors:* Binder concentration, compression force, particle size.  
   - *Response:* Tablet disintegration time (seconds), hardness (kP).  
   - *Application:* Develop robust tablet formulations.

6. **Crop Yield Field Trials**  
   - *Factors:* Fertilizer type, irrigation level, seed variety.  
   - *Response:* Yield per hectare (tonnes).  
   - *Application:* Identify best agricultural practices using RBD.

#### Part (b) — Detailed Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Hypotheses**

$$
H_0: \mu_{M1} = \mu_{M2} = \mu_{M3} = \mu_{M4}
$$

$$
H_1: \text{At least one mean differs}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Column Totals**

$$
T_1 = 32+34+33+35+31 = 165
$$

$$
T_2 = 38+40+39+41+37 = 195
$$

$$
T_3 = 36+35+37+36+38 = 182
$$

$$
T_4 = 30+31+29+32+30 = 152
$$

$$
T_{..} = 165 + 195 + 182 + 152 = 694
$$

**Step 3: Correction Factor**

$$
\text{CF} = \frac{T_{..}^2}{N} = \frac{694^2}{20} = \frac{481636}{20} = 24081.80
$$

**Step 4: Compute SST**

Squares of all observations:

M1: $32^2+34^2+33^2+35^2+31^2 = 1024+1156+1089+1225+961 = 5455$
M2: $38^2+40^2+39^2+41^2+37^2 = 1444+1600+1521+1681+1369 = 7615$
M3: $36^2+35^2+37^2+36^2+38^2 = 1296+1225+1369+1296+1444 = 6630$
M4: $30^2+31^2+29^2+32^2+30^2 = 900+961+841+1024+900 = 4626$

$$
\sum Y^2 = 5455 + 7615 + 6630 + 4626 = 24326
$$

$$
\text{SST} = 24326 - 24081.80 = 244.20
$$

**Step 5: Compute SSTR**

$$
\text{SSTR} = \frac{165^2 + 195^2 + 182^2 + 152^2}{5} - 24081.80
$$

$$
= \frac{27225 + 38025 + 33124 + 23104}{5} - 24081.80
$$

$$
= \frac{121478}{5} - 24081.80 = 24295.60 - 24081.80 = 213.80
$$

**Step 6: Compute SSE**

$$
\text{SSE} = \text{SST} - \text{SSTR} = 244.20 - 213.80 = 30.40
$$

**Step 7: ANOVA Table**

| Source | SS | df | MS | F₀ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Between Mixes | 213.80 | 3 | 71.27 | **27.13** |
| Within Mixes (Error) | 30.40 | 16 | 1.90 | — |
| Total | 244.20 | 19 | — | — |

**Step 8: Decision**

Critical value: $F_{0.05, 3, 16} = 3.24$.

Since $F_0 = 27.13 \gg 3.24$, we **reject $H_0$** at the 5% significance level.

**Engineering Conclusion:** The four concrete mix designs produce **significantly different** compressive strengths. Mix design **M2** (mean = 39 MPa) yields the highest strength, and the engineer should adopt M2 for production.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Discuss the role of Design of Experiments in **(i) Six Sigma Quality Improvement**, **(ii) Research and Development**, and **(iii) Response Surface Methodology**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Three brands of lubricating oil (Brand A, Brand B, Brand C) are tested on 4 different cars (used as blocks). Each car tests all 3 brands in random order. The friction reduction data (in arbitrary units) are:

| Car (Block) | Brand A | Brand B | Brand C |
|---|---|---|---|
| Car 1 | 22 | 28 | 25 |
| Car 2 | 24 | 30 | 27 |
| Car 3 | 20 | 26 | 24 |
| Car 4 | 23 | 29 | 26 |

Perform a **Randomized Block Design (RBD) ANOVA** at $\alpha = 0.05$. Given $F_{0.05, 2, 6} = 5.14$ and $F_{0.05, 3, 6} = 4.76$. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Model Answer

**(i) Role of DOE in Six Sigma Quality Improvement:**

Six Sigma's **DMAIC** framework (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control) relies heavily on DOE in the **Improve** phase. DOE helps:
- Identify critical process parameters (X's) affecting the output (Y).
- Reduce **process variability** by finding robust settings.
- Quantify the effect of each factor using ANOVA.
- Achieve defect levels below **3.4 DPMO** (Defects Per Million Opportunities).

Taguchi's Loss Function combined with DOE helps convert quality into measurable economic terms.

**(ii) Role of DOE in Research and Development:**

R&D uses DOE for:
- **Screening experiments** to identify key factors from a large pool (using fractional factorial designs).
- **Comparative experiments** to select the best material, catalyst, or method.
- **Optimization experiments** to find the best combination of factors.
- **Modeling** the relationship between inputs and outputs using empirical regression models.

**(iii) Role of DOE in Response Surface Methodology (RSM):**

RSM is a collection of mathematical and statistical techniques useful for:
- Developing, improving, and optimizing processes.
- Building an empirical model $\hat{Y} = f(X_1, X_2, ..., X_k) + \epsilon$.
- Using **Central Composite Design (CCD)** or **Box-Behnken Design (BBD)**.
- Locating the **optimum operating conditions** of a process.
- Visualizing the response surface in 2D (contour plots) or 3D (surface plots).

#### Part (b) — RBD ANOVA Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Hypotheses**

For Brands: $H_0: \mu_A = \mu_B = \mu_C$ vs $H_1:$ at least one brand mean differs.
For Blocks (Cars): $H_0:$ Cars have no effect vs $H_1:$ Cars have an effect.

**Step 2: Compute Totals**

Row totals (Cars):
- $R_1 = 22+28+25 = 75$
- $R_2 = 24+30+27 = 81$
- $R_3 = 20+26+24 = 70$
- $R_4 = 23+29+26 = 78$

Column totals (Brands):
- $C_A = 22+24+20+23 = 89$
- $C_B = 28+30+26+29 = 113$
- $C_C = 25+27+24+26 = 102$

Grand total: $T_{..} = 89 + 113 + 102 = 304$
$N = 4 \times 3 = 12$

**Step 3: Correction Factor**

$$
\text{CF} = \frac{304^2}{12} = \frac{92416}{12} = 7701.33
$$

**Step 4: Compute SST**

$\sum Y^2 = 22^2+28^2+25^2+24^2+30^2+27^2+20^2+26^2+24^2+23^2+29^2+26^2$

$= 484+784+625+576+900+729+400+676+576+529+841+676 = 7796$

$$
\text{SST} = 7796 - 7701.33 = 94.67
$$

**Step 5: Compute SS Brands (Treatments)**

$$
\text{SS}_{\text{Brands}} = \frac{C_A^2 + C_B^2 + C_C^2}{4} - \text{CF} = \frac{7921 + 12769 + 10404}{4} - 7701.33
$$

$$
= \frac{31094}{4} - 7701.33 = 7773.50 - 7701.33 = 72.17
$$

**Step 6: Compute SS Blocks (Cars)**

$$
\text{SS}_{\text{Blocks}} = \frac{R_1^2 + R_2^2 + R_3^2 + R_4^2}{3} - \text{CF} = \frac{5625 + 6561 + 4900 + 6084}{3} - 7701.33
$$

$$
= \frac{23170}{3} - 7701.33 = 7723.33 - 7701.33 = 22.00
$$

**Step 7: Compute SSE (Error)**

$$
\text{SSE} = \text{SST} - \text{SS}_{\text{Brands}} - \text{SS}_{\text{Blocks}} = 94.67 - 72.17 - 22.00 = 0.50
$$

**Step 8: ANOVA Table**

| Source | SS | df | MS | F₀ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Brands (Treatments) | 72.17 | 2 | 36.08 | **433.00** |
| Cars (Blocks) | 22.00 | 3 | 7.33 | **87.96** |
| Error | 0.50 | 6 | 0.0833 | — |
| Total | 94.67 | 11 | — | — |

**Step 9: Decision**

For Brands: $F_0 = 433.00 > F_{0.05, 2, 6} = 5.14$ → **Reject $H_0$**.
For Cars: $F_0 = 87.96 > F_{0.05, 3, 6} = 4.76$ → **Reject $H_0$** (blocking was necessary).

**Engineering Conclusion:** The three lubricant brands produce significantly different friction-reduction effects, with **Brand B (mean ≈ 28.25)** giving the best performance. The blocking by car was effective — car-to-car variation is statistically significant, justifying the RBD structure.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for "Typical Applications of Experimental Design"**

### ✅ Core Concepts to Memorize

- **DOE Definition:** A statistical methodology to plan, conduct, and analyze controlled experiments for studying cause-effect relationships.
- **Three Pillars of DOE:** Replication, Randomization, Blocking (Local Control).
- **7-Step Application Roadmap:** Problem → Response → Factors/L Levels → Design Choice → Run Experiment → Analyze (ANOVA) → Conclusion.
- **Key Statistical Identity:** SST = SSTR + SSE (Total SS = Between SS + Within SS).
- **F-statistic:** $F_0 = \text{MSTR} / \text{MSE}$.

### ✅ Application Domains to Remember

- **Industrial:** CNC, Chemical, Welding, Heat Treatment.
- **Quality:** Six Sigma DMAIC, Taguchi Robust Design.
- **R&D:** Screening, Comparative, RSM Optimization.
- **Agriculture:** CRD for homogeneous plots, RBD for field trials.
- **Pharmaceutical:** Tablet formulation, drug stability, FDA-compliant validation.
- **Marketing:** A/B testing, pricing experiments.

### ✅ KTU Exam Pitfalls to Avoid

| ❌ Common Mistake | ✅ Correct Practice |
|---|---|
| Skipping the $H_0, H_1$ statement | Always write both hypotheses explicitly |
| Forgetting degrees of freedom in ANOVA table | df for treatments = $k-1$, error = $N-k$ |
| Drawing conclusion without engineering context | Always end with engineering interpretation |
| Confusing "SST" and "SSTR" | SST = Total, SSTR = Between Treatments |
| Using wrong $F_{\alpha}$ values | Recheck numerator and denominator df |

### ✅ Quick Numerical Checklist

1. Compute $T_i$ (column totals) and $T_{..}$ (grand total).
2. Calculate CF = $T_{..}^2 / N$.
3. Compute SST = $\sum Y^2 - CF$.
4. Compute SSTR = $\sum (T_i^2 / n) - CF$.
5. Compute SSE = SST − SSTR.
6. Construct ANOVA table (SS, df, MS, F).
7. Compare $F_0$ with $F_{\alpha}$; draw engineering conclusion.

### ✅ Most Probable KTU Question Types

- **3-mark:** List applications, advantages over OFAT, define DOE.
- **14-mark:** Full CRD or RBD numerical ANOVA with engineering interpretation.
- **Theoretical 14-mark:** Compare DOE with OFAT, explain DMAIC, discuss RSM.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When asked to discuss "applications of DOE", structure your answer by **industry domains** (Industrial, R&D, Quality, Agriculture, Pharma, Marketing). This shows the examiner your breadth of understanding and guarantees full marks under KTU 2024 Scheme's Course Outcome (CO1: Knowledge) mapping.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Principles of Experimental Design: Replication
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Replication** is the independent repetition of an experimental treatment (or condition) under identical or near-identical settings, such that each repetition provides a fresh, independent estimate of the experimental error variance and improves the precision of the treatment mean estimate.

In the formal language of **Design of Experiments (DoE)** as prescribed by Montgomery & Runger (the canonical KTU reference for OEBBT727), replication is the **deliberate, planned repetition of the basic experiment**. Each replication:

1. Involves the **simultaneous application** of all treatments under study on **different experimental units** (subjects, batches, parts, specimens).
2. Generates an **independent** random observation $y_{ij}$ for the $i^{th}$ treatment in the $j^{th}$ replicate.
3. Allows the experimenter to obtain an **unbiased estimate of the experimental error ($\sigma^2$)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Replication ≠ Repetition.** KTU examiners specifically test this distinction. Repetition refers to taking *multiple measurements on the same experimental unit* (e.g., measuring the diameter of one bolt 5 times), while Replication refers to applying the *same treatment to multiple, independent experimental units* (e.g., testing 5 different bolts under the same load).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a quality engineer trying to verify whether a new manufacturing process produces ball bearings with a target mean diameter of **10 mm**.

- **Without Replication (n = 1):** You manufacture **one** ball bearing and measure it. You get 9.98 mm. Is the process good? Is the error large? You simply **cannot tell**, because you have no idea of the underlying variability — your single number confounds "process mean shift" with "random noise".

- **With Replication (n = 5):** You manufacture **five separate ball bearings** using the new process and measure each independently. Now you have a *distribution* of outcomes: {9.98, 10.02, 9.99, 10.01, 10.00}. You can now:
  - Compute the **sample mean** (a more precise estimate of the true process mean).
  - Compute the **sample variance** (an estimate of inherent process noise).
  - Run **statistical inference** (t-tests, ANOVA, confidence intervals).

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Hook for Exams:** Think of replication as "casting a wider net" to sample the population of possible experimental outcomes. Each replicate is one draw from this population. The more independent draws (replicates), the sharper your inference.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in Replication

The key statistical metrics that change with replication level $r$ are:

- **Standard Error of the Mean:** $SE = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{r}}$
- **Degrees of Freedom for Error:** $df_E = N - a = ar - a = a(r-1)$
- **Width of $(1-\alpha)\%$ Confidence Interval:** $\propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{r}}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Standard Error decreases with the SQUARE ROOT of $r$, not linearly.** To halve the standard error, you must quadruple the number of replicates. This is the single most tested relationship in KTU Module 1 questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Threefold Purpose of Replication

Replication serves **three fundamental purposes** in experimental design. These are high-yield KTU Module 1 items.

| # | Purpose | Mechanism | Engineering Utility |
|---|---------|-----------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Reduction of Variability** | Reduces standard error of the mean by factor $\sqrt{r}$ | Sharper estimate of treatment effect |
| 2 | **Estimation of Experimental Error** | Provides $df_E = a(r-1)$ to estimate $\sigma^2$ | Enables ANOVA / hypothesis testing |
| 3 | **Increased Scope of Inference** | Larger sample covers wider population of experimental units | Generalizes results to "process" not "part" |

### 2.2 Statistical Model Framework

Consider a **Single-Factor Experiment** with $a$ treatments and $r$ replicates. The standard linear statistical model is:

$$y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \epsilon_{ij}$$

where:
- $i = 1, 2, \dots, a$ indexes the **treatment levels**
- $j = 1, 2, \dots, r$ indexes the **replicate number**
- $y_{ij}$ is the observed response
- $\mu$ is the **overall (grand) mean**
- $\tau_i$ is the **fixed effect** of treatment $i$, subject to $\sum_{i=1}^{a}\tau_i = 0$
- $\epsilon_{ij} \sim \text{i.i.d. } N(0, \sigma^2)$ is the **random error**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why replication is non-negotiable:** The error terms $\epsilon_{ij}$ are unobservable. Without replication (i.e., $r=1$), there is **no way to estimate $\sigma^2$**, because every observation is fully absorbed by the treatment means. Replication "frees up" degrees of freedom to estimate the noise floor.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula Name | Equation | Description |
|--------------|----------|-------------|
| Standard Error of Treatment Mean | $SE(\bar{y}_{i\cdot}) = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{r}}$ | Decreases with replication |
| Standard Error of Difference of Means | $SE(\bar{y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{y}_{j\cdot}) = \sigma\sqrt{\dfrac{2}{r}}$ | For pairwise comparisons |
| Mean Square Error (MSE) | $MSE = \dfrac{SS_E}{a(r-1)}$ | Unbiased estimator of $\sigma^2$ |
| Error Sum of Squares | $SS_E = \sum_{i=1}^{a}\sum_{j=1}^{r}(y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{i\cdot})^2$ | Within-treatment variability |
| Confidence Interval Width | $W = 2 \cdot t_{\alpha/2, df_E} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{r}}$ | Width $\propto 1/\sqrt{r}$ |
| Sample Size for Half-Width | $r = \left(\dfrac{2 \cdot t_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{d}\right)^2$ | Required replicates for precision $d$ |
| Total Observations | $N = a \cdot r$ | Total sample size |

### 2.4 Why Replication Works: The Intuitive "Why"

> [!TIP]
> **The Central Limit Theorem (CLT) Connection:** When you replicate, you are essentially applying the CLT. The sample mean $\bar{y}_{i\cdot} = \dfrac{1}{r}\sum_{j=1}^{r}y_{ij}$ has variance $\sigma^2/r$. So as $r$ grows, the distribution of $\bar{y}_{i\cdot}$ becomes increasingly concentrated around the true $\mu + \tau_i$.

**Three Quantitative Effects of Replication:**

1. **Precision Enhancement:** Doubling $r$ from $r$ to $2r$ reduces the standard error by a factor of $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$.
2. **Power Enhancement:** With more $df_E$, the $F$-test in ANOVA gains power to detect smaller treatment effects.
3. **Confidence Interval Narrowing:** The $(1-\alpha)\%$ CI for $\tau_i - \tau_j$ narrows, allowing finer discrimination between treatments.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing:** In a **SPC / Six Sigma** context, replication allows estimation of process capability indices ($C_p$, $C_{pk}$).
- **Materials Testing:** Replication of tensile tests on different coupons gives an estimate of material strength variability.
- **Pharmaceutical/Biotech:** Replication of dosage-response trials is mandated by **FDA** for inferential validity.
- **Semiconductor Fabrication:** Replication across multiple wafers is essential because wafer-to-wafer variability dominates within-wafer variability.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Standard Error of the Treatment Mean

**Goal:** Show that replication reduces the standard error of $\bar{y}_{i\cdot}$ by a factor of $\sqrt{r}$.

**Step 1:** Recall the linear model:
$$y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \epsilon_{ij}$$

with $\epsilon_{ij} \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$ independently.

**Step 2:** Compute the treatment mean for treatment $i$:
$$\bar{y}_{i\cdot} = \frac{1}{r}\sum_{j=1}^{r} y_{ij}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the model into the mean:
$$\bar{y}_{i\cdot} = \frac{1}{r}\sum_{j=1}^{r}(\mu + \tau_i + \epsilon_{ij})$$

**Step 4:** Apply linearity (constants come out, sum splits):
$$\bar{y}_{i\cdot} = \mu + \tau_i + \frac{1}{r}\sum_{j=1}^{r}\epsilon_{ij}$$

**Step 5:** Identify the expectation:
$$E[\bar{y}_{i\cdot}] = \mu + \tau_i + \frac{1}{r}\sum_{j=1}^{r}E[\epsilon_{ij}] = \mu + \tau_i$$

since $E[\epsilon_{ij}] = 0$.

**Step 6:** Compute the variance using the **independence** of $\epsilon_{ij}$:
$$\text{Var}(\bar{y}_{i\cdot}) = \text{Var}\left(\frac{1}{r}\sum_{j=1}^{r}\epsilon_{ij}\right) = \frac{1}{r^2}\sum_{j=1}^{r}\text{Var}(\epsilon_{ij}) = \frac{1}{r^2}\cdot r\sigma^2 = \frac{\sigma^2}{r}$$

**Step 7:** Take the square root to get the standard error:
$$SE(\bar{y}_{i\cdot}) = \sqrt{\text{Var}(\bar{y}_{i\cdot})} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{r}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The key derivation result:** $SE(\bar{y}_{i\cdot}) \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{r}}$. This is the most heavily tested formula in KTU Module 1 questions on replication.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Degrees of Freedom for Experimental Error

**Step 1:** Total number of observations:
$$N = a \cdot r$$

**Step 2:** Total degrees of freedom (free variation among $N$ observations):
$$df_T = N - 1 = ar - 1$$

**Step 3:** Degrees of freedom for treatments (constrained by $\sum \tau_i = 0$):
$$df_{\text{Treatment}} = a - 1$$

**Step 4:** Apply the fundamental ANOVA partition identity:
$$df_T = df_{\text{Treatment}} + df_E$$

**Step 5:** Solve for the error degrees of freedom:
$$df_E = df_T - df_{\text{Treatment}} = (ar - 1) - (a - 1) = ar - a = a(r-1)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Consequence:** Replication directly controls $df_E$. With $r = 1$, $df_E = 0$, meaning the error variance cannot be estimated. KTU examiners often pose questions like "Why is $r=1$ inadequate for ANOVA?"

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Increasing $r$ on Key Statistics

Assume a single-factor experiment with $a = 4$ treatments, true error std. dev. $\sigma = 2.0$, and $\alpha = 0.05$.

| Replicates $r$ | $df_E$ | $SE = \sigma/\sqrt{r}$ | 95% CI Half-Width $t_{0.025,df_E} \cdot SE$ | Relative Cost |
|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|
| 1 | 0 | 2.000 | Undefined | $1\times$ |
| 2 | 4 | 1.414 | $2.776 \times 1.414 = 3.926$ | $2\times$ |
| 3 | 8 | 1.155 | $2.306 \times 1.155 = 2.664$ | $3\times$ |
| 4 | 12 | 1.000 | $2.179 \times 1.000 = 2.179$ | $4\times$ |
| 5 | 16 | 0.894 | $2.120 \times 0.894 = 1.896$ | $5\times$ |
| 10 | 36 | 0.632 | $2.028 \times 0.632 = 1.282$ | $10\times$ |
| 20 | 76 | 0.447 | $1.992 \times 0.447 = 0.891$ | $20\times$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Insight:** Notice that going from $r=1$ to $r=2$ more than doubles $df_E$ (from 0 to 4), but going from $r=10$ to $r=11$ only adds 4 to $df_E$ (from 36 to 40). The marginal benefit of replication diminishes — this is the **principle of diminishing returns**, frequently tested in KTU numericals.

---

### 3.4 Sample Size Determination: Required Replicates for a Specified Precision

**Problem:** Determine the number of replicates $r$ required so that the $(1-\alpha)\%$ confidence interval for $\mu_i$ has half-width $d$.

**Step 1:** The half-width formula is:
$$d = t_{\alpha/2,\, df_E} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{r}}$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $\sqrt{r}$:
$$\sqrt{r} = \frac{t_{\alpha/2,\, df_E} \cdot \sigma}{d}$$

**Step 3:** Square both sides to obtain $r$:
$$r = \left(\frac{t_{\alpha/2,\, df_E} \cdot \sigma}{d}\right)^2$$

**Step 4:** Since $df_E = a(r-1)$ depends on $r$, an **iterative procedure** is used:
- Round up $t$-quantile is approximate using $z_{\alpha/2}$ for an initial estimate.
- Refine using the updated $df_E$.

**Worked Example:**

Suppose $a = 3$ treatments, $\sigma = 4$, $d = 2$, and $\alpha = 0.05$.

**Iteration 1:** Use $z_{0.025} = 1.96$:
$$r_1 = \left(\frac{1.96 \times 4}{2}\right)^2 = (3.92)^2 = 15.37 \implies r = 16$$

**Iteration 2:** Compute $df_E = a(r-1) = 3 \times 15 = 45$. From $t$-table: $t_{0.025, 45} \approx 2.014$.
$$r_2 = \left(\frac{2.014 \times 4}{2}\right)^2 = (4.028)^2 = 16.22 \implies r = 17$$

**Iteration 3:** Compute $df_E = 3 \times 16 = 48$. $t_{0.025, 48} \approx 2.011$.
$$r_3 = \left(\frac{2.011 \times 4}{2}\right)^2 = (4.022)^2 = 16.18 \implies r = 17$$

**Converged:** $r = 17$ replicates per treatment.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: The Role of Replication in an Experimental Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hypothesis Formulation] --> B[Define Treatments a]
    B --> C[Choose Replication Level r]
    C --> D{Cost / Precision Tradeoff}
    D -- High budget --> E[Large r = 20]
    D -- Moderate budget --> F[Medium r = 5]
    D -- Low budget --> G[Small r = 2]
    E --> H[Randomized Assignment]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Execute Experiment]
    I --> J[Collect Observations]
    J --> K[Estimate sigma squared via MSE]
    K --> L[ANOVA F Test]
    L --> M{Treatment Effect Significant?}
    M -- Yes --> N[Post hoc Tukey HSD]
    M -- No --> O[Report No Difference]
    N --> P[Confidence Intervals on tau]
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Engineering Decision]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Notice that the choice of $r$ propagates through the entire analytical pipeline. The replication level $r$ affects (a) degrees of freedom, (b) MSE, (c) F-test power, (d) CI width, and ultimately (e) the engineering decision. KTU Module 1 emphasizes that $r$ is a **design-time decision**, not an analysis-time fix.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Replication vs. Repetition Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Multiple Measurements Obtained] --> B{Same Experimental Unit?}
    B -- Yes --> C[REPETITION]
    B -- No --> D[Same Treatment Applied?]
    D -- Yes --> E[REPLICATION]
    D -- No --> F[Different Treatment]
    C --> G[Variance Comes from Measurement Noise Only]
    E --> H[Variance Comes from EE plus Measurement Noise]
    F --> I[Estimates Treatment Differences]
    G --> J[Do NOT count toward replication]
    H --> K[Counts toward replication]
    I --> L[Use contrast and ANOVA]
    J --> M[Include as df within units]
    K --> N[Use df_E = a times r minus 1]
    L --> N
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Replication vs Repetition (KTU Favorite Question):**
> - **Repetition** = Multiple readings on **one** experimental unit → measures instrument/measurement noise.
> - **Replication** = Treatment applied to **multiple distinct** experimental units → measures experimental error (unit-to-unit variability) + measurement noise.
> Replication is the only one that increases $df_E$ for ANOVA.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Cost-Precision Tradeoff Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LowCost[Low Cost Region]
        R1[r equals 2] --> SE1[SE equals sigma over sqrt 2]
        R2[r equals 3] --> SE2[SE equals sigma over sqrt 3]
    end
    subgraph MidCost[Moderate Cost Region]
        R3[r equals 5] --> SE3[SE equals sigma over sqrt 5]
        R4[r equals 10] --> SE4[SE equals sigma over sqrt 10]
    end
    subgraph HighCost[High Cost Region]
        R5[r equals 20] --> SE5[SE equals sigma over sqrt 20]
        R6[r equals 50] --> SE6[SE equals sigma over sqrt 50]
    end
    SE1 --> Diminishing[Diminishing Returns]
    SE2 --> Diminishing
    SE3 --> Diminishing
    SE4 --> Diminishing
    SE5 --> Diminishing
    SE6 --> Diminishing
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight:** Engineers always face a cost-precision tradeoff. The standard error decreases as $1/\sqrt{r}$, but cost increases **linearly** with $r$. The optimal $r$ balances statistical precision against experimental cost. KTU numericals often ask: "Determine the minimum $r$ such that the half-width does not exceed $d$."

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

Define replication in the context of experimental design. Why is replication considered essential for any statistical experiment?

**Model Answer:**

Replication refers to the **independent repetition of a treatment** on multiple experimental units such that each replicate provides an independent observation of the response variable under that treatment.

Replication is essential because it:

1. **Provides an estimate of experimental error variance $\sigma^2$** through the Mean Square Error ($MSE$), without which no formal hypothesis test (e.g., the $F$-test) can be conducted.
2. **Reduces the standard error of treatment means** as $SE = \sigma/\sqrt{r}$, increasing the precision of inference.
3. **Increases the scope of inference**, allowing generalization of conclusions to the broader population of experimental units rather than to a single unit.
4. **Yields degrees of freedom for error** $df_E = a(r-1)$, which determines the critical values of $t$ and $F$ distributions.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

Distinguish clearly between **replication** and **repetition** in experimental design. Illustrate with one example each.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Replication | Repetition |
|--------|-------------|------------|
| Definition | Application of the same treatment to multiple, independent experimental units | Multiple measurements taken on the same experimental unit |
| Variability Captured | Between-unit (experimental error) + measurement noise | Measurement/instrument noise only |
| Effect on $df_E$ | Increases $df_E = a(r-1)$ | Does **not** increase $df_E$ |
| Example | Testing 5 different concrete specimens under the same curing method | Measuring the compressive strength of **one** concrete specimen 5 times |

---

### Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]

**(a)** A single-factor experiment is conducted with $a = 4$ treatments and $r = 6$ replicates per treatment. The estimated standard deviation of the experimental error is $s = 1.5$.

    **(i)** Compute the standard error of each treatment mean.

    **(ii)** Compute the standard error of the difference between any two treatment means.

    **(iii)** If the experimenter wishes to halve the standard error in part (i), how many additional replicates per treatment are required? **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Discuss **three reasons** why replication is fundamental to good experimental design. How does replication interact with **randomization** in ensuring valid inference? **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**(i)** Standard error of treatment mean:
$$SE(\bar{y}_{i\cdot}) = \frac{s}{\sqrt{r}} = \frac{1.5}{\sqrt{6}} = \frac{1.5}{2.449} = 0.6124$$

**(ii)** Standard error of the difference between two treatment means:
$$SE(\bar{y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{y}_{j\cdot}) = s\sqrt{\frac{2}{r}} = 1.5 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2}{6}} = 1.5 \cdot 0.5774 = 0.8660$$

**(iii)** Let the new replication count be $r^*$. To halve the standard error, we require:
$$\frac{s}{\sqrt{r^*}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{s}{\sqrt{r}}$$

Cancel $s$ and rearrange:
$$\sqrt{r^*} = 2\sqrt{r} \implies r^* = 4r = 4 \times 6 = 24$$

Additional replicates required per treatment:
$$\Delta r = r^* - r = 24 - 6 = 18$$

**Additional replicates = 18 per treatment.**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

The three reasons replication is fundamental are:

1. **Estimation of Experimental Error:** Replication provides the within-treatment variability, which is used to construct the Mean Square Error (MSE). MSE is an unbiased estimator of $\sigma^2$, the very quantity required to test whether treatment effects are statistically significant.

2. **Reduction in Standard Error:** Replication increases precision. The standard error of the treatment mean decreases as $\sigma/\sqrt{r}$. For example, doubling the number of replicates reduces the standard error by a factor of $\sqrt{2}$.

3. **Increased Scope of Inference:** With replication, conclusions drawn from the sample of experimental units can be generalized to the entire population of units. Without replication, conclusions apply only to the single unit tested.

**Interaction with Randomization:**

Randomization and replication are complementary. Replication provides multiple, independent observations per treatment, ensuring the error degrees of freedom are nonzero. Randomization assigns these replicates to experimental units in a probabilistic manner, which:

- **Breaks systematic bias** between treatments and nuisance factors (e.g., machine drift, time trends).
- **Validates the i.i.d. assumption** $N(0, \sigma^2)$ for errors $\epsilon_{ij}$.
- **Permits valid inference** under the randomization distribution, even when distributional assumptions are mildly violated.

Together, replication and randomization ensure that the experimental error is both *estimable* (replication) and *unbiased* (randomization). Blocking further refines this by reducing known sources of variability.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]

**(a)** Explain with suitable derivations how the **standard error of a treatment mean** depends on the number of replicates. An engineer wants the 95% confidence interval half-width for a treatment mean to be **at most $d = 1.0$ units**, given $\sigma = 2.5$. How many replicates per treatment are needed (assume $a = 3$)? **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** In a 3-treatment comparison, the experimental error variance is unknown. Describe how replication allows estimation of this variance via ANOVA. Also explain why a single replicate ($r=1$) per treatment makes formal inference **impossible**. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Derivation:**

For treatment $i$ with $r$ replicates, the sample mean is:
$$\bar{y}_{i\cdot} = \frac{1}{r}\sum_{j=1}^{r} y_{ij}$$

Using the linear model $y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \epsilon_{ij}$ with $\epsilon_{ij} \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$ independent:

$$E[\bar{y}_{i\cdot}] = \mu + \tau_i$$

$$\text{Var}(\bar{y}_{i\cdot}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{r}$$

$$\therefore SE(\bar{y}_{i\cdot}) = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{r}}$$

This shows that $SE$ decreases as $1/\sqrt{r}$, hence as $r$ increases, precision increases at a *square-root* rate.

**Sample Size Calculation:**

The 95% CI half-width is:
$$d = t_{\alpha/2, df_E} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{r}}$$

**Iteration 1:** Use $z_{0.025} \approx 1.96$ for initial estimate:
$$r_1 = \left(\frac{1.96 \times 2.5}{1.0}\right)^2 = (4.9)^2 = 24.01 \implies r = 25$$

**Iteration 2:** Compute $df_E = a(r-1) = 3 \times 24 = 72$. From tables: $t_{0.025, 72} \approx 1.993$.
$$r_2 = \left(\frac{1.993 \times 2.5}{1.0}\right)^2 = (4.9825)^2 = 24.825 \implies r = 25$$

**Converged:** $r = 25$ replicates per treatment are required.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Estimation of $\sigma^2$ via ANOVA:**

The Total Sum of Squares partitions as:
$$SS_T = SS_{\text{Treatment}} + SS_E$$

where:
$$SS_E = \sum_{i=1}^{a}\sum_{j=1}^{r}(y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{i\cdot})^2$$

with $df_E = a(r-1)$ degrees of freedom. The Mean Square Error is:
$$MSE = \frac{SS_E}{df_E} = \frac{SS_E}{a(r-1)}$$

It can be shown that $E[MSE] = \sigma^2$, so MSE is an unbiased estimator of $\sigma^2$. This estimated variance then enters the $F$-statistic:
$$F_0 = \frac{MS_{\text{Treatment}}}{MSE}$$

which is compared to $F_{\alpha,\, a-1,\, a(r-1)}$.

**Why $r = 1$ Makes Inference Impossible:**

If $r = 1$, then $df_E = a(r-1) = 0$. Consequently:
- $SS_E = 0$ identically, because there is no within-treatment variability to measure.
- $MSE$ is undefined ($0/0$).
