- The $F$-statistic cannot be computed.
- The critical value $F_{\alpha, a-1, 0}$ does not exist.

Thus, with $r = 1$, the experimenter **cannot distinguish** between treatment effects and random noise — every observed difference between treatment means is fully absorbed by the model with zero residual. **Replication is therefore a prerequisite for valid inferential statistics.**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing Replication with Repetition** (–2 marks): Many students interchange these terms. Replication = different units; Repetition = same unit measured multiple times.
> 2. **Forgetting the Square Root** (–2 marks): Writing $SE \propto 1/r$ instead of $SE \propto 1/\sqrt{r}$. Always show the derivation step $\text{Var}(\bar{y}) = \sigma^2/r$.
> 3. **Using $z$ instead of $t$ without iteration** (–1 mark): When $\sigma$ is estimated from pilot data, the $t$-distribution must be used with iterative refinement of $df_E$.
> 4. **Ignoring $df_E = a(r-1)$ formula** (–1 mark): Many students write $df_E = N$ instead of $a(r-1)$.
> 5. **Stating $r=1$ is "less useful" instead of "impossible"** (–1 mark): Use the precise language — ANOVA cannot be performed at $r=1$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Replication:**

- ✅ **Replication = independent repetition of treatment on multiple, distinct experimental units.** NOT the same as repetition.
- ✅ **Three Purposes:** (1) Estimate $\sigma^2$ via MSE; (2) Reduce $SE = \sigma/\sqrt{r}$; (3) Broaden scope of inference.
- ✅ **Standard Error Formula:** $SE(\bar{y}_{i\cdot}) = \sigma/\sqrt{r}$.
- ✅ **Error Degrees of Freedom:** $df_E = a(r-1)$. **At $r=1$, $df_E = 0$ → no ANOVA possible.**
- ✅ **SE of Difference:** $SE(\bar{y}_i - \bar{y}_j) = \sigma\sqrt{2/r}$.
- ✅ **Halving SE requires quadrupling $r$** (square-root law).
- ✅ **Sample Size Formula:** $r = \left(\dfrac{t_{\alpha/2,\, df_E} \cdot \sigma}{d}\right)^2$ — solved iteratively.
- ✅ **Diminishing Returns:** Going from $r=2$ to $r=4$ doubles $df_E$, but $r=20$ to $r=22$ adds only 2 to $df_E$ per treatment.
- ✅ **Replication interacts with Randomization** (validity) and **Blocking** (precision).
- ✅ **Cost-Precision Tradeoff:** Linear cost vs. $\sqrt{r}$ precision gain.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for KTU Viva:**
> **"R-R-R"** — Replication Reduces Root variance. Always recall that $SE$ falls with the **square root** of replicates, not linearly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Randomization and Blocking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT727 - DESIGN OF EXPERIMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Randomization and Blocking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Randomization — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Randomization** is the process of assigning experimental units to treatments (and the order in which the experimental runs are performed) using a random mechanism such that every unit has an equal and independent probability of receiving any treatment. It is the cornerstone of Sir R. A. Fisher's three principles of experimental design (1935).

In statistical terms, randomization **breaks the systematic correlation** that may exist between treatments and uncontrolled nuisance factors (lurking variables), thereby ensuring that the estimates of treatment effects are **unbiased** and possess a valid estimate of experimental error.

## 1.2 Blocking — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Blocking** is the design technique of grouping homogeneous experimental units into similar clusters (called *blocks*) so that within each block the units are as alike as possible, while between blocks they may differ in a known, controllable, but *irrelevant* way. Each treatment is then applied to **one unit within every block**.

When every treatment appears **exactly once in every block**, the arrangement is termed a **Randomized Complete Block Design (RCBD)** — the most widely used blocking structure in engineering and agricultural experimentation.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — A Real-World Story

> [!TIP]
> Imagine you are a quality engineer comparing **four brands of engine oil** (T1, T2, T3, T4) for their effect on fuel efficiency of a car. You have **three test cars** available — a Maruti, a Hyundai, and a Toyota.

> **Without Blocking:**
> You randomly assign each oil to each car in any order. Suppose the Maruti (older car) gets the *best* oil and the Toyota (newest car) gets the *worst*. The "car effect" gets tangled up with the "oil effect" — your results will be misleading.

> **With Blocking:**
> You treat each **car as a block**. Inside every car (block), you test all 4 oils in a **randomized** sequence. The variability *between* cars (different engines) is removed from the experimental error, isolating the true effect of the oil brand. **Within-block randomization** still protects against time-related biases such as engine warming, fuel gauge drift, or driver fatigue.

This simple car-vs-oil story captures the **dual philosophy** of DOE:
- **Block what you can control** (car identity — nuisance variable).
- **Randomize what you cannot control** (time order, ambient temperature fluctuations, operator fatigue).

## 1.4 Why KTU Emphasizes These Two Concepts First

In the **2024 Scheme syllabus**, Module 1 lays the philosophical groundwork before introducing ANOVA calculations. Randomization and Blocking are the **two ideas** without which no valid statistical inference is possible, regardless of how sophisticated the model becomes.

### Terminology Reference (KTU-aligned vocabulary)

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| Experimental Unit (EU) | Smallest entity to which a treatment is independently applied |
| Treatment | The condition whose effect we wish to measure |
| Nuisance Variable | A factor that affects the response but is not of primary interest |
| Block | A grouping of similar EUs |
| Replicate | An independent repetition of a treatment |
| Run | A single measurement of the response |
| Error | Random variation not attributable to treatments or blocks |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Experimental Design (Fisher's Principles)

```
                    ┌────────────────────────────┐
                    │   VALID INFERENCE ENGINE   │
                    └────────────┬───────────────┘
                                 │
        ┌────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┐
        │                        │                        │
        ▼                        ▼                        ▼
   REPLICATION              RANDOMIZATION            BLOCKING
 (Repeat each tx        (Assign & order via       (Group similar units
  independently)         chance mechanism)         into homogeneous sets)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Replication** → provides an estimate of experimental error and increases precision.
> **Randomization** → ensures validity (unbiased estimates + valid error).
> **Blocking** → increases precision by removing known sources of variability from error.

## 2.2 Purpose & Mechanics of Randomization

**Why randomize?**
1. Validates the assumption of **independence of errors**.
2. Eliminates **systematic bias** from confounding with time/order effects.
3. Allows construction of an **unbiased estimate** of experimental error.
4. Justifies the use of **F-tests and t-tests** (whose validity depends on random allocation).

**Common randomization procedures:**
- **Toss of fair coins / dice** — for small experiments.
- **Random number tables** (Fisher-Yates, Tippet's tables).
- **Software RNG** — `RANDOM()`, `RANDBETWEEN()`, `rand()` functions in Excel/Python.

> [!WARNING]
> KTU Pitfall: Randomization refers to **both** the **allocation** of treatments to units **and** the **order** in which experimental runs are conducted. Students frequently randomize only one of these — losing marks.

## 2.3 Purpose & Mechanics of Blocking

**When to block?**
- When the experimental material is **heterogeneous** (different batches, machines, days, operators, raw-material lots).
- When a known nuisance factor (e.g., shift, ambient humidity, raw-material supplier) can be held constant within a block.

**Types of Blocking Structures:**

| Block Type | Description | When Used |
|------------|-------------|-----------|
| **Complete Block** | Every treatment appears once in every block | Small number of treatments, ample units per block |
| **Incomplete Block** (BIBD, PBIBD) | Only a subset of treatments per block | Many treatments; limited units per block |
| **Balanced Incomplete Block (BIBD)** | Every pair of treatments occurs together in λ blocks | When complete blocking is infeasible |

## 2.4 Linear Statistical Model for RCBD

For a Randomized Complete Block Design with **t** treatments and **b** blocks (each treatment replicated **r** times, where here **r = b** in the symmetric case):

$$
Y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \beta_j + \epsilon_{ij}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
i &= 1, 2, \ldots, t \quad (\text{treatment index}) \\
j &= 1, 2, \ldots, b \quad (\text{block index}) \\
\epsilon_{ij} &\stackrel{iid}{\sim} N(0, \sigma^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

| Symbol | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| $Y_{ij}$ | Observed response of $i$-th treatment in $j$-th block |
| $\mu$ | Overall (grand) mean |
| $\tau_i$ | Effect of $i$-th treatment (with $\sum \tau_i = 0$) |
| $\beta_j$ | Effect of $j$-th block (with $\sum \beta_j = 0$) |
| $\epsilon_{ij}$ | Random error, NID with mean **0** and variance $\sigma^2$ |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Master these formulas — they appear in **every KTU RCBD problem** and account for ~14 marks per question.

| # | Formula | Purpose |
|---|---------|---------|
| 1 | $CF = \dfrac{G^2}{N}$ where $G = \sum_{i,j} Y_{ij}$ and $N = tb$ | Correction Factor |
| 2 | $SS_T = \sum_{i,j} Y_{ij}^{2} - CF$ | Total Sum of Squares |
| 3 | $SS_{Tr} = \dfrac{1}{b}\sum_i T_i^{2} - CF$ | Treatments Sum of Squares |
| 4 | $SS_{Bl} = \dfrac{1}{t}\sum_j B_j^{2} - CF$ | Blocks Sum of Squares |
| 5 | $SS_E = SS_T - SS_{Tr} - SS_{Bl}$ | Error Sum of Squares |
| 6 | $MS = \dfrac{SS}{df}$ | Mean Square |
| 7 | $F_{Tr} = \dfrac{MS_{Tr}}{MS_E}$, $\;F_{Bl} = \dfrac{MS_{Bl}}{MS_E}$ | Test statistics |
| 8 | $\text{SE}(\bar{Y}_{i\cdot}) = \sqrt{\dfrac{MS_E}{b}}$ | SE of treatment mean |
| 9 | $LSD = t_{\alpha/2,\,df_E}\sqrt{\dfrac{2\,MS_E}{b}}$ | Pairwise comparison |
| 10 | $\text{CV (\%)} = \dfrac{\sqrt{MS_E}}{\bar{Y}_{\cdot\cdot}} \times 100$ | Coefficient of Variation |

### Hypothesis Testing (KTU 2024 pattern)

$$
H_0: \tau_1 = \tau_2 = \ldots = \tau_t = 0 \quad\text{(no treatment effect)}
$$

$$
H_1: \text{At least one } \tau_i \neq 0
$$

**Decision rule:** Reject $H_0$ if $F_{calc} > F_{\alpha,\, (t-1),\, (t-1)(b-1)}$.

## 2.6 Engineering Applications

| Field | Block | Treatment |
|-------|-------|-----------|
| Manufacturing | Production shift (Morning/Evening/Night) | Welding current setting |
| Civil Engineering | Different concrete batches | Curing methods |
| Pharmaceutical | Different patient age-groups | Drug dosage |
| Agriculture | Soil fertility zones | Fertilizer brand |
| Software Testing | Hardware configuration | Algorithm variant |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example — Complete RCBD Analysis

> [!TIP]
> **Problem Statement (KTU Exam-style):** A researcher investigates the effect of **4 catalysts** (T1, T2, T3, T4) on reaction yield (%). Because the laboratory uses **3 different reactors** with slightly different temperature-control characteristics, **reactor identity is used as the blocking factor**. The yield values are recorded below. Perform the ANOVA and state your conclusions.

### Step 1 — Lay Out the Data Table

| Treatment \ Block | B1 | B2 | B3 | **Treatment Total ($T_i$)** | **Mean ($\bar{T}_i$)** |
|-------------------|----|----|----|-----------------------------|------------------------|
| **T1** | 25 | 29 | 31 | **85** | 28.33 |
| **T2** | 30 | 33 | 35 | **98** | 32.67 |
| **T3** | 28 | 32 | 30 | **90** | 30.00 |
| **T4** | 32 | 36 | 34 | **102** | 34.00 |
| **Block Total ($B_j$)** | **115** | **130** | **130** | **G = 375** | — |
| **Block Mean** | 28.75 | 32.50 | 32.50 | — | $\bar{Y}_{..}=31.25$ |

### Step 2 — Compute the Correction Factor (CF)

$$
\begin{aligned}
CF &= \frac{G^2}{N} = \frac{(375)^2}{t \times b} = \frac{140625}{12} \\[4pt]
CF &= 11718.75
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Total Sum of Squares ($SS_T$)

Sum of squares of every observation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum Y_{ij}^{2} &= 25^2 + 29^2 + 31^2 + 30^2 + 33^2 + 35^2 \\
&\quad + 28^2 + 32^2 + 30^2 + 32^2 + 36^2 + 34^2 \\
&= 625 + 841 + 961 + 900 + 1089 + 1225 \\
&\quad + 784 + 1024 + 900 + 1024 + 1296 + 1156 \\
&= 11825
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
SS_T = \sum Y_{ij}^{2} - CF = 11825 - 11718.75 = 106.25
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Treatment Sum of Squares ($SS_{Tr}$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
SS_{Tr} &= \frac{1}{b}\sum_{i=1}^{t} T_i^{2} - CF \\
&= \frac{1}{3}\left(85^2 + 98^2 + 90^2 + 102^2\right) - 11718.75 \\
&= \frac{1}{3}\left(7225 + 9604 + 8100 + 10404\right) - 11718.75 \\
&= \frac{35333}{3} - 11718.75 \\
&= 11777.67 - 11718.75 \\
&= 58.92
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute Block Sum of Squares ($SS_{Bl}$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
SS_{Bl} &= \frac{1}{t}\sum_{j=1}^{b} B_j^{2} - CF \\
&= \frac{1}{4}\left(115^2 + 130^2 + 130^2\right) - 11718.75 \\
&= \frac{1}{4}\left(13225 + 16900 + 16900\right) - 11718.75 \\
&= \frac{47025}{4} - 11718.75 \\
&= 11756.25 - 11718.75 \\
&= 37.50
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Compute Error Sum of Squares ($SS_E$)

$$
SS_E = SS_T - SS_{Tr} - SS_{Bl} = 106.25 - 58.92 - 37.50 = 9.83
$$

### Step 7 — Degrees of Freedom

$$
\begin{aligned}
df_T &= tb - 1 = 12 - 1 = 11 \\
df_{Tr} &= t - 1 = 4 - 1 = 3 \\
df_{Bl} &= b - 1 = 3 - 1 = 2 \\
df_E &= (t - 1)(b - 1) = 3 \times 2 = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 8 — Mean Squares

$$
\begin{aligned}
MS_{Tr} &= \frac{SS_{Tr}}{df_{Tr}} = \frac{58.92}{3} = 19.64 \\[4pt]
MS_{Bl} &= \frac{SS_{Bl}}{df_{Bl}} = \frac{37.50}{2} = 18.75 \\[4pt]
MS_E &= \frac{SS_E}{df_E} = \frac{9.83}{6} = 1.64
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 9 — F-ratios and Decision

$$
F_{Tr} = \frac{MS_{Tr}}{MS_E} = \frac{19.64}{1.64} = 11.98
$$

Critical value: $F_{0.05,\, 3,\, 6} = 4.76$ and $F_{0.01,\, 3,\, 6} = 9.78$.

Since $F_{calc} = 11.98 > F_{0.01,\, 3,\, 6} = 9.78$, we **reject $H_0$ at the 1% level**.

### Step 10 — ANOVA Table (KTU 2024 board format)

| Source | SS | df | MS | F | $F_{0.05}$ | $F_{0.01}$ | Significance |
|--------|-----|----|------|------|----------|----------|--------------|
| Treatments | 58.92 | 3 | 19.64 | **11.98** | 4.76 | 9.78 | **\*\*** |
| Blocks | 37.50 | 2 | 18.75 | 11.43 | 5.14 | 10.92 | **\*\*** |
| Error | 9.83 | 6 | 1.64 | — | — | — | — |
| **Total** | **106.25** | **11** | — | — | — | — | — |

> [!NOTE]
> \*\* indicates significance at $\alpha = 0.01$.

### Step 11 — Inference

> The **catalysts differ significantly** in their effect on reaction yield ($p < 0.01$). The **blocking factor (reactor) is also significant**, confirming that the reactors genuinely differ — blocking was a justified and successful strategy. The grand mean yield is **31.25%**, and **T4 is the best performer** with a mean of **34.00%**.

### Step 12 — Pairwise Comparison (LSD at $\alpha = 0.05$)

$$
LSD = t_{0.025,\, 6} \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot MS_E}{b}} = 2.447 \times \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.64}{3}} = 2.447 \times 1.045 = 2.557
$$

Any pair of treatment means differing by more than **2.56** is statistically significant. Comparing $\bar{T}_4 - \bar{T}_1 = 34.00 - 28.33 = 5.67 > 2.56$ ⟹ **T4 significantly better than T1**.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Randomization Procedure (KTU-board format)

> **Problem:** Assign 4 treatments (T1, T2, T3, T4) randomly to 4 experimental units within a single block.

**Step-by-step method using random number table:**

1. Number the units **1, 2, 3, 4**.
2. Pick a random starting point in the random number table (e.g., row 12, column 5).
3. Read 4 random digits: **4, 1, 7, 3**.
4. Drop repeats and out-of-range digits. Sort ascending: **1, 3, 4, 7** → assign to T1, T2, T3, T4.
5. The first unit (Unit #1) gets T2 (smallest rank goes to T1, second smallest to T2, etc.)

| Unit | Random Digit | Rank | Treatment Assigned |
|------|--------------|------|--------------------|
| 1 | 4 | 2 | T2 |
| 2 | 1 | 1 | T1 |
| 3 | 7 | 4 | T4 |
| 4 | 3 | 3 | T3 |

**Order of running:** T2 → T1 → T4 → T3.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Fisher's Three Principles — Knowledge Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Valid Statistical Inference"] --> B["Replication"]
    A --> C["Randomization"]
    A --> D["Blocking Local Control"]
    B --> B1["Independent repetition of treatments"]
    B --> B2["Provides estimate of MS Error"]
    B --> B3["Increases precision"]
    C --> C1["Random allocation of treatments to units"]
    C --> C2["Random order of experimental runs"]
    C --> C3["Ensures unbiased error estimate"]
    C --> C4["Validates F test assumptions"]
    D --> D1["Group similar experimental units"]
    D --> D2["Remove nuisance variation from error"]
    D --> D3["Increases precision via MS Block"]
    D --> D4["Common types RCBD BIBD"]
```

## 4.2 RCBD Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Block1
        direction LR
        B1T1["Unit 1.1 T1"] --> B1T2["Unit 1.2 T2"]
        B1T2 --> B1T3["Unit 1.3 T3"]
        B1T3 --> B1T4["Unit 1.4 T4"]
    end
    subgraph Block2
        direction LR
        B2T1["Unit 2.1 T1"] --> B2T2["Unit 2.2 T2"]
        B2T2 --> B2T3["Unit 2.3 T3"]
        B2T3 --> B2T4["Unit 2.4 T4"]
    end
    subgraph Block3
        direction LR
        B3T1["Unit 3.1 T1"] --> B3T2["Unit 3.2 T2"]
        B3T2 --> B3T3["Unit 3.3 T3"]
        B3T3 --> B3T4["Unit 3.4 T4"]
    end
    Block1 --> ANALYSIS["ANOVA Computation"]
    Block2 --> ANALYSIS
    Block3 --> ANALYSIS
    ANALYSIS --> INFER["Inference on Tr effects"]
    ANALYSIS --> INFER2["Inference on Block effects"]
    ANALYSIS --> INFER3["Estimate MS Error"]
```

## 4.3 ANOVA Computation Cascade

```mermaid
graph TD
    RAW["Raw Data Matrix t by b"] --> CF["Compute CF G squared / N"]
    RAW --> SY2["Sum of Y squared ij"]
    RAW --> TI["Treatment Totals T i"]
    RAW --> BJ["Block Totals B j"]
    SY2 --> SST["SS Total = SumY2 minus CF"]
    TI --> SSTR["SS Treatments = Sum Ti2 by b minus CF"]
    BJ --> SSBL["SS Blocks = Sum Bj2 by t minus CF"]
    SST --> SSE["SS Error = SS Total minus SS Tr minus SS Bl"]
    SSTR --> MSTR["MS Tr = SS Tr divided by t minus 1"]
    SSBL --> MSBL["MS Bl = SS Bl divided by b minus 1"]
    SSE --> MSE["MS Error = SS Error divided by t minus 1 by b minus 1"]
    MSTR --> FTR["F Tr = MS Tr divided by MS Error"]
    MSBL --> FBL["F Bl = MS Bl divided by MS Error"]
    FTR --> DEC["Compare with F table alpha t minus 1 dfE"]
    DEC --> OUT["Reject H0 or Accept H0"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 marks each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between Randomization and Replication in the context of Design of Experiments.**

**Model Answer:**
| Aspect | Randomization | Replication |
|--------|---------------|-------------|
| **Definition** | Allocation of treatments to units and order of runs via a random mechanism | Independent repetition of the same treatment on multiple units |
| **Primary Purpose** | Ensures **validity** (unbiased estimates + valid error term) | Ensures **precision** (reduces variance of treatment means and provides an estimate of error) |
| **What it controls** | Systematic/lurking biases | Sampling variability |
| **Method** | Random number tables, RNG software, dice | Multiple experimental units per treatment |

Randomization is about *how* treatments are assigned; replication is about *how many* times each treatment is applied.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**State the linear statistical model for a Randomized Complete Block Design. Explain the role of each term.**

**Model Answer:**
$$
Y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \beta_j + \epsilon_{ij}
$$

| Term | Role |
|------|------|
| $Y_{ij}$ | Observed response of the $i$-th treatment in the $j$-th block |
| $\mu$ | Overall (grand) mean response across all treatments and blocks |
| $\tau_i$ | Effect of the $i$-th treatment (deviation from $\mu$ due to treatment) |
| $\beta_j$ | Effect of the $j$-th block (deviation from $\mu$ due to blocking) |
| $\epsilon_{ij}$ | Random error, $\text{NID}(0, \sigma^2)$ |

Assumptions: additivity of effects, normality of errors, independence, homogeneity of variances.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 marks — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]

#### **Part (a) — 7 marks**
**Explain the principles of randomization and blocking in the Design of Experiments. Why are they considered essential for valid statistical inference?**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Randomization** is the foundation of statistical validity. By allocating treatments to units and ordering runs through a random mechanism, we ensure that:
- The estimates of treatment means are **unbiased** (no systematic over- or under-estimation).
- The **experimental errors are independent** and satisfy the assumptions of the F-test.
- **Lurking variables** (humidity drift, operator fatigue, machine warm-up) get *averaged out* across treatments rather than being confounded with them.

**Blocking** is the precision-enhancement technique. By grouping similar experimental units into homogeneous blocks, the variability **between blocks** is removed from the **error sum of squares** and quantified separately as the block sum of squares. This yields a smaller MS_E, larger F-ratio, and hence higher statistical power.

**Together**, randomization and blocking satisfy **two complementary needs**:
1. Randomization ⟹ *validity* (no bias, valid error).
2. Blocking ⟹ *precision* (lower error variance).

Without these two principles, any conclusion drawn from an experiment is at risk of being misleading — even with replication.

#### **Part (b) — 7 marks**
**An experiment compares 4 rubber compounds (A, B, C, D) for abrasion resistance. Three batches of raw material are used as blocks. Data (in suitable units):**

| Compound \ Block | B1 | B2 | B3 |
|------------------|----|----|-----|
| A | 65 | 60 | 70 |
| B | 75 | 70 | 78 |
| C | 70 | 65 | 72 |
| D | 80 | 75 | 85 |

**Perform the ANOVA and test whether the compounds differ significantly at $\alpha = 0.05$.**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Totals:
- Treatment totals: A = 195, B = 223, C = 207, D = 240; G = 865.
- Block totals: B1 = 290, B2 = 270, B3 = 305.

**Step 2:** $CF = \dfrac{865^2}{12} = \dfrac{748225}{12} = 62352.08$.

**Step 3:**
$$
\sum Y^2 = 65^2 + 60^2 + 70^2 + 75^2 + 70^2 + 78^2 + 70^2 + 65^2 + 72^2 + 80^2 + 75^2 + 85^2
$$
$$
= 4225 + 3600 + 4900 + 5625 + 4900 + 6084 + 4900 + 4225 + 5184 + 6400 + 5625 + 7225 = 62893
$$

**Step 4:**
$$
SS_T = 62893 - 62352.08 = 540.92
$$

$$
SS_{Tr} = \frac{195^2 + 223^2 + 207^2 + 240^2}{3} - 62352.08 = \frac{38025 + 49729 + 42849 + 57600}{3} - 62352.08
$$
$$
= \frac{188203}{3} - 62352.08 = 62734.33 - 62352.08 = 382.25
$$

$$
SS_{Bl} = \frac{290^2 + 270^2 + 305^2}{4} - 62352.08 = \frac{84100 + 72900 + 93025}{4} - 62352.08
$$
$$
= \frac{250025}{4} - 62352.08 = 62506.25 - 62352.08 = 154.17
$$

**Step 5:**
$$
SS_E = 540.92 - 382.25 - 154.17 = 4.50
$$

**Step 6:** ANOVA Table

| Source | SS | df | MS | F |
|--------|------|----|------|------|
| Treatments | 382.25 | 3 | 127.42 | **169.89** |
| Blocks | 154.17 | 2 | 77.08 | 102.78 |
| Error | 4.50 | 6 | 0.75 | — |
| **Total** | **540.92** | **11** | | |

**Step 7:** Critical value $F_{0.05,\,3,\,6} = 4.76$. Since $F_{calc} = 169.89 \gg 4.76$, we **reject $H_0$**.

**Conclusion:** The 4 rubber compounds differ significantly in their abrasion resistance. Compound **D** is the best (mean = 80) and Compound **A** is the worst (mean = 65).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]

#### **Part (a) — 7 marks**
**What is blocking? Discuss the various types of blocking designs with examples.**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Blocking** is the grouping of experimental units into sets (blocks) such that units within a block are homogeneous, while units between blocks may be heterogeneous. Each treatment is then applied to one or more units within each block.

**Types of Blocking Designs:**

1. **Randomized Complete Block Design (RCBD):** Every treatment appears **exactly once** in every block.
   - *Example:* Comparing 4 fertilizers on wheat yield using 5 different soil-type fields as blocks.

2. **Balanced Incomplete Block Design (BIBD):** Each block contains only **k < t** treatments, but every pair of treatments occurs together in exactly **λ** blocks.
   - *Example:* A taste-testing panel can only evaluate 3 out of 7 beverages in a single session; each pair of beverages is compared the same number of times.

3. **Partially Balanced Incomplete Block Design (PBIBD):** A relaxed version of BIBD where treatment pairs occur with **two or more** different association classes.

4. **Latin Square Design (LSD):** A special 2-way blocking where rows and columns both represent blocking factors.
   - *Example:* Comparing 5 machine settings using 5 operators and 5 days as two blocking factors.

5. **Graeco-Latin Square:** Extension of LSD using **three** blocking factors, each at the same level as the number of treatments.

#### **Part (b) — 7 marks**
**Describe in detail the procedure of randomization using random number tables for allocating 6 treatments to 6 experimental units within a block.**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Label the experimental units** with serial numbers: **1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6**.

**Step 2 — Choose a random starting point** in Tippet's or Fisher-Yates random number table (a common KTU-board convention). Suppose we begin at **Row 14, Column 3**.

**Step 3 — Read two-digit random numbers** until 6 distinct digits in the range 1–6 are obtained:
Random sequence encountered: **47, 12, 38, 25, 51, 09, 64, 33, 47 (repeat), 18, 52, 06, 41**
Unique valid digits (ignoring repeats, >6, or 00): **12, 38, 25, 51, 09, 18** — wait, 38 > 6 is invalid; let me re-extract: **2, 5, 4, 1, 6, 3**.

**Step 4 — Rank the random digits** in ascending order:

| Unit | Random Digit | Rank | Treatment Assigned |
|------|--------------|------|--------------------|
| 1 | 2 | 2 | T2 |
| 2 | 5 | 4 | T4 |
| 3 | 4 | 3 | T3 |
| 4 | 1 | 1 | T1 |
| 5 | 6 | 6 | T6 |
| 6 | 3 | 5 | T5 |

**Step 5 — Run order** is now randomized: **T2 → T4 → T3 → T1 → T6 → T5**.

> This randomization procedure is **reproducible** (a crucial KTU-board requirement) if the random starting position is documented. Each unit has an equal probability $\left(\tfrac{1}{6!}\right)$ of receiving any particular sequence of treatments.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (read carefully):**
>
> 1. **Confusing RCBD with CRD:** In CRD (Completely Randomized Design), there is **no block factor**. If a problem mentions blocks, the model must include $\beta_j$.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract CF:** Many students compute sums of squares but forget to subtract the correction factor $CF = G^2 / N$. **Every** SS term must be anchored to CF.
> 3. **Wrong df for Error:** In RCBD, $df_E = (t-1)(b-1)$, **not** $tb - t - b$. This single error loses 2 marks easily.
> 4. **Not interpreting the block effect:** Even if the question only asks about treatments, you **must still compute and interpret $F_{Bl}$** — it justifies why blocking was worthwhile.
> 5. **Writing the model without assumptions:** The full RCBD model statement must include $\epsilon_{ij} \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$ and the constraint $\sum \tau_i = 0$.
> 6. **Skipping the LSD step:** When asked for "best treatment", merely reporting the highest mean is incomplete — you must confirm significance via LSD or Tukey's HSD.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Quick-Revision Checklist for Randomization and Blocking**

- **Randomization** ⟹ *Validity* (unbiased estimates, valid error, satisfies F-test assumptions).
- **Blocking** ⟹ *Precision* (reduces error variance, increases statistical power).
- **RCBD model:** $Y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \beta_j + \epsilon_{ij}$ with $\epsilon_{ij} \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$.
- **Constraints:** $\sum \tau_i = 0$ and $\sum \beta_j = 0$ (over-parameterization removal).
- **CF (Correction Factor):** Always compute first as $G^2 / N$.
- **Total SS identity:** $SS_T = SS_{Tr} + SS_{Bl} + SS_E$ — sanity-check your numbers.
- **Degrees of freedom identity:** $(N-1) = (t-1) + (b-1) + (t-1)(b-1)$.
- **F-statistic:** Compare with table value $F_{\alpha,\, t-1,\, (t-1)(b-1)}$.
- **Decision rule:** $F_{calc} > F_{table} \Rightarrow$ reject $H_0$ (treatments / blocks significant).
- **LSD formula:** $t_{\alpha/2, df_E} \sqrt{2 MS_E / b}$ for pairwise comparison.
- **CV (%)** is a dimensionless measure of experimental precision (typically 5–15% is acceptable in engineering experiments).
- **KTU 2024 pattern:** Block factor is **almost always** significant — when computing, expect this.
- **Randomization procedure** must be **documented** (starting point in RNG table or seed value).
- **Blocking is most effective** when $MS_{Block} > MS_{Error}$ — otherwise blocking was unnecessary.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Statistical Concepts: Concept of Random Variable
Probability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT727 - DESIGN OF EXPERIMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Probability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Probability

In the context of **Design of Experiments (DOE)**, **Probability** is the mathematical framework that quantifies the **likelihood of occurrence of a random outcome** arising from an experiment. Formally, if $S$ is the sample space (the set of all mutually exclusive and exhaustive outcomes) of a random experiment, then for any event $A \subseteq S$, the probability of $A$ is a real-valued function $P$ satisfying the **Kolmogorov Axioms (1933)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Probability is a measure (lying between **0** and **1**) assigned to every event in a sample space, expressing the degree of belief or long-run relative frequency with which that event is expected to occur.

Mathematically expressed as:

$$
P(A) = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{n_A}{n}
$$

where $n$ is the total number of trials and $n_A$ is the number of trials in which event $A$ occurred.

---

### 1.2 Key Terminology for KTU Board Exams

| Term | KTU-Standard Definition |
|---|---|
| **Random Experiment** | An experiment whose outcome cannot be predicted with certainty (e.g., tossing a coin, measuring tensile strength). |
| **Sample Space ($S$)** | The set of **all possible outcomes** of a random experiment. |
| **Event ($A$)** | Any subset of the sample space; a collection of one or more outcomes. |
| **Mutually Exclusive Events** | Two events $A$ and $B$ that cannot occur simultaneously, i.e., $A \cap B = \emptyset$. |
| **Exhaustive Events** | A set of events whose union covers the entire sample space, i.e., $A_1 \cup A_2 \cup \dots \cup A_n = S$. |
| **Independent Events** | Events whose occurrence (or non-occurrence) does **not affect** the probability of the other. |

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The Engineering Perspective

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Manufacturing Yield Problem":**
> Imagine a CNC machining cell producing **1000** pistons per shift. Historically, the defect rate is **2%**. If you randomly pick a piston, *what is the chance it is defective?* The answer **0.02** is a probability. In DOE, every measurement (yield, hardness, surface roughness, fatigue life) is treated as a random variable whose behavior is governed by probability laws. Without probability, we cannot separate **signal (treatment effect)** from **noise (random error)** in any experiment.

---

### 1.4 Why Probability is the Backbone of DOE

Every statistical test used in DOE — the **F-test in ANOVA**, **t-test for means**, **Chi-square test for count data**, and the construction of **Confidence Intervals** — is built upon probability theory. Understanding probability is therefore non-negotiable for any KTU engineering student specializing in Industrial, Production, or Quality Engineering.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Kolmogorov Axioms

The entire edifice of modern probability rests on three axioms. For any events $A$ and $B$ defined on sample space $S$:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Axiom 1 (Non-negativity):** $P(A) \geq 0$ for every event $A$.
> **Axiom 2 (Normalization):** $P(S) = 1$.
> **Axiom 3 (Additivity):** If $A \cap B = \emptyset$, then $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)$.

---

### 2.2 Conditional Probability & Independence

**Conditional Probability** is the probability of an event $A$ given that event $B$ has already occurred:

$$
P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}, \quad P(B) > 0
$$

**Two events are Independent** if and only if:

$$
P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)
$$

Equivalently, under independence: $P(A \mid B) = P(A)$.

---

### 2.3 Bayes' Theorem (KTU High-Yield Topic)

Bayes' Theorem is the engine behind **posterior analysis** in DOE and **Bayesian Optimization** of experimental designs.

$$
P(A_i \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A_i) \cdot P(A_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} P(B \mid A_j) \cdot P(A_j)}
$$

where:
- $P(A_i)$ → **Prior probability** of hypothesis $A_i$.
- $P(B \mid A_i)$ → **Likelihood** of observing $B$ given $A_i$.
- $P(A_i \mid B)$ → **Posterior probability** of $A_i$ after observing $B$.

---

### 2.4 Random Variables & Expectation

A **Random Variable (RV)** is a real-valued function $X: S \to \mathbb{R}$ that assigns a numerical value to each outcome in the sample space. Random variables are classified as:

- **Discrete RV**: Takes countable values (e.g., number of defectives in a lot).
- **Continuous RV**: Takes values over a continuous interval (e.g., tensile strength in MPa).

**Expected Value (Mean):**

$$
E[X] = \mu = \sum_{i} x_i \, p(x_i) \quad \text{(Discrete)} \qquad E[X] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x \, f(x)\, dx \quad \text{(Continuous)}
$$

**Variance:**

$$
\text{Var}(X) = \sigma^2 = E[(X - \mu)^2] = E[X^2] - (E[X])^2
$$

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Boundary / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Classical Probability | $P(A) = \dfrac{n_A}{n}$ | Valid only when outcomes are equally likely |
| 2 | Complement Rule | $P(A^c) = 1 - P(A)$ | Always valid |
| 3 | Addition Rule | $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$ | General form |
| 4 | Multiplication Rule | $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A)$ | Chain rule extension |
| 5 | Conditional Probability | $P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$ | Requires $P(B) > 0$ |
| 6 | Independence | $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$ | Mutual and pairwise |
| 7 | Total Probability | $P(B) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(B \mid A_i) P(A_i)$ | Partition $\{A_i\}$ |
| 8 | Bayes' Theorem | $P(A_i \mid B) = \dfrac{P(B \mid A_i) P(A_i)}{\sum_j P(B \mid A_j) P(A_j)}$ | Posterior updating |
| 9 | Mean of Binomial | $\mu = np$ | $n$ trials, success $p$ |
| 10 | Variance of Binomial | $\sigma^2 = np(1-p)$ | Used in $p$-charts |
| 11 | Mean of Poisson | $\mu = \lambda$ | $\lambda$ = rate |
| 12 | Mean of Normal | $\mu$ | Location parameter |
| 13 | Variance of Normal | $\sigma^2$ | Dispersion parameter |
| 14 | Standard Normal | $Z = \dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$ | $Z \sim N(0,1)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** All boundary values like $\mu = 0$, $\sigma = 1$ for the standard normal distribution must be **memorized explicitly**. Board questions frequently test $Z$-transformation.

---

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Application | Probability Tool Used |
|---|---|
| **Quality Control Charts** ($p$-chart, $c$-chart) | Binomial & Poisson distributions |
| **Reliability Engineering** | Exponential & Weibull distributions |
| **Process Capability (Cp, Cpk)** | Normal distribution |
| **Hypothesis Testing in ANOVA** | F-distribution (built from ratios of variances) |
| **Tolerance Stack-up Analysis** | Convolution of independent random variables |
| **Six-Sigma DMAIC Projects** | Normal distribution + $Z$-scores |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Bayes' Theorem from First Principles

**Starting Point:** The definition of conditional probability applied symmetrically to two events $A$ and $B$.

**Step 1 — Write the conditional probability of $A$ given $B$:**

$$
P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the intersection $P(A \cap B)$:**

$$
P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B)
$$

**Step 3 — Similarly write the conditional probability of $B$ given $A$:**

$$
P(B \mid A) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(A)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad P(A \cap B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)
$$

**Step 4 — Equate the two expressions for $P(A \cap B)$:**

$$
P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)
$$

**Step 5 — Isolate $P(A \mid B)$ on the left-hand side:**

$$
P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the Law of Total Probability** to expand $P(B)$ over a partition $\{A_i\}$:

$$
P(B) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(B \mid A_i) \cdot P(A_i)
$$

**Step 7 — Substitute Step 6 into Step 5:**

$$
P(A_i \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A_i) \cdot P(A_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} P(B \mid A_j) \cdot P(A_j)}
$$

This is the **generalized Bayes' Theorem**, and it is the cornerstone of posterior inference in modern DOE.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Expected Value of a Binomial Random Variable

**Starting Point:** A Binomial RV $X \sim B(n, p)$ is the sum of $n$ independent Bernoulli trials:

$$
X = X_1 + X_2 + \dots + X_n, \quad \text{where } X_i \in \{0, 1\}
$$

**Step 1 — Recall the expectation of a Bernoulli RV $X_i$:**

$$
E[X_i] = (1)(p) + (0)(1-p) = p
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the linearity of expectation:**

$$
E[X] = E\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i\right] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} E[X_i] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p = np
$$

**Conclusion:** $\mu_X = np$.

**Step 3 — Compute $\text{Var}(X)$ using independence of $X_i$'s:**

$$
\text{Var}(X) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Var}(X_i)
$$

**Step 4 — Variance of a single Bernoulli trial:**

$$
\text{Var}(X_i) = E[X_i^2] - (E[X_i])^2 = p - p^2 = p(1-p)
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back:**

$$
\text{Var}(X) = n \cdot p(1-p) = npq, \quad \text{where } q = 1 - p
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Standard Normal Transformation

**Starting Point:** Let $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$. We seek a transformation $Z = aX + b$ such that $Z \sim N(0, 1)$.

**Step 1 — Apply the linear transformation property of normal RVs:**

$$
Z = aX + b \sim N(a\mu + b,\; a^2\sigma^2)
$$

**Step 2 — Impose the standard normal constraints:**

$$
a\mu + b = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad a^2 \sigma^2 = 1
$$

**Step 3 — Solve the system:**

From the second equation: $a = \dfrac{1}{\sigma}$.

Substituting into the first equation:

$$
\frac{\mu}{\sigma} + b = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad b = -\frac{\mu}{\sigma}
$$

**Step 4 — The standardized form:**

$$
Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}
$$

This is the **Z-score**, used in every hypothesis test in DOE to convert raw experimental data into standardized units.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example: Applying Bayes' Theorem in a Quality Inspection Context

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU-style):** A factory has two machines, $M_1$ and $M_2$, producing **60%** and **40%** of the output, respectively. The defective rates are $P(D \mid M_1) = 0.02$ and $P(D \mid M_2) = 0.05$. A randomly chosen item is found to be defective. Find the probability that it came from machine $M_1$.

**Step 1 — Identify priors and likelihoods:**

$$
P(M_1) = 0.60, \quad P(M_2) = 0.40
$$

$$
P(D \mid M_1) = 0.02, \quad P(D \mid M_2) = 0.05
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the marginal probability of a defective item (Total Probability):**

$$
P(D) = P(D \mid M_1) P(M_1) + P(D \mid M_2) P(M_2)
$$

$$
P(D) = (0.02)(0.60) + (0.05)(0.40) = 0.012 + 0.020 = 0.032
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Bayes' Theorem for $P(M_1 \mid D)$:**

$$
P(M_1 \mid D) = \frac{P(D \mid M_1) P(M_1)}{P(D)} = \frac{0.012}{0.032} = 0.375
$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{P(M_1 \mid D) = 0.375}$ or **37.5%**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Probability Concepts Hierarchy (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Random Experiment] --> B[Sample Space S]
    B --> C[Events A, B, ...]
    C --> D[Axioms of Probability]
    D --> D1[P(A) >= 0]
    D --> D2[P(S) = 1]
    D --> D3[Additivity]
    C --> E[Conditional Probability]
    E --> F[Multiplication Rule]
    E --> G[Independence Test]
    C --> H[Total Probability]
    H --> I[Bayes Theorem]
    I --> J[Posterior Probability]
    C --> K[Random Variable]
    K --> L[Discrete RV]
    K --> M[Continuous RV]
    L --> L1[Binomial B np]
    L --> L2[Poisson lambda]
    M --> M1[Normal mu sigma2]
    M --> M2[Exponential]
    L --> N[Expected Value E X]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Variance sigma2]
    O --> P[Standard Deviation sigma]
    P --> Q[Z score Standard Normal]
    Q --> R[DOE Hypothesis Testing]
```

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram: How Probability Feeds into the DOE Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Probability Theory] --> S[Statistical Inference]
    S --> A[ANOVA F Test]
    S --> T[Students t Test]
    S --> C[Chi Square Test]
    A --> DOER[Design of Experiments]
    T --> DOER
    C --> DOER
    DOER --> OPT[Process Optimization]
    DOER --> QC[Quality Control]
    OPT --> ENG[Engineering Decisions]
    QC --> ENG
```

---

### 4.3 Conceptual Architecture of a Probability Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RE[Random Experiment E] --> SS[Define Sample Space S]
    SS --> EV[Identify Event A in S]
    EV --> AX[Apply Kolmogorov Axioms]
    AX --> PMF[Discrete: Probability Mass Function p x]
    AX --> PDF[Continuous: Probability Density Function f x]
    PMF --> CD[Cumulative Distribution F x]
    PDF --> CD
    CD --> EX[Extract E X and Var X]
    EX --> IN[Statistical Inference for DOE]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

Define **Sample Space** and **Event** with one engineering example each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Sample Space ($S$)** is the set of all possible outcomes of a random experiment. For example, when a load cell measures the breaking strength of a steel rod, the sample space is $S = \{x : x \geq 0\}$ where $x$ is the force in kN.

An **Event ($A$)** is any subset of the sample space. For the same experiment, the event "the rod breaks above 50 kN" is $A = \{x : x > 50\}$. Each point in $S$ corresponds to one possible experimental outcome.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

State and explain the **three Kolmogorov Axioms of Probability**.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

For any event $A$ in a sample space $S$:

1. **Non-negativity:** $P(A) \geq 0$. Probability is never negative.
2. **Normalization:** $P(S) = 1$. The sample space is certain to occur.
3. **Additivity:** If $A$ and $B$ are mutually exclusive ($A \cap B = \emptyset$), then $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)$.

These three axioms together form the rigorous foundation for all probability calculations used in Design of Experiments.

---

### Part B Question — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** State and prove the **Law of Total Probability**. Explain its significance in a manufacturing process where defects can arise from multiple sources. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A pharmaceutical company uses three suppliers $S_1$, $S_2$, $S_3$ providing **50%**, **30%**, and **20%** of raw material, respectively. The defect rates are **2%**, **3%**, and **5%**, respectively. A batch is tested and found to be defective. Using **Bayes' Theorem**, find the probability that the defective batch came from supplier $S_1$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Statement:** If $\{A_1, A_2, \dots, A_n\}$ is a partition of the sample space $S$ (i.e., mutually exclusive and exhaustive) and $B$ is any event, then:

$$
P(B) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(B \mid A_i) \cdot P(A_i)
$$

**Proof Sketch:**

For any $i$, by the multiplication rule:

$$
P(B \cap A_i) = P(B \mid A_i) \cdot P(A_i)
$$

Since the $A_i$ are mutually exclusive, the events $B \cap A_i$ are also mutually exclusive. Also, their union is $B$ (because the $A_i$ exhaust $S$). Therefore:

$$
P(B) = P\!\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^{n} (B \cap A_i)\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(B \cap A_i) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(B \mid A_i) \cdot P(A_i)
$$

**Significance in Manufacturing:** In a plant where defects can originate from multiple sources (machine wear, operator error, raw material impurities), the **Total Probability Law** allows us to compute the overall defect probability by aggregating the contributions of each source.

**(b) Solution:**

Given: $P(S_1) = 0.50$, $P(S_2) = 0.30$, $P(S_3) = 0.20$.

Defect rates: $P(D \mid S_1) = 0.02$, $P(D \mid S_2) = 0.03$, $P(D \mid S_3) = 0.05$.

**Step 1 — Total probability of defect:**

$$
P(D) = (0.02)(0.50) + (0.03)(0.30) + (0.05)(0.20)
$$

$$
P(D) = 0.010 + 0.009 + 0.010 = 0.029
$$

**Step 2 — Bayes' Theorem:**

$$
P(S_1 \mid D) = \frac{P(D \mid S_1) \cdot P(S_1)}{P(D)} = \frac{(0.02)(0.50)}{0.029} = \frac{0.010}{0.029} \approx 0.3448
$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{P(S_1 \mid D) \approx 0.3448 \text{ or } 34.48\%}$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Define a **Random Variable**. Distinguish between **Discrete** and **Continuous** random variables with examples relevant to industrial engineering. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The number of defects per square meter in a fabric roll follows a **Poisson distribution** with mean $\lambda = 4$. Find: **(7 Marks)**
   (i) The probability that a randomly selected square meter contains **exactly 3** defects.
   (ii) The probability that it contains **at most 2** defects.
   (iii) The variance of the number of defects.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Definition:** A **Random Variable (RV)** is a real-valued function $X : S \to \mathbb{R}$ that assigns a numerical value to each outcome of a random experiment.

**Discrete RV:** Takes a finite or countably infinite set of values.
*Example:* Number of defective items in a lot of 100 → $X \in \{0, 1, 2, \dots, 100\}$.

**Continuous RV:** Takes values over a continuous interval of real numbers.
*Example:* Diameter of a turned shaft (in mm) → $X \in (0, \infty)$.

The distinction is critical in DOE because the choice of probability mass function (PMF) versus probability density function (PDF) determines the analytical tools used.

**(b) Poisson Distribution:** $P(X = k) = \dfrac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^k}{k!}$, with $\lambda = 4$.

**(i) Probability of exactly 3 defects:**

$$
P(X = 3) = \frac{e^{-4} \cdot 4^3}{3!} = \frac{e^{-4} \cdot 64}{6}
$$

Using $e^{-4} \approx 0.01832$:

$$
P(X = 3) = \frac{(0.01832)(64)}{6} = \frac{1.1725}{6} \approx 0.1954
$$

**(ii) Probability of at most 2 defects:**

$$
P(X \leq 2) = P(0) + P(1) + P(2)
$$

$$
P(0) = \frac{e^{-4} \cdot 4^0}{0!} = 0.01832
$$

$$
P(1) = \frac{e^{-4} \cdot 4^1}{1!} = 0.07326
$$

$$
P(2) = \frac{e^{-4} \cdot 4^2}{2!} = \frac{(0.01832)(16)}{2} = 0.14653
$$

$$
P(X \leq 2) = 0.01832 + 0.07326 + 0.14653 \approx 0.2381
$$

**(iii) Variance of Poisson RV:**

For a Poisson distribution, the variance equals the mean:

$$
\sigma^2 = \lambda = 4
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Students often forget to include the $k!$ denominator in Poisson calculations — partial marks are deducted for this.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** For Bayes' Theorem problems, students apply only the numerator formula without computing $P(D)$ first using the Law of Total Probability. This leads to a wrong answer worth **3 marks** lost.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Misinterpreting "at most" as "exactly". In $P(X \leq 2)$, you must **sum** $P(0) + P(1) + P(2)$ — adding only one term is a **2-mark deduction**.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** Confusing the Binomial mean $np$ with the Poisson mean $\lambda$ when transferring formulas across distributions.
> 5. **Common Mistake 5:** Failing to state the assumption of *independence* when applying multiplication rule for events.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exams:**

- **Definition:** Probability is a function $P : S \to [0, 1]$ satisfying the three Kolmogorov axioms.
- **Complement Rule:** $P(A^c) = 1 - P(A)$ — always use this trick to simplify "at least one" problems.
- **Addition Rule:** Always subtract the intersection; for mutually exclusive events, intersection = 0.
- **Multiplication Rule:** $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A)$; if independent, $P(B \mid A) = P(B)$.
- **Bayes' Theorem:** Two-step process — first compute $P(B)$ via Total Probability, then compute the posterior.
- **Total Probability Law:** Always verify that the events $A_i$ form a partition (mutually exclusive AND exhaustive).
- **Standard Normal Z-score:** $Z = (X - \mu)/\sigma$ — required for every hypothesis test.
- **Binomial:** Mean = $np$, Variance = $npq$; valid when $n$ is fixed, $p$ is constant, trials are independent.
- **Poisson:** Mean = Variance = $\lambda$; used for rare events in a fixed interval (defects, arrivals).
- **Normal:** Symmetric bell curve; ~**68.26%** within $\pm 1\sigma$, ~**95.44%** within $\pm 2\sigma$, ~**99.74%** within $\pm 3\sigma$ (empirical rule).
- **Always state assumptions** (independence, equal likelihood, large sample size) explicitly in derivations — KTU examiners reward this.
- **Practice plotting** sample spaces for compound experiments (e.g., two dice, coin tossed thrice) using tree diagrams.
- **Engineering Context:** Every probability calculation in DOE must end with an *interpretation* — examiners award full marks only when the numerical answer is contextualized.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Probability Density Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT727 - DESIGN OF EXPERIMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Probability Density Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 1 — PROBABILITY DENSITY FUNCTION (PDF)

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Probability Density Function (PDF)** is a function $f_X(x)$ associated with a **continuous random variable** $X$ that completely specifies the probability distribution of $X$. For any two real numbers $a$ and $b$ where $a < b$, the probability that $X$ lies in the interval $[a, b]$ is given by the definite integral of $f_X(x)$ over that interval.

$$P(a \le X \le b) = \int_{a}^{b} f_X(x)\, dx$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Definition (Module 1, Unit 1)**
> A PDF does **NOT** give the probability of a single point. Instead, the *area under the curve* between two limits gives the probability. Mathematically, $f_X(x)$ itself can take values **greater than 1** — only the integral has meaning as a probability.

The PDF is the continuous analogue of the Probability Mass Function (PMF). Because $X$ is continuous, the probability at any exact point is zero:
$$P(X = x_0) = \int_{x_0}^{x_0} f_X(x)\, dx = 0$$

This is why we must always talk about **intervals** when computing probabilities for continuous random variables.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Density of Sand on a Floor"**
>
> Imagine you sprinkle fine sand along a long straight track. The **PDF is the density (thickness) of the sand** at every point on the track.
> - Where the sand is thick → **high probability** that $X$ falls in that region.
> - Where the sand is thin → **low probability** that $X$ falls in that region.
> - The **total amount of sand** (area under the density curve) is always **exactly 1**, because all possible outcomes together must form a *certainty*.

**A more statistical example:** Suppose we record the heights of 10,000 engineering students in a college. Plotting the relative frequencies yields a smooth, bell-shaped curve. That curve is essentially the **PDF of student heights**. The peak of the curve sits near the mean height, and the spread reflects the standard deviation. The area between two heights (e.g., 160 cm and 170 cm) gives the proportion of students whose height lies in that interval.

---

## 1.3 Why PDF Matters in Design of Experiments (DOE)

In DOE, the response variable (e.g., yield of a chemical process, tensile strength of a material) is modelled as a **random variable**. The PDF tells us how that random variable is distributed, which is the foundation of:
- **Hypothesis testing** (t-test, F-test, ANOVA),
- **Confidence interval construction**,
- **Process capability analysis** (Six-Sigma, SPC charts),
- **Statistical modelling of experimental error**.

The most commonly used PDF in DOE is the **Normal (Gaussian) Distribution**, embedded in the central assumptions of the General Linear Model.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📐 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Fundamental Properties of a Valid PDF

For $f_X(x)$ to be a legitimate Probability Density Function, it must satisfy **three necessary and sufficient conditions**:

### Property 1 — Non-Negativity
$$f_X(x) \ge 0 \quad \text{for all } x \in \mathbb{R}$$

**Why?** A probability cannot be negative. Geometrically, the entire PDF curve must lie on or above the x-axis. There can be no "dips" below zero.

### Property 2 — Total Area Equals Unity
$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_X(x)\, dx = 1$$

**Why?** The total probability over all possible outcomes of $X$ must equal 1 (a certainty). This is also called the **normalisation condition**.

### Property 3 — Probability via Integration
$$P(a \le X \le b) = \int_{a}^{b} f_X(x)\, dx$$

**Why?** All probabilities are obtained by integrating the density function over the relevant interval. This is the operational definition of how to use a PDF.

---

## 2.2 Relationship Between PDF and CDF

The **Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF)** $F_X(x)$ is defined as:
$$F_X(x) = P(X \le x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f_X(t)\, dt$$

Differentiating the CDF with respect to $x$ recovers the PDF (Fundamental Theorem of Calculus):
$$f_X(x) = \frac{d}{dx} F_X(x)$$

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | PDF Probability | $P(a \le X \le b) = \int_{a}^{b} f_X(x)\, dx$ | Dimensionless probability |
| 2 | Normalisation | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_X(x)\, dx = 1$ | Total area |
| 3 | Non-negativity | $f_X(x) \ge 0$ | Always true |
| 4 | CDF Definition | $F_X(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f_X(t)\, dt$ | Monotonically non-decreasing |
| 5 | PDF from CDF | $f_X(x) = \dfrac{d}{dx}F_X(x)$ | Derivative recovers PDF |
| 6 | Expected Value | $E(X) = \mu = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x \cdot f_X(x)\, dx$ | Mean of distribution |
| 7 | Variance | $\text{Var}(X) = \sigma^2 = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (x - \mu)^2 f_X(x)\, dx$ | Squared units of $X$ |
| 8 | Std. Deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\text{Var}(X)}$ | Same units as $X$ |
| 9 | Standard Normal | $Z = \dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma} \sim N(0,1)$ | Z-transformation |
| 10 | Normal PDF | $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ | Bell-shaped curve |
| 11 | Uniform PDF on $[a,b]$ | $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{b-a}$ for $a \le x \le b$ | Rectangular shape |
| 12 | Exponential PDF | $f(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}$ for $x \ge 0$ | Memory-less property |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU examiners frequently ask for the **PDF → CDF** conversion and vice-versa. Memorise the symmetry: integration gives CDF; differentiation gives PDF.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | PDF Application |
|---|---|
| **Quality Control (SPC)** | Normal PDF models process variation; $\pm 3\sigma$ limits |
| **Reliability Engineering** | Exponential PDF models time-to-failure of components |
| **Materials Testing** | Weibull PDF models tensile strength, fatigue life |
| **Communication Systems** | Gaussian PDF models additive white noise |
| **Hydrology / Civil** | Log-Normal PDF models rainfall, river discharge |
| **Manufacturing (DOE)** | Normal PDF is the backbone of ANOVA, regression analysis |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL WORKED EXAMPLES

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Expected Value from the PDF

The expected value (or **population mean**) $\mu = E(X)$ of a continuous random variable is the **first moment** of its PDF about the origin.

### Step-by-step derivation:

**Step 1 — Start from the axiomatic definition of expectation.**
For a discrete random variable: $E(X) = \sum_i x_i \cdot p_i$. For a continuous variable, the sum becomes an integral:
$$E(X) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x \cdot f_X(x)\, dx$$

**Step 2 — Justification (intuitive).**
Divide the x-axis into infinitesimally small strips of width $\Delta x$. The probability mass in the strip around $x$ is approximately $f_X(x)\,\Delta x$. The contribution to the mean from that strip is $x \cdot f_X(x)\,\Delta x$. Summing (integrating) all contributions:
$$\mu = \lim_{\Delta x \to 0}\sum_i x_i\, f_X(x_i)\,\Delta x = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x\, f_X(x)\, dx$$

**Step 3 — Final compact form:**
$$\boxed{\mu = E(X) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x\, f_X(x)\, dx}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Variance from the PDF

**Step 1 — Variance is the expectation of the squared deviation from the mean.**
$$\sigma^2 = \text{Var}(X) = E\!\left[(X - \mu)^2\right]$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the continuous expectation operator.**
$$\sigma^2 = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (x - \mu)^2\, f_X(x)\, dx$$

**Step 3 — Alternative computational form (using linearity of expectation).**
Expand $(x-\mu)^2 = x^2 - 2\mu x + \mu^2$:
$$\sigma^2 = \int x^2 f_X(x)\, dx - 2\mu\!\int x\, f_X(x)\, dx + \mu^2\!\int f_X(x)\, dx$$

Apply the normalisation property $\int f_X(x)\, dx = 1$ and $\int x f_X(x)\, dx = \mu$:
$$\sigma^2 = E(X^2) - 2\mu^2 + \mu^2 = E(X^2) - \mu^2$$

$$\boxed{\sigma^2 = E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2}$$

This is the **most exam-friendly formula** — you only need to compute two integrals.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 1 — Uniform Distribution on $[0, 2]$

**Given PDF:**
$$f_X(x) = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{2}, & 0 \le x \le 2 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Task:** Compute $\mu$ and $\sigma^2$.

### Solution (Exhaustive Step-by-Step):

**Step 1 — Verify normalisation.**
$$\int_{0}^{2} \frac{1}{2}\, dx = \frac{1}{2}[x]_{0}^{2} = \frac{1}{2}(2-0) = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Compute the mean $E(X)$.**
$$\begin{aligned}
E(X) &= \int_{0}^{2} x \cdot \frac{1}{2}\, dx = \frac{1}{2}\int_{0}^{2} x\, dx \\
&= \frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{x^2}{2}\right]_{0}^{2} = \frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{4}{2} = 1
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $E(X^2)$.**
$$\begin{aligned}
E(X^2) &= \int_{0}^{2} x^2 \cdot \frac{1}{2}\, dx = \frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{x^3}{3}\right]_{0}^{2} \\
&= \frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{8}{3} = \frac{4}{3}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the variance shortcut formula.**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}(X) &= E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2 = \frac{4}{3} - (1)^2 = \frac{4}{3} - \frac{3}{3} = \frac{1}{3}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:**
$$\mu = 1, \quad \sigma^2 = \tfrac{1}{3} \approx 0.333, \quad \sigma \approx 0.577$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 2 — Solving for an Unknown Constant $k$

**Given PDF:**
$$f_X(x) = \begin{cases} kx^2, & 0 \le x \le 1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Task:** Find $k$, $E(X)$, and $\text{Var}(X)$.

### Step 1 — Apply normalisation to find $k$.
$$\begin{aligned}
\int_{0}^{1} kx^2\, dx &= 1 \\
k\left[\frac{x^3}{3}\right]_{0}^{1} &= 1 \\
k \cdot \frac{1}{3} &= 1 \implies k = 3
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Compute $E(X)$.
$$\begin{aligned}
E(X) &= \int_{0}^{1} x \cdot 3x^2\, dx = 3\int_{0}^{1} x^3\, dx \\
&= 3\left[\frac{x^4}{4}\right]_{0}^{1} = 3\cdot\frac{1}{4} = \frac{3}{4}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Compute $E(X^2)$.
$$\begin{aligned}
E(X^2) &= \int_{0}^{1} x^2 \cdot 3x^2\, dx = 3\int_{0}^{1} x^4\, dx \\
&= 3\left[\frac{x^5}{5}\right]_{0}^{1} = \frac{3}{5}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Variance.
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}(X) &= E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2 = \frac{3}{5} - \left(\frac{3}{4}\right)^2 \\
&= \frac{3}{5} - \frac{9}{16} = \frac{48 - 45}{80} = \frac{3}{80} = 0.0375
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** $k = 3,\ \mu = 0.75,\ \sigma^2 = 0.0375$.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 3 — Standardising a Normal Variable

**Given:** $X \sim N(\mu = 100,\ \sigma^2 = 16)$, so $\sigma = 4$.  
**Task:** Find $P(96 \le X \le 108)$.

### Step 1 — Convert to Standard Normal $Z$.
$$Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma} = \frac{X - 100}{4}$$

### Step 2 — Transform the limits.
$$z_1 = \frac{96 - 100}{4} = -1, \qquad z_2 = \frac{108 - 100}{4} = 2$$

### Step 3 — Express in standard normal form.
$$P(-1 \le Z \le 2) = \Phi(2) - \Phi(-1)$$

### Step 4 — Use symmetry: $\Phi(-1) = 1 - \Phi(1)$.
$$\begin{aligned}
P(-1 \le Z \le 2) &= \Phi(2) - [1 - \Phi(1)] \\
&= \Phi(2) + \Phi(1) - 1
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Look up standard normal table values.
$$\Phi(1) = 0.8413, \qquad \Phi(2) = 0.9772$$

### Step 6 — Final numerical value.
$$P(-1 \le Z \le 2) = 0.9772 + 0.8413 - 1 = 0.8185$$

**Final Answer:** $P(96 \le X \le 108) \approx 0.8185$ (about **81.85%** probability).

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Common PDFs in DOE

| Distribution | PDF $f_X(x)$ | Parameters | Mean $\mu$ | Variance $\sigma^2$ | DOE Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Uniform | $\dfrac{1}{b-a}$ | $a, b$ | $\dfrac{a+b}{2}$ | $\dfrac{(b-a)^2}{12}$ | Random number generation |
| Normal | $\dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ | $\mu, \sigma$ | $\mu$ | $\sigma^2$ | ANOVA, regression |
| Exponential | $\lambda e^{-\lambda x}$ | $\lambda > 0$ | $\dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | $\dfrac{1}{\lambda^2}$ | Reliability testing |
| t-distribution | $\dfrac{\Gamma\!\left(\tfrac{\nu+1}{2}\right)}{\sqrt{\nu\pi}\,\Gamma\!\left(\tfrac{\nu}{2}\right)}\!\left(1+\tfrac{x^2}{\nu}\right)^{-\tfrac{\nu+1}{2}}$ | $\nu$ (d.o.f.) | $0$ | $\dfrac{\nu}{\nu-2}$ | Small-sample t-tests |
| F-distribution | $\dfrac{\sqrt{\tfrac{(\nu_1 x)^{\nu_1}\nu_2^{\nu_2}}{(\nu_1 x + \nu_2)^{\nu_1+\nu_2}}}}{x\, B(\tfrac{\nu_1}{2},\tfrac{\nu_2}{2})}$ | $\nu_1, \nu_2$ | $\dfrac{\nu_2}{\nu_2-2}$ | Complex | ANOVA, F-tests |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS — PDF–CDF RELATIONSHIP & ARCHITECTURE

## 4.1 Functional Flow of PDF and CDF Operations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["CONTINUOUS RANDOM VARIABLE X"] --> B["PDF F OF X"]
    B --> P1["PROPERTY 1 F OF X greater than or equal to 0"]
    B --> P2["PROPERTY 2 INTEGRAL FROM MINUS INFINITY TO INFINITY OF F OF X DX EQUALS 1"]
    B --> P3["PROPERTY 3 PROBABILITY IN A TO B EQUALS INTEGRAL FROM A TO B OF F OF T DT"]
    B --> C["CDF F OF X EQUALS INTEGRAL FROM MINUS INFINITY TO X OF F OF T DT"]
    C --> D["DIFFERENTIATE TO RECOVER PDF F OF X EQUALS D BY DX OF F OF X"]
    B --> E["MEAN MU EQUALS INTEGRAL X F OF X DX"]
    B --> F["VARIANCE SIGMA SQUARE EQUALS INTEGRAL X MINUS MU WHOLE SQUARE F OF X DX"]
    E --> G["SUMMARY OUTPUTS MU AND SIGMA SQUARE"]
    F --> G
    P1 --> H["VALID PDF CURVE"]
    P2 --> H
    P3 --> H
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Application in a DOE Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT["EXPERIMENTAL DATA SET"] --> MODEL["ASSUME NORMAL PDF MODEL"]
    MODEL --> EST1["ESTIMATE MU FROM SAMPLE MEAN"]
    MODEL --> EST2["ESTIMATE SIGMA SQUARE FROM SAMPLE VARIANCE"]
    EST1 --> TEST["HYPOTHESIS TEST T TEST OR F TEST"]
    EST2 --> TEST
    TEST --> DECISION["ACCEPT OR REJECT NULL HYPOTHESIS H0"]
    TEST --> CI["CONFIDENCE INTERVAL CONSTRUCTION"]
    CI --> REPORT["ENGINEERING DECISION AND REPORT"]
    DECISION --> REPORT
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Curve Schematic (ASCII Visualisation)

```
            f_X(x)
              ▲
              │           ╭───╮
              │          ╱     ╲
              │         ╱       ╲
              │        ╱         ╲
              │      ╱             ╲
              │    ╱                 ╲
              │  ╱                     ╲
        ──────┴───────────────────────────────► x
              │  a              b
              │
        TOTAL AREA UNDER CURVE = 1
        AREA FROM a TO b = P(a ≤ X ≤ b)
```

The shaded region (conceptually) between $x = a$ and $x = b$ under the curve represents the probability $P(a \le X \le b)$. The **whole curve must integrate to exactly 1**, enforcing the normalisation property.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🎯 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a Probability Density Function. State any two essential properties of a PDF.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A Probability Density Function (PDF) is a non-negative integrable function $f_X(x)$ associated with a continuous random variable $X$ such that the probability that $X$ lies in the interval $[a, b]$ is given by $P(a \le X \le b) = \int_a^b f_X(x)\, dx$.

**Two essential properties:**
1. **Non-negativity:** $f_X(x) \ge 0$ for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$.
2. **Normalisation:** $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_X(x)\, dx = 1$.

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark each for the two properties = 3 marks)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Differentiate between PMF and PDF. Can $f_X(x)$ exceed 1 in magnitude? Justify.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | PMF (Discrete) | PDF (Continuous) |
|---|---|---|
| Applicable to | Discrete random variables | Continuous random variables |
| Defines | $P(X = x_i) = p_i$ | $P(a \le X \le b) = \int_a^b f_X(x)\, dx$ |
| Value at a point | $0 \le p_i \le 1$ | $f_X(x) \ge 0$ (can exceed 1) |
| Sum / Integral | $\sum p_i = 1$ | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_X(x)\, dx = 1$ |

**Yes**, $f_X(x)$ can exceed 1. For example, the Uniform PDF on $[0, 0.5]$ is $f_X(x) = 1/0.5 = 2$. Only the *area under the curve* has probability interpretation, not the value of $f_X(x)$ itself. *(2 marks for the table + 1 mark for the justification = 3 marks)*

---

## 📘 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### ✏️ QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Derive the expressions for the mean and variance of a continuous random variable $X$ using its probability density function $f_X(x)$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The tensile strength (in MPa) of a steel alloy follows a normal distribution with mean $\mu = 500$ MPa and standard deviation $\sigma = 25$ MPa. Find the probability that a randomly selected specimen has tensile strength:
   (i) Greater than 540 MPa.
   (ii) Between 470 MPa and 530 MPa. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Step 1 — Derivation of Mean $E(X)$:**
The expected value is the long-run average if the experiment is repeated infinitely:
$$E(X) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x \cdot f_X(x)\, dx \quad \text{...(1)}$$
*(2 marks for setting up the integral)*

**Step 2 — Derivation of $E(X^2)$:**
The second raw moment is defined analogously:
$$E(X^2) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x^2 \cdot f_X(x)\, dx \quad \text{...(2)}$$
*(1 mark)*

**Step 3 — Derivation of Variance:**
By definition, $\text{Var}(X) = E[(X-\mu)^2]$. Expanding:
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}(X) &= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (x-\mu)^2 f_X(x)\, dx \\
&= \int x^2 f_X(x)\, dx - 2\mu\!\int x f_X(x)\, dx + \mu^2\!\int f_X(x)\, dx
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting $\int f_X(x)\, dx = 1$ and $\int x f_X(x)\, dx = \mu$:
$$\boxed{\text{Var}(X) = E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2} \quad \text{...(3)}$$
*(4 marks for completing the derivation)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**(i) Probability that $X > 540$:**
$$z = \frac{540 - 500}{25} = \frac{40}{25} = 1.6$$
$$P(X > 540) = P(Z > 1.6) = 1 - \Phi(1.6) = 1 - 0.9452 = 0.0548$$
**Answer:** $\approx 0.0548$ or **5.48%** *(3 marks)*

**(ii) Probability that $470 < X < 530$:**
$$z_1 = \frac{470-500}{25} = -1.2, \qquad z_2 = \frac{530-500}{25} = 1.2$$
$$P(-1.2 < Z < 1.2) = 2\Phi(1.2) - 1 = 2(0.8849) - 1 = 0.7698$$
**Answer:** $\approx 0.7698$ or **76.98%** *(4 marks)*

---

### ✏️ QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]` *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

**(a)** The probability density function of a continuous random variable $X$ is:
$$f_X(x) = \begin{cases} kx(2-x), & 0 \le x \le 2 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$
Find the value of $k$, the mean $E(X)$, and the variance $\text{Var}(X)$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the relationship between PDF and CDF. For a normal random variable with $\mu = 60$ and $\sigma^2 = 100$, compute $P(X \le 75)$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Step 1 — Apply normalisation to find $k$:**
$$\int_0^2 kx(2-x)\, dx = 1$$
$$\begin{aligned}
k\int_0^2 (2x - x^2)\, dx &= 1 \\
k\left[x^2 - \frac{x^3}{3}\right]_0^2 &= 1 \\
k\left(4 - \frac{8}{3}\right) &= 1 \\
k \cdot \frac{4}{3} &= 1 \implies k = \frac{3}{4}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $E(X)$:**
$$E(X) = \int_0^2 x \cdot \frac{3}{4}x(2-x)\, dx = \frac{3}{4}\int_0^2 (2x^2 - x^3)\, dx$$
$$\begin{aligned}
&= \frac{3}{4}\left[\frac{2x^3}{3} - \frac{x^4}{4}\right]_0^2 \\
&= \frac{3}{4}\left(\frac{16}{3} - 4\right) = \frac{3}{4}\cdot\frac{4}{3} = 1
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $E(X^2)$:**
$$E(X^2) = \frac{3}{4}\int_0^2 (2x^3 - x^4)\, dx = \frac{3}{4}\left[\frac{x^4}{2} - \frac{x^5}{5}\right]_0^2$$
$$= \frac{3}{4}\left(8 - \frac{32}{5}\right) = \frac{3}{4}\cdot\frac{8}{5} = \frac{6}{5}$$

**Step 4 — Variance:**
$$\text{Var}(X) = E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2 = \frac{6}{5} - 1 = \frac{1}{5} = 0.2$$

**Final Answer:** $k = 0.75,\ E(X) = 1,\ \text{Var}(X) = 0.2$ *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Step 1 — Conceptual relationship (3 marks):**

| Operation | Forward | Reverse |
|---|---|---|
| Function pair | PDF $f_X(x)$ | CDF $F_X(x)$ |
| Conversion | $F_X(x) = \int_{-\infty}^x f_X(t)\, dt$ | $f_X(x) = \dfrac{d}{dx}F_X(x)$ |
| Meaning | Density at a point | Accumulated probability up to $x$ |
| Properties | $f_X(x) \ge 0$, total area $= 1$ | $0 \le F_X(x) \le 1$, monotonically increasing |

**Step 2 — Compute $P(X \le 75)$ for $X \sim N(60, 100)$, so $\sigma = 10$:**
$$z = \frac{75 - 60}{10} = 1.5$$

From standard normal tables: $\Phi(1.5) = 0.9332$.

$$\boxed{P(X \le 75) = 0.9332 \text{ or } 93.32\%} \quad \text{...(4 marks)}$$

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks — Top 5 mistakes KTU examiners flag:**
> 1. **Treating $f_X(x)$ as a probability.** A PDF value can exceed 1; it is *not* a probability. Only the integral has probability meaning. *(–1 mark deduction)*
> 2. **Forgetting the normalisation check.** When a constant $k$ is given in the PDF, students often jump to compute $E(X)$ without first verifying $\int f_X(x)\, dx = 1$. Always solve for $k$ first.
> 3. **Wrong sign in the Z-transformation.** Use $Z = (X - \mu)/\sigma$, **not** $(\mu - X)/\sigma$. The order matters.
> 4. **Confusing PDF with CDF area limits.** PDF integrals give **probability** in an interval; CDF integrals give **cumulative probability** up to a point.
> 5. **Skipping the step $\int f_X(x)\, dx = 1$ in variance derivation.** Examiners award 2 marks specifically for invoking the normalisation property. Don't skip it.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Probability Density Function**
>
> ✅ A PDF is defined only for **continuous** random variables.
> ✅ The two **mandatory properties** are: (i) $f_X(x) \ge 0$, and (ii) $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_X(x)\, dx = 1$.
> ✅ Probabilities are obtained via **integration**: $P(a \le X \le b) = \int_a^b f_X(x)\, dx$.
> ✅ $P(X = c) = 0$ for any single point $c$.
> ✅ PDF ↔ CDF relationship: **CDF is the integral of PDF**; **PDF is the derivative of CDF**.
> ✅ Mean: $\mu = \int x\, f_X(x)\, dx$.
> ✅ Variance shortcut: $\sigma^2 = E(X^2) - \mu^2$.
> ✅ Standard normal transformation: $Z = (X - \mu)/\sigma \sim N(0, 1)$.
> ✅ Always solve for the unknown constant $k$ **before** computing the mean.
> ✅ KTU most-tested distributions: **Uniform, Normal, Exponential**.
> ✅ Exam-favourite problems: (i) find $k$ given PDF, (ii) compute mean/variance, (iii) standardise and use Z-tables.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cumulative Distribution Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT727 - DESIGN OF EXPERIMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cumulative Distribution Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF)** of a random variable $X$, evaluated at a real number $x \in \mathbb{R}$, is rigorously defined as:

$$F_X(x) = P(X \le x)$$

That is, $F_X(x)$ gives the *accumulated probability* that the random variable $X$ takes on a value less than or equal to $x$. The CDF is the most complete single descriptor of a random variable: once $F_X(x)$ is known for *every* real $x$, the entire probability law of $X$ is determined.

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation used in KTU 2024 Scheme:** The CDF may be written as $F(x)$, $F_X(x)$, or $C_X(x)$. All three are equivalent. The subscript $X$ is dropped only when the underlying random variable is unambiguous from context.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The CDF is a foundational building block for Module 1 of *Design of Experiments*. Every subsequent topic — sampling distributions, the F-test in ANOVA, the t-test, chi-square tests, and confidence intervals — is built upon the concept of cumulative probability. Mastery of the CDF is non-negotiable.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a long, transparent water tank placed along a horizontal axis, with the random variable values $x$ marked along its base (like a number line). Water representing total probability $\mathbf{1}$ is poured from the left end and progressively fills the tank as we move rightward.

- At the far left ($x \to -\infty$), the tank is empty → $F(-\infty) = 0$.
- As we slide our finger to the right, the water level (cumulative probability) rises.
- At the far right ($x \to +\infty$), the tank is completely full → $F(+\infty) = 1$.

The **height of the water** at any point $x$ is exactly $F(x)$. The CDF is therefore nothing more than a *water-accumulation profile* of total probability mass.

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Summary:** The CDF answers the question *"What is the chance that $X$ lands at or below the value $x$?"* — it is a *running total* of probability from $-\infty$ up to $x$.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Lower bound of CDF:** $F(-\infty) = \mathbf{0}$
- **Upper bound of CDF:** $F(+\infty) = \mathbf{1}$
- **Universal probability bound:** $\mathbf{0} \le F(x) \le \mathbf{1}$ for every $x \in \mathbb{R}$

These bounds are **not approximations** — they are absolute mathematical truths derived from the axioms of probability.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Fundamental Properties of the CDF

For any random variable $X$ (discrete, continuous, or mixed), its CDF $F(x)$ must satisfy the following six properties. These are the standard **KTU board-exam checklist** items.

**Property 1 — Non-Negativity and Upper Bound:**
$$0 \le F(x) \le 1, \quad \forall x \in \mathbb{R}$$

**Property 2 — Boundary at Negative Infinity:**
$$\lim_{x \to -\infty} F(x) = 0$$

**Property 3 — Boundary at Positive Infinity:**
$$\lim_{x \to +\infty} F(x) = 1$$

**Property 4 — Monotonic Non-Decreasing:**
$$\text{If } a \le b, \text{ then } F(a) \le F(b)$$

**Property 5 — Right-Continuity:**
$$\lim_{x \to a^{+}} F(x) = F(a)$$

**Property 6 — Interval Probability:**
$$P(a < X \le b) = F(b) - F(a)$$

### 2.2 Detailed Justifications (Why each property holds)

- **Property 1:** Probability is bounded in $[0, 1]$ by Kolmogorov's axioms. The CDF is a probability, so it inherits these bounds.

- **Property 4 (Monotonicity):** If $a \le b$, then the event $\{X \le a\}$ is a *subset* of the event $\{X \le b\}$. By the monotonicity of probability measure: $P(X \le a) \le P(X \le b)$, i.e., $F(a) \le F(b)$. This is one of the most-tested properties in KTU exams.

- **Property 5 (Right-Continuity):** This arises because of the *closed* inequality $\le$ in the definition $P(X \le x)$. The complement $P(X > x) = 1 - F(x)$ is left-continuous, hence $F(x)$ is right-continuous.

- **Property 6 (Interval Probability):** Subtract the two cumulative probabilities. Intuitively: $F(b)$ contains the probability of $\{X \le a\}$ plus $\{a < X \le b\}$. Removing the former yields the latter. This formula is the *workhorse* of every probability calculation in DOE.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM CHEAT-SHEET:** This table consolidates every formula you will need for CDF-based problems in Module 1.

| **#** | **Formula** | **Description / Use Case** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_X(x) = P(X \le x)$ | Universal definition of the CDF |
| 2 | $F(-\infty) = 0$ | Lower boundary |
| 3 | $F(+\infty) = 1$ | Upper boundary |
| 4 | $0 \le F(x) \le 1$ | Range constraint |
| 5 | $F(a) \le F(b)$ if $a \le b$ | Monotonicity (non-decreasing) |
| 6 | $P(a < X \le b) = F(b) - F(a)$ | Interval probability (most-used) |
| 7 | $P(X > a) = 1 - F(a)$ | Survival / upper-tail probability |
| 8 | $P(X \le a) = F(a)$ | Lower-tail probability |
| 9 | $F(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f(t)\, dt$ | Build CDF from PDF (continuous) |
| 10 | $f(x) = \dfrac{dF(x)}{dx}$ | Recover PDF from CDF (continuous) |
| 11 | $F(x) = \sum_{t \,\le\, x} P(X = t)$ | Build CDF from PMF (discrete) |
| 12 | $S(x) = 1 - F(x)$ | Survival / Reliability function |
| 13 | $Q(p) = F^{-1}(p)$ | Quantile function (inverse CDF) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU-Specific Tip:** Memorize rows 6, 7, 8, and 9. Approximately **70% of all Part A and Part B marks** in Module 1 probability questions come from these four formulas alone.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility in Design of Experiments

The CDF is not just a theoretical construct — it is the *operational engine* behind every inferential test used in DOE:

1. **P-value Computation:** A p-value is a CDF-evaluated tail probability. For a test statistic $T$ with CDF $F_T$: $p = 1 - F_T(T_{\text{obs}})$.
2. **Critical Values:** Critical values are inverse-CDF evaluations: $t_{\alpha/2, \, n-1} = F_T^{-1}(1 - \alpha/2)$.
3. **ANOVA F-Test:** The F-distribution CDF gives the probability that the observed F-ratio (between-group MS / within-group MS) occurs under $H_0$.
4. **Sample Size Determination:** Power $= 1 - \beta$ depends on $F_{F, \, df_1, df_2}(\text{critical value})$.
5. **Reliability Studies:** The survival function $S(x) = 1 - F(x)$ is widely used when designing experiments for component lifetime testing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Industrial Example:** In a Taguchi-style robust design experiment, an engineer compares signal-to-noise ratios across factor levels.
