$$P(-3 \leq Z \leq 3) = 0.99865 - 0.00135 = 0.9973 \approx \mathbf{99.73\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Exams:** The 68-95-99.7 rule is also known as the **"Three Sigma Rule"** and is the basis for control charts and Six Sigma quality standards. The probability of an observation falling outside $\pm 3\sigma$ is only $0.0027$, i.e., about 1 in 370 chances.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Properties of Different Normal Curves

| Scenario | Mean $\mu$ | Std Dev $\sigma$ | Curve Behavior | Peak Height $\frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}$ |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- | :---: |
| $N(0, 1)$ | $0$ | $1$ | Standard bell | $\approx 0.3989$ |
| $N(0, 4)$ | $0$ | $2$ | Wider, shorter | $\approx 0.1995$ |
| $N(0, 0.25)$ | $0$ | $0.5$ | Narrower, taller | $\approx 0.7979$ |
| $N(5, 1)$ | $5$ | $1$ | Shifted right | $\approx 0.3989$ |
| $N(-3, 9)$ | $-3$ | $3$ | Shifted left, wide | $\approx 0.1330$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Changing $\mu$ **translates** the curve left/right without changing its shape. Changing $\sigma$ **scales** the curve vertically and horizontally without moving its center. This is a frequent short-answer question in KTU.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** The tensile strength of a steel wire follows $X \sim N(500, 100)$ MPa, where $\mu = 500$ MPa and $\sigma^2 = 100$ (so $\sigma = 10$ MPa). Find:
- **(a)** $P(X > 515)$
- **(b)** $P(490 \leq X \leq 510)$
- **(c)** The value $x$ such that $P(X < x) = 0.95$

### Solution

**Part (a):** Convert to Z-score.

$$Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma} = \frac{515 - 500}{10} = 1.5$$

$$P(X > 515) = P(Z > 1.5) = 1 - \Phi(1.5) = 1 - 0.9332 = \mathbf{0.0668}$$

**Part (b):** Convert both limits.

$$Z_1 = \frac{490 - 500}{10} = -1.0, \quad Z_2 = \frac{510 - 500}{10} = 1.0$$

$$P(490 \leq X \leq 510) = P(-1 \leq Z \leq 1) = \Phi(1) - \Phi(-1)$$

$$= 0.8413 - 0.1587 = \mathbf{0.6826}$$

**Part (c):** We need the 95th percentile. From Z-table, $\Phi(1.645) = 0.95$.

$$Z = 1.645 \Rightarrow X = \mu + Z\sigma = 500 + (1.645)(10) = 500 + 16.45 = \mathbf{516.45 \text{ MPa}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Always **show the Z-transformation step explicitly** before plugging into the table. Skipping this step is the #1 reason students lose marks even when the final answer is correct.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flowchart: From Raw Data to Standard Normal

The following block-level architecture illustrates how raw experimental data is transformed and interpreted using the Normal Distribution in a DOE workflow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Raw Experimental Data X"] --> B["Compute Sample Mean mu_hat"]
    A --> C["Compute Sample Std Dev sigma_hat"]
    B --> D["Form Estimated Distribution N of mu_hat and sigma_hat"]
    C --> D
    D --> E["Apply Z Transformation Z equals X minus mu over sigma"]
    E --> F["Standard Normal Z with mean 0 and variance 1"]
    F --> G["Consult Z Table or Compute CDF Phi z"]
    G --> H["Extract Probability Area or Critical Value"]
    H --> I["Statistical Inference Decision for DOE"]
    I --> J["Accept or Reject Null Hypothesis in ANOVA"]
    I --> K["Construct Control Limits for SPC Charts"]
    I --> L["Estimate Confidence Interval for Mean Response"]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: The Anatomy of the Normal PDF

This schematic maps out the structural anatomy of the bell curve with its landmark points.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Left Tail Region x less than mu minus 3 sigma"] --> B["Left Inflection Point mu minus sigma"]
    B --> C["Lower Shoulder mu minus 2 sigma"]
    C --> D["Peak Mode and Mean and Median at mu"]
    D --> E["Upper Shoulder mu plus 2 sigma"]
    E --> F["Right Inflection Point mu plus sigma"]
    F --> G["Right Tail Region x greater than mu plus 3 sigma"]
    D -.Peak Height.-> H["1 over sigma times sqrt of 2 pi"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Properties and Applications of Normal Distribution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root["Normal Distribution N of mu and sigma squared"] --> P1["Symmetric about mu"]
    Root --> P2["Mean equals Median equals Mode equals mu"]
    Root --> P3["Total Area under PDF equals 1"]
    Root --> P4["Empirical Rule 68 95 99.7 within 1 2 3 sigma"]
    Root --> P5["Z Transformation gives Standard Normal"]
    P5 --> P6["Used in Z Tables for Probability Lookup"]
    P5 --> P7["Used in Confidence Interval Construction"]
    P5 --> P8["Used in Hypothesis Testing and P value"]
    P1 --> App1["Application 1 ANOVA Residual Assumption"]
    P2 --> App2["Application 2 Process Capability Cp Cpk"]
    P4 --> App3["Application 3 Six Sigma Quality Control"]
    P8 --> App4["Application 4 DOE F Tests and t Tests"]
```

## 4.4 Diagram: Probability Regions under the Standard Normal Curve

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Standard Normal Curve N of 0 and 1"] --> B["Region 1 P Z less than negative 1.96 equals 0.025"]
    A --> C["Region 2 P negative 1.96 less than Z less than 1.96 equals 0.95"]
    A --> D["Region 3 P Z greater than 1.96 equals 0.025"]
    B --> E["Combined Tail Probability 0.05 for two tailed test"]
    D --> E
    C --> F["Confidence Level 95 percent"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** These flowcharts are designed as **functional architecture maps**. In a KTU exam, if asked to "explain the role of Normal Distribution in DOE," you can reproduce a similar flowchart to score full marks on structural understanding.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Define the Normal Distribution. Write its probability density function and state any four properties.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Normal (or Gaussian) Distribution is a continuous symmetric probability distribution characterized by its bell-shaped curve. A random variable $X$ follows $N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ if its PDF is:

$$f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left[-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{x-\mu}{\sigma}\right)^2\right], \quad -\infty < x < \infty$$

**Four Properties (any 4):**
1. The curve is **symmetric** about the mean $\mu$.
2. **Mean = Median = Mode = $\mu$**.
3. The total **area under the curve = 1**.
4. The peak height is $\frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}$.
5. Inflection points occur at $x = \mu \pm \sigma$.
6. Skewness = 0 and excess kurtosis = 0.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**What is a Standard Normal Distribution? Explain the process of standardization.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Standard Normal Distribution** is a special case of the Normal Distribution with mean $\mu = 0$ and standard deviation $\sigma = 1$. Its PDF is:

$$\phi(z) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-z^2/2}$$

**Standardization** is the process of converting any Normal variable $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ to the standard normal $Z$ using:

$$Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$$

This produces $Z \sim N(0, 1)$, allowing the use of a single Z-table for all probability calculations regardless of the original $\mu$ and $\sigma$. *(1.5 marks for definition, 1.5 marks for standardization explanation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the probability density function (PDF) of the Normal Distribution from its defining differential equation. State and prove five important properties of the Normal Distribution. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The diameter of ball bearings produced by a machine follows a Normal Distribution with mean $\mu = 2.5$ cm and standard deviation $\sigma = 0.05$ cm. If specifications require the diameter to be between 2.4 cm and 2.6 cm:
  - **(i)** Find the probability that a randomly selected bearing meets the specification. **(4 Marks)**
  - **(ii)** If 10,000 bearings are produced, how many are expected to be **outside** the specification? **(3 Marks)**

---

### Question B (Alternative to Question A) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the importance of the Normal Distribution in Design of Experiments (DOE). Discuss the **Empirical Rule (68-95-99.7)** with the help of Z-table values. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** In a manufacturing process, the time to assemble a product is Normally distributed with mean $\mu = 35$ minutes and standard deviation $\sigma = 4$ minutes.
  - **(i)** What is the probability that the assembly takes **less than 30 minutes**? **(3 Marks)**
  - **(ii)** Find the assembly time $x$ such that only **10%** of products take longer than $x$ to assemble. **(4 Marks)**

---

### Model Solutions for Question A

#### Part (a) — Model Answer [7 Marks]

**Derivation Outline:**
The Normal PDF can be derived by solving the differential equation:

$$\frac{d}{dx}\left[\ln f(x)\right] = \frac{-(x - \mu)}{c}$$

for some constant $c$. Integrating:

$$\ln f(x) = -\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2c} + K_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad f(x) = K \exp\left[-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2c}\right]$$

Imposing the conditions $f(x) \geq 0$ everywhere, $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x) dx = 1$, and symmetry forces $c = \sigma^2$ and $K = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}$, giving the final PDF.

**Five Properties (state and prove as shown in Section 3):**
1. Symmetry: $f(\mu + k) = f(\mu - k)$ because $(x-\mu)^2$ is symmetric.
2. Mode at $\mu$: From derivative test, $\frac{d}{dx}\ln f(x) = 0 \Rightarrow x = \mu$.
3. Mean = Median = Mode = $\mu$ (due to symmetry and unimodality).
4. Inflection points at $\mu \pm \sigma$: Setting $\frac{d^2 f}{dx^2} = 0$ yields $x = \mu \pm \sigma$.
5. Total probability = 1: $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\, dx = 1$ (via Gaussian integral).

**Mark Distribution:** [2 Marks for PDF expression, 2 Marks for derivation steps, 3 Marks for properties]

---

#### Part (b) — Model Answer [7 Marks]

**Given:** $\mu = 2.5$ cm, $\sigma = 0.05$ cm, specification range $2.4 \leq X \leq 2.6$ cm.

**(i) Probability of meeting specification [4 Marks]:**

Convert both limits to Z-scores:

$$Z_1 = \frac{2.4 - 2.5}{0.05} = -2.0, \quad Z_2 = \frac{2.6 - 2.5}{0.05} = 2.0$$

$$P(2.4 \leq X \leq 2.6) = P(-2 \leq Z \leq 2) = \Phi(2) - \Phi(-2)$$

$$= 0.9772 - 0.0228 = \mathbf{0.9544}$$

**(ii) Bearings outside specification [3 Marks]:**

$$P(\text{outside}) = 1 - 0.9544 = 0.0456$$

$$\text{Expected number outside} = 10{,}000 \times 0.0456 = \mathbf{456 \text{ bearings}}$$

---

### Model Solutions for Question B

#### Part (a) — Model Answer [7 Marks]

**Importance in DOE [3 Marks]:**
- Normality of residuals ($\varepsilon \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$) is the **fundamental assumption** of ANOVA, which is the most-used statistical test in DOE.
- Enables the use of **F-tests, t-tests, and p-values** to determine factor significance.
- Forms the basis of **regression analysis** confidence intervals.
- Justifies construction of **control charts** ($\pm 3\sigma$ limits) for monitoring experimental stability.

**Empirical Rule Discussion [4 Marks]:**
Refer to derivation in Section 3.3. Mention:
- Within $\pm 1\sigma$: **68.27%** of observations
- Within $\pm 2\sigma$: **95.45%** of observations
- Within $\pm 3\sigma$: **99.73%** of observations
- Outside $\pm 3\sigma$: only **0.27%** (~1 in 370)

This rule underlies the **3-sigma control limits** in SPC charts and the **Six Sigma** quality philosophy (which extends to $\pm 6\sigma$).

---

#### Part (b) — Model Answer [7 Marks]

**Given:** $\mu = 35$ min, $\sigma = 4$ min.

**(i) $P(X < 30)$ [3 Marks]:**

$$Z = \frac{30 - 35}{4} = -1.25$$

$$P(X < 30) = P(Z < -1.25) = 1 - \Phi(1.25) = 1 - 0.8944 = \mathbf{0.1056}$$

**(ii) Find $x$ such that $P(X > x) = 0.10$ [4 Marks]:**

We need the 90th percentile. From Z-table, $\Phi(1.28) = 0.90$, so $Z = 1.28$.

$$Z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma} \Rightarrow 1.28 = \frac{x - 35}{4}$$

$$x = 35 + (1.28)(4) = 35 + 5.12 = \mathbf{40.12 \text{ minutes}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Notation Trap:** Many students confuse $N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ with $N(\mu, \sigma)$. Always re-read the question to check whether the second parameter is the **variance** or the **standard deviation**.
> 2. **Forgetting Standardization:** Marks are heavily penalized if you substitute raw $X$ values directly into Z-table without first computing $Z$.
> 3. **Wrong Tail:** When a question asks $P(X > x)$, you must compute $1 - \Phi(z)$, not $\Phi(z)$. Drawing a shaded diagram before computation eliminates this error.
> 4. **Interpolation Errors:** If the exact Z-value is not in the table, KTU expects **linear interpolation** or rounding to the nearest tabulated value. Failing to show interpolation steps costs method marks.
> 5. **Final Units:** Always write the final probability as a decimal between 0 and 1, and report answers involving $X$ with correct units (cm, minutes, MPa, etc.).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** The Normal Distribution has PDF $f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left[-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{x-\mu}{\sigma}\right)^2\right]$, characterized by mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$.
- **Notation:** $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ — the second parameter is the **variance**, not the standard deviation.
- **Standardization:** Any $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ converts to $Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma} \sim N(0, 1)$.
- **Symmetry:** The curve is symmetric about $\mu$, so Mean = Median = Mode = $\mu$.
- **Peak Height:** $f(\mu) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \approx \frac{0.3989}{\sigma}$.
- **Inflection Points:** Occur exactly at $\mu - \sigma$ and $\mu + \sigma$.
- **Empirical Rule:** 68.27% within $\pm 1\sigma$, 95.45% within $\pm 2\sigma$, 99.73% within $\pm 3\sigma$.
- **Key Z-Values to Memorize:**
  - $P(-1.96 \leq Z \leq 1.96) = 0.95$ (95% CI)
  - $P(-2.576 \leq Z \leq 2.576) = 0.99$ (99% CI)
  - $\Phi(1.645) = 0.95$, $\Phi(2.33) = 0.99$
- **DOE Connection:** ANOVA assumes residuals are $\sim N(0, \sigma^2)$. Always check normality using Q-Q plots or Shapiro-Wilk test before interpreting F-test results.
- **Linear Combinations:** $aX + b \sim N(a\mu + b, a^2\sigma^2)$ — variance scales by $a^2$.
- **Symmetry Identity:** $\Phi(-z) = 1 - \Phi(z)$; this is your most powerful tool for Z-table lookups.
- **Total Probability:** $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\, dx = 1$ — the entire area under the bell curve is unity.
- **Sums of Independent Normals:** $X + Y \sim N(\mu_X + \mu_Y, \sigma_X^2 + \sigma_Y^2)$ — variances add, not standard deviations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Note for KTU 2024:** The Normal Distribution is the **backbone** of statistical inference in DOE. Every module in this course (ANOVA, Factorial Designs, Regression) ultimately relies on the assumption of Normality. Master the Z-transformation, memorize the critical values (1.96, 2.576, 1.645), and always **draw the shaded area** before calculating probabilities.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Log Normal and Weibull Distributions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT727 - DESIGN OF EXPERIMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Log Normal and Weibull Distributions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Log-Normal Distribution

**Formal Definition:**
A continuous random variable $X$ is said to follow a **Log-Normal Distribution** if its natural logarithm $Y = \ln(X)$ follows a Normal Distribution with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$. In other words, the logarithm of the variable is normally distributed, while the variable itself has a positively skewed, non-negative distribution.

The probability density function (PDF) is given by:

$$f(x) = \frac{1}{x \sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{(\ln x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right), \quad x > 0$$

where $\mu$ is the mean of $\ln(X)$ and $\sigma > 0$ is the standard deviation of $\ln(X)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Log-Normal Distribution is the cornerstone of reliability theory and is used to model the distribution of products that have a multiplicative structure (e.g., time to failure, particle size distributions, financial asset prices).

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine you invest ₹1,000 in a stock. Each day, the stock's return is a small *random multiplicative factor* (e.g., 1.02 on a good day, 0.97 on a bad day). After many days, the final price is the product of all these factors. Due to the Central Limit Theorem, the **logarithm** of this final price will be approximately Normal. Hence, the price itself is Log-Normal. This is why Log-Normal distributions appear everywhere in **finance, biology (cell growth), and engineering (fatigue life, corrosion)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** A Log-Normal variable is **always positive** ($X > 0$) and is **right-skewed** (long tail on the right). The degree of skewness is controlled by $\sigma$.

### 1.2 Weibull Distribution

**Formal Definition:**
The **Weibull Distribution** is a continuous probability distribution widely used in reliability engineering, survival analysis, and failure analysis. It is characterized by two parameters: a **shape parameter** $\beta > 0$ and a **scale parameter** $\alpha > 0$.

The probability density function (PDF) is given by:

$$f(x) = \frac{\beta}{\alpha}\left(\frac{x}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1} \exp\left(-\left(\frac{x}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right), \quad x \geq 0$$

The corresponding cumulative distribution function (CDF) is:

$$F(x) = 1 - \exp\left(-\left(\frac{x}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right), \quad x \geq 0$$

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of the Weibull distribution as the **"Swiss Army Knife"** of lifetime distributions. The shape parameter $\beta$ acts like a *personality switch*:
- If $\beta < 1$: The item is **burning-in** (failing early, like defective electronics at startup).
- If $\beta = 1$: It becomes the **Exponential distribution** (constant failure rate — random failures).
- If $\beta > 1$: The item is **aging/wearing out** (like bearings, light bulbs, or human mortality curves).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Relevance:** The Weibull distribution is the *most commonly used* distribution in Weibull Analysis for predicting the lifetime of mechanical components (bearings, capacitors, ball bearings, electric motors). The scale parameter $\alpha$ represents the **characteristic life** — the time at which 63.2% of the population has failed.

### 1.3 Standard Parameters & Constants

| Symbol | Parameter Name | Domain | Role |
|--------|---------------|--------|------|
| $\mu$ | Log-mean | $(-\infty, \infty)$ | Location of $\ln(X)$ |
| $\sigma$ | Log-std deviation | $\sigma > 0$ | Spread of $\ln(X)$ |
| $\beta$ | Weibull shape | $\beta > 0$ | Determines skewness & failure mode |
| $\alpha$ | Weibull scale | $\alpha > 0$ | Characteristic life (63.2% failure point) |
| $\Gamma(\cdot)$ | Gamma function | — | $\Gamma(n) = (n-1)!$ for integer $n$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Log-Normal Distribution: Theoretical Breakdown

**Origin & Construction:**
1. Let $Y \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ be a Normal random variable.
2. Define the transformation $X = e^Y$, so $Y = \ln(X)$.
3. By the **change of variables** formula in probability, the PDF of $X$ is:
   $$f_X(x) = f_Y(\ln x) \cdot \left|\frac{d}{dx}\ln x\right| = \frac{1}{x\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{(\ln x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$
4. This is valid **only** for $x > 0$, since $\ln(x)$ must be defined.

**Why and How it Works:**
- The transformation $X = e^Y$ "stretches" the symmetric Normal distribution. The right tail (large positive $Y$) becomes exponentially large, while the left tail (negative $Y$) is compressed near zero. The result is an asymmetric, right-skewed distribution.
- The mode of the distribution occurs at $x = e^{\mu - \sigma^2}$, and the median at $x = e^{\mu}$. Note that **mean $\neq$ median $\neq$ mode** for Log-Normal.

**Moment Generating Property:**
The $n$-th moment of a Log-Normal variable is given by a clean closed form:

$$E(X^n) = \exp\left(n\mu + \frac{n^2\sigma^2}{2}\right)$$

Setting $n = 1$ gives the mean; setting $n = 2$ gives $E(X^2)$, which is then used to find the variance.

**Real-World Utility:**
- **Financial Engineering:** Modeling stock prices, option pricing (Black-Scholes uses Log-Normal).
- **Biomedical Engineering:** Modeling incubation periods of diseases, survival times.
- **Materials Science:** Modeling particle size in powder metallurgy, fatigue crack growth.
- **Hydrology:** Modeling rainfall and river discharge data.

### 2.2 Weibull Distribution: Theoretical Breakdown

**Origin & Construction:**
1. The Weibull distribution arises naturally from the **Weakest Link Theory** — the strength of a chain is determined by its weakest link.
2. If the number of links in a chain is very large, and each link has a Weibull-distributed strength, the resulting failure time follows a Weibull distribution.
3. Mathematically, it is derived from the Extreme Value distribution by a power transformation.

**Key Operational Properties:**

**Reliability Function (Survival Function):**
The probability that a component survives beyond time $t$ is:

$$R(t) = 1 - F(t) = \exp\left(-\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right)$$

**Hazard Rate (Instantaneous Failure Rate):**
$$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{\beta}{\alpha}\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1}$$

This is the most diagnostic feature — the shape of $h(t)$ reveals the failure mechanism.

**Cumulative Hazard Function:**
$$H(t) = \left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}$$

**Real-World Utility:**
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Bearing life prediction, fatigue analysis.
- **Electrical Engineering:** Capacitor and semiconductor failure analysis.
- **Civil Engineering:** Concrete strength, wind speed modeling.
- **Quality Control:** Acceptance sampling for lifetime data.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

#### Log-Normal Distribution

| Formula | Expression |
|---------|------------|
| PDF | $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{x\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\dfrac{(\ln x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ |
| CDF | $F(x) = \Phi\left(\dfrac{\ln x - \mu}{\sigma}\right)$ |
| Mean | $E(X) = \exp\left(\mu + \dfrac{\sigma^2}{2}\right)$ |
| Variance | $\text{Var}(X) = \left[\exp(\sigma^2) - 1\right]\exp(2\mu + \sigma^2)$ |
| Mode | $e^{\mu - \sigma^2}$ |
| Median | $e^{\mu}$ |
| $n$-th Moment | $E(X^n) = \exp\left(n\mu + \dfrac{n^2\sigma^2}{2}\right)$ |
| Skewness | $\dfrac{1}{2}\left[\exp(\sigma^2) - 1\right]^{1/2}\left[\exp(\sigma^2) + 2\right]$ |
| Support | $x > 0$ |

#### Weibull Distribution

| Formula | Expression |
|---------|------------|
| PDF | $f(x) = \dfrac{\beta}{\alpha}\left(\dfrac{x}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1}\exp\left(-\left(\dfrac{x}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right)$ |
| CDF | $F(x) = 1 - \exp\left(-\left(\dfrac{x}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right)$ |
| Mean | $E(X) = \alpha \cdot \Gamma\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\beta}\right)$ |
| Variance | $\text{Var}(X) = \alpha^2\left[\Gamma\left(1 + \dfrac{2}{\beta}\right) - \Gamma^2\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\beta}\right)\right]$ |
| Median | $\alpha (\ln 2)^{1/\beta}$ |
| Mode | $\alpha \left(\dfrac{\beta-1}{\beta}\right)^{1/\beta}$ for $\beta > 1$ |
| Reliability | $R(t) = \exp\left(-\left(\dfrac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right)$ |
| Hazard Rate | $h(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\alpha}\left(\dfrac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1}$ |
| Support | $x \geq 0$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Check for Weibull Mode:** The mode exists **only if** $\beta > 1$. For $\beta = 1$ (Exponential), the mode is at $x = 0$. For $\beta < 1$, the density is monotonically decreasing and has no interior mode.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Mean and Variance of Log-Normal Distribution

**Setup:** Let $X$ be Log-Normal, so $Y = \ln(X) \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$, meaning $X = e^Y$.

**Step 1:** Write the expectation using the definition $E(X) = E(e^Y)$.

$$E(X) = E(e^Y) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^y \cdot \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{(y-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) dy$$

**Step 2:** Combine the exponential terms.

$$E(X) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(y - \frac{(y-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) dy$$

**Step 3:** Complete the square in the exponent.

The exponent is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y - \frac{(y-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2} &= y - \frac{y^2 - 2\mu y + \mu^2}{2\sigma^2} \\
&= -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\left[y^2 - 2\mu y + \mu^2 - 2\sigma^2 y\right] \\
&= -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\left[y^2 - 2y(\mu + \sigma^2) + \mu^2\right] \\
&= -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\left[(y - (\mu + \sigma^2))^2 - (\mu + \sigma^2)^2 + \mu^2\right] \\
&= -\frac{(y - (\mu + \sigma^2))^2}{2\sigma^2} + \frac{2\mu\sigma^2 + \sigma^4}{2\sigma^2} \\
&= -\frac{(y - (\mu + \sigma^2))^2}{2\sigma^2} + \mu + \frac{\sigma^2}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Recognize the resulting integral as a Normal PDF.

The integrand is now of the form $\exp(\mu + \sigma^2/2)$ times a Normal density with mean $(\mu + \sigma^2)$ and variance $\sigma^2$. The integral of any Normal PDF over its full range is **exactly 1**.

$$E(X) = \exp\left(\mu + \frac{\sigma^2}{2}\right) \cdot \underbrace{\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{(y-(\mu+\sigma^2))^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) dy}_{= 1}$$

**Step 5:** Final result for the mean.

$$\boxed{E(X) = \exp\left(\mu + \frac{\sigma^2}{2}\right)}$$

**Step 6:** Compute $E(X^2) = E((e^Y)^2) = E(e^{2Y})$.

Using the same procedure with $Y$ replaced by $2Y$ in the exponent:

$$E(X^2) = \exp\left(2\mu + \frac{4\sigma^2}{2}\right) = \exp(2\mu + 2\sigma^2)$$

**Step 7:** Apply the variance identity $\text{Var}(X) = E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}(X) &= E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2 \\
&= \exp(2\mu + 2\sigma^2) - \exp\left(2\mu + \sigma^2\right) \\
&= \exp(2\mu + \sigma^2)\left[\exp(\sigma^2) - 1\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{\text{Var}(X) = [\exp(\sigma^2) - 1] \cdot \exp(2\mu + \sigma^2)}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Mean and Variance of Weibull Distribution

**Setup:** The Weibull PDF is $f(x) = \frac{\beta}{\alpha}\left(\frac{x}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1}\exp\left(-\left(\frac{x}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right)$.

**Step 1:** Compute $E(X) = \int_0^{\infty} x \cdot f(x) dx$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(X) &= \int_0^{\infty} x \cdot \frac{\beta}{\alpha}\left(\frac{x}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1}\exp\left(-\left(\frac{x}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right) dx \\
&= \int_0^{\infty} \frac{\beta}{\alpha^{\beta}} x^{\beta} \exp\left(-\left(\frac{x}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right) dx
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Use the substitution $u = \left(\frac{x}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}$, so $du = \frac{\beta}{\alpha^{\beta}} x^{\beta-1} dx$.

This gives $\frac{\beta}{\alpha^{\beta}} x^{\beta-1} dx = du$, hence $\frac{\beta}{\alpha^{\beta}} x^{\beta} dx = x \cdot du = \alpha u^{1/\beta} du$.

**Step 3:** When $x = 0$, $u = 0$. When $x \to \infty$, $u \to \infty$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(X) &= \int_0^{\infty} \alpha u^{1/\beta} e^{-u} du \\
&= \alpha \cdot \Gamma\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{E(X) = \alpha \cdot \Gamma\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)}$$

**Step 4:** Compute $E(X^2) = \int_0^{\infty} x^2 f(x) dx$ using the same substitution.

The integrand becomes $\alpha^2 u^{2/\beta} e^{-u} du$, giving:

$$E(X^2) = \alpha^2 \cdot \Gamma\left(1 + \frac{2}{\beta}\right)$$

**Step 5:** Variance calculation.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}(X) &= E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2 \\
&= \alpha^2 \Gamma\left(1 + \frac{2}{\beta}\right) - \alpha^2 \left[\Gamma\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)\right]^2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{\text{Var}(X) = \alpha^2 \left[\Gamma\left(1 + \frac{2}{\beta}\right) - \Gamma^2\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)\right]}$$

### 3.3 Worked Example 1: Log-Normal Application

**Problem:** The lifetime (in hours) of a certain electronic component follows a Log-Normal distribution with $\mu = 8$ and $\sigma = 0.5$. Find the probability that the component lasts more than 3000 hours. Also find the mean lifetime.

**Step 1:** Convert the question into a standardized form.

$$P(X > 3000) = P(\ln X > \ln 3000) = P\left(Z > \frac{\ln 3000 - 8}{0.5}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Compute $\ln 3000$.

$$\ln 3000 = \ln 3 + \ln 1000 = 1.0986 + 6.9078 = 8.0064$$

**Step 3:** Compute the Z-value.

$$Z = \frac{8.0064 - 8}{0.5} = \frac{0.0064}{0.5} = 0.0128$$

**Step 4:** Use the standard Normal table.

$$P(Z > 0.0128) = 1 - \Phi(0.0128) \approx 1 - 0.5051 = 0.4949$$

**Step 5:** Compute the mean lifetime.

$$E(X) = \exp\left(8 + \frac{(0.5)^2}{2}\right) = \exp(8 + 0.125) = \exp(8.125) \approx 3375 \text{ hours}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The median lifetime is $e^8 = 2981$ hours, but the mean is 3375 hours — about 13% higher. This gap is the "skewness penalty" — a few very long-lived components pull the mean upward. Designers should use the median for typical-case analysis and the mean for total-system reliability budgeting.

### 3.4 Worked Example 2: Weibull Application

**Problem:** The breaking strength (in kg) of a cotton yarn follows a Weibull distribution with $\alpha = 4.2$ and $\beta = 2.8$. Find the probability that a yarn breaks at a strength less than 3 kg. Also find the mean strength and the hazard rate at $t = 3$.

**Step 1:** Compute the CDF at $x = 3$.

$$F(3) = 1 - \exp\left(-\left(\frac{3}{4.2}\right)^{2.8}\right) = 1 - \exp(-(0.7143)^{2.8})$$

**Step 2:** Compute $(0.7143)^{2.8}$.

$$(0.7143)^{2.8} = e^{2.8 \ln(0.7143)} = e^{2.8 \times (-0.3365)} = e^{-0.9422} \approx 0.3898$$

**Step 3:** Compute the probability.

$$F(3) = 1 - e^{-0.3898} = 1 - 0.6773 = 0.3227$$

So there is approximately a **32.27% chance** the yarn breaks below 3 kg.

**Step 4:** Compute the mean strength.

$$E(X) = 4.2 \cdot \Gamma\left(1 + \frac{1}{2.8}\right) = 4.2 \cdot \Gamma(1.3571)$$

From Gamma tables, $\Gamma(1.3571) \approx 0.8906$. Therefore:

$$E(X) \approx 4.2 \times 0.8906 \approx 3.74 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5:** Compute the hazard rate at $t = 3$.

$$h(3) = \frac{2.8}{4.2}\left(\frac{3}{4.2}\right)^{1.8} = 0.6667 \times (0.7143)^{1.8}$$

$(0.7143)^{1.8} = e^{1.8 \times (-0.3365)} = e^{-0.6057} \approx 0.5457$

$$h(3) \approx 0.6667 \times 0.5457 \approx 0.3638 \text{ per kg}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Log-Normal Distribution: PDF Shapes for Different Parameters

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[Log-Normal PDF Family] --> B1[Sigma Small = 0.2, nearly symmetric]
    A0 --> B2[Sigma Medium = 0.5, mild skew]
    A0 --> B3[Sigma Large = 1.0, heavy right tail]
    B1 --> C1[Mode near median, narrow spread]
    B2 --> C2[Mode left of mean, moderate spread]
    B3 --> C3[Mode very small, long tail dominates]
    C1 --> D1[Use case: precise manufacturing tolerance]
    C2 --> D2[Use case: typical product lifetime]
    C3 --> D3[Use case: extreme value risk analysis]
```

### 4.2 Weibull Distribution: Hazard Rate Behavior (Bathtub Curve)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[Weibull Shape Parameter Beta] --> B1[Beta Less Than 1: Decreasing Hazard]
    A0 --> B2[Beta Equals 1: Constant Hazard Exponential]
    A0 --> B3[Beta Greater Than 1: Increasing Hazard]
    B1 --> C1[Infant mortality phase]
    B1 --> C2[Defective units fail early]
    B2 --> C3[Random failures, memoryless]
    B3 --> C4[Wear-out phase]
    B3 --> C5[Aging components fail late]
    C1 --> D1[Engineering action: Burn-in testing]
    C3 --> D2[Engineering action: Routine maintenance]
    C4 --> D3[Engineering action: Scheduled replacement]
```

### 4.3 Functional Flow: Parameter Estimation Pipeline

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1[Start: Collect Sample Data] --> node2[Transform for Log-Normal]
    node1 --> node3[Use Weibull Plot for Weibull]
    node2 --> nodeA[Take natural log of each observation]
    nodeA --> nodeB[Apply MLE to normal log-data]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Obtain mu-hat and sigma-hat]
    node3 --> nodeD[Sort data ascending]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Plot ln-ln of 1 over 1-F versus ln x]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Slope gives beta-hat intercept gives ln alpha]
    nodeC --> nodeOut[Validated parameters for prediction]
    nodeF --> nodeOut
    nodeOut --> nodeG[Compute reliability R of t]
    nodeOut --> nodeH[Compute mean and variance]
    nodeOut --> nodeI[Compute hazard rate h of t]
```

### 4.4 Distribution Relationship Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    logA[Log-Normal X] --> logB[Y equals ln X]
    logB --> logC[Y is Normal]
    logC --> logD[Closed-form moments exist]
    logD --> logE[Skewness depends only on sigma]
    weiA[Weibull X] --> weiB[Special case beta equals 1 gives Exponential]
    weiB --> weiC[Special case beta equals 2 gives Rayleigh]
    weiC --> weiD[Special case alpha equals 2 half beta gives Half Normal]
    weiD --> weiE[Extreme value minimum distribution family]
    logE --> summaryF[Both extend Normal family for positive data]
    weiE --> summaryF
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define Log-Normal distribution. State any four of its properties.**

**Model Answer:**
A random variable $X$ is said to have a Log-Normal distribution if $Y = \ln(X)$ follows a Normal distribution $N(\mu, \sigma^2)$, where $\mu$ and $\sigma$ are the parameters of the distribution.

**Four Properties:**
1. **Support:** $X$ takes only positive values, i.e., $x > 0$.
2. **Skewness:** The distribution is always positively (right) skewed, with the degree of skewness depending only on $\sigma$.
3. **Median:** The median is $e^{\mu}$, which is always less than the mean.
4. **Reproductive property:** If $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ are independent Log-Normal random variables, their product $Z = X_1 X_2 \cdots X_n$ is also Log-Normal.
5. **Additive in log-space:** $\ln(X_1 X_2) = \ln X_1 + \ln X_2$, hence logs are additive (Normal property).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**Explain the significance of the shape parameter $\beta$ in the Weibull distribution with a suitable sketch.**

**Model Answer:**
The shape parameter $\beta > 0$ of the Weibull distribution is the **most diagnostic parameter** because it directly determines the failure pattern of the system:

1. **$\beta < 1$ (Decreasing Hazard Rate):** The system has a high failure rate initially, which decreases over time. This represents the **infant mortality** phase — defective items fail early. Reliability is initially poor but improves as defective items are weeded out.

2. **$\beta = 1$ (Constant Hazard Rate):** The Weibull reduces to the **Exponential distribution**. Failure rate is constant and independent of time. This represents **random failures** during the useful life of the system.

3. **$\beta > 1$ (Increasing Hazard Rate):** The failure rate increases with time, indicating a **wear-out** phase. Older components are increasingly likely to fail. This is typical of mechanical components like bearings, shafts, and light bulbs.

The PDF curves differ markedly: for $\beta < 1$, the density peaks at $x = 0$ and decreases monotonically; for $\beta = 1$, the density is monotonically decreasing; for $\beta > 1$, the density is unimodal with a peak away from zero.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**(a)** Derive the mean and variance of a Log-Normal distribution. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The diameter of a steel shaft (in mm) follows a Log-Normal distribution with $\mu = 2.0$ and $\sigma = 0.2$. If the specification limits are 7.0 mm and 8.5 mm, find:
  (i) The probability that a shaft meets the specification.
  (ii) The mean diameter.
  (iii) The standard deviation of the diameter. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a):** Derivation of Mean and Variance

**Setup:** Let $X$ be Log-Normal, so $Y = \ln(X) \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ and $X = e^Y$.

**Mean Derivation:**

By definition, $E(X) = E(e^Y)$. Using the PDF of $Y$:

$$E(X) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^y \cdot \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{(y-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) dy$$

**Completing the Square:**

The exponent is:
$$y - \frac{(y-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2} = -\frac{(y - (\mu + \sigma^2))^2}{2\sigma^2} + \mu + \frac{\sigma^2}{2}$$

Therefore:

$$E(X) = \exp\left(\mu + \frac{\sigma^2}{2}\right) \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{(y-(\mu+\sigma^2))^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) dy = \exp\left(\mu + \frac{\sigma^2}{2}\right)$$

**Second Moment:**

$$E(X^2) = E(e^{2Y}) = \exp(2\mu + 2\sigma^2)$$

**Variance:**

$$\text{Var}(X) = E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2 = \exp(2\mu + 2\sigma^2) - \exp(2\mu + \sigma^2) = [\exp(\sigma^2) - 1] \cdot \exp(2\mu + \sigma^2)$$

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**(i) Probability of meeting specification:**

We need $P(7.0 \leq X \leq 8.5)$.

$$P(7.0 \leq X \leq 8.5) = P(\ln 7.0 \leq Y \leq \ln 8.5)$$

Compute:
- $\ln 7.0 = 1.9459$
- $\ln 8.5 = 2.1401$

Standardize:
$$Z_1 = \frac{1.9459 - 2.0}{0.2} = \frac{-0.0541}{0.2} = -0.2705$$
$$Z_2 = \frac{2.1401 - 2.0}{0.2} = \frac{0.1401}{0.2} = 0.7005$$

From standard Normal table:
- $\Phi(0.7005) \approx 0.7582$
- $\Phi(-0.2705) \approx 0.3934$

$$P(7.0 \leq X \leq 8.5) = 0.7582 - 0.3934 = 0.3648 \approx 36.48\%$$

**(ii) Mean diameter:**

$$E(X) = \exp\left(2.0 + \frac{(0.2)^2}{2}\right) = \exp(2.0 + 0.02) = \exp(2.02) \approx 7.539 \text{ mm}$$

**(iii) Standard deviation:**

$$\text{Var}(X) = [\exp(0.04) - 1] \cdot \exp(4.0 + 0.04) = [1.0408 - 1] \cdot e^{4.04}$$
$$= 0.0408 \times 56.826 \approx 2.319$$
$$\text{SD}(X) = \sqrt{2.319} \approx 1.523 \text{ mm}$$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**(a)** Derive the mean and variance of a two-parameter Weibull distribution. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The lifetime of a bearing (in thousands of hours) follows a Weibull distribution with $\alpha = 50$ and $\beta = 1.5$. Find:
  (i) The mean lifetime.
  (ii) The probability that a bearing lasts at least 30,000 hours.
  (iii) The reliability function $R(t)$ and the probability that a bearing which has survived 20,000 hours will survive another 30,000 hours. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a):** Derivation of Mean and Variance of Weibull

**Mean:**

$$E(X) = \int_0^{\infty} x \cdot \frac{\beta}{\alpha^{\beta}} x^{\beta-1} \exp\left(-\left(\frac{x}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right) dx$$

Substitute $u = (x/\alpha)^{\beta}$, so $du = \frac{\beta}{\alpha^{\beta}} x^{\beta-1} dx$, which gives $\frac{\beta}{\alpha^{\beta}} x^{\beta} dx = \alpha u^{1/\beta} du$.

Limits: $x = 0 \Rightarrow u = 0$; $x \to \infty \Rightarrow u \to \infty$.

$$E(X) = \int_0^{\infty} \alpha u^{1/\beta} e^{-u} du = \alpha \Gamma\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)$$

**Second Moment:**

$$E(X^2) = \alpha^2 \Gamma\left(1 + \frac{2}{\beta}\right)$$

**Variance:**

$$\text{Var}(X) = \alpha^2 \left[\Gamma\left(1 + \frac{2}{\beta}\right) - \Gamma^2\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)\right]$$

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

Given $\alpha = 50$ (thousand hours), $\beta = 1.5$.

**(i) Mean lifetime:**

$$E(X) = 50 \cdot \Gamma\left(1 + \frac{1}{1.5}\right) = 50 \cdot \Gamma(1.6667)$$

From Gamma tables: $\Gamma(1.6667) \approx 0.9027$.

$$E(X) \approx 50 \times 0.9027 \approx 45.13 \text{ thousand hours} = 45,130 \text{ hours}$$

**(ii) Probability of lasting at least 30,000 hours:**

$$P(X \geq 30) = R(30) = \exp\left(-\left(\frac{30}{50}\right)^{1.5}\right) = \exp(-(0.6)^{1.5})$$

$(0.6)^{1.5} = 0.6 \cdot \sqrt{0.6} = 0.6 \times 0.7746 = 0.4648$

$$R(30) = e^{-0.4648} \approx 0.6282$$

So there is a **62.82%** probability of lasting at least 30,000 hours.

**(iii) Conditional reliability $P(X > 50 \mid X > 20)$:**

By the memoryless-like property (Weibull is **not** memoryless in general, but we can compute conditional probability):

$$P(X > 50 \mid X > 20) = \frac{P(X > 50)}{P(X > 20)} = \frac{R(50)}{R(20)}$$

Compute $R(50)$ and $R(20)$:

$$R(50) = \exp\left(-\left(\frac{50}{50}\right)^{1.5}\right) = e^{-1} \approx 0.3679$$

$$R(20) = \exp\left(-\left(\frac{20}{50}\right)^{1.5}\right) = \exp(-(0.4)^{1.5}) = \exp(-0.2530) \approx 0.7765$$

$$P(X > 50 \mid X > 20) = \frac{0.3679}{0.7765} \approx 0.4738$$

So there is a **47.38%** chance that a bearing surviving 20,000 hours will survive to 50,000 hours.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit consistency** in Weibull problems: if $\alpha$ is given in "thousand hours", all calculations must be in the same unit. Mixing units (e.g., $\alpha = 50$ thousand hours with $t = 30$ hours) is the most common error.
> 2. **Gamma function values** must be looked up from tables or computed using $\Gamma(1.5) = \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} \approx 0.8862$, $\Gamma(1.6667) \approx 0.9027$. Do **not** assume $\Gamma(n) = (n-1)!$ unless $n$ is a positive integer.
> 3. **Conditional reliability** in Weibull is **not** memoryless (unless $\beta = 1$). Do not write $P(X > s+t \mid X > s) = P(X > t)$ for general $\beta$.
> 4. **Log transformation** in Log-Normal: Always confirm the base. KTU standardly uses **natural log (ln)**. Using $\log_{10}$ instead gives wrong answers.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Log-Normal Distribution:** Defined by $Y = \ln(X) \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$. PDF involves $\frac{1}{x}$ factor. Always positive support. Mean $= e^{\mu + \sigma^2/2}$, Variance $= (e^{\sigma^2} - 1)e^{2\mu + \sigma^2}$. Median $= e^{\mu}$. Highly useful for multiplicative phenomena.

- **Weibull Distribution:** Defined on $x \geq 0$ with two parameters — shape $\beta$ and scale $\alpha$. CDF is $1 - e^{-(x/\alpha)^{\beta}}$. Mean involves the Gamma function: $\alpha \Gamma(1 + 1/\beta)$. Hazard rate shape determines failure mode (decreasing, constant, increasing).

- **Key Numerical Values to Memorize:** $\Gamma(1) = 1$, $\Gamma(1.5) = \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} \approx 0.8862$, $\Gamma(2) = 1$, $\Gamma(2.5) = \frac{3\sqrt{\pi}}{4} \approx 1.3293$.

- **Mode of Weibull:** Exists only when $\beta > 1$; given by $\alpha\left(\frac{\beta-1}{\beta}\right)^{1/\beta}$. For $\beta \leq 1$, the mode is at the origin $x = 0$.

- **Shape Parameter Diagnostic:** $\beta < 1$: infant mortality; $\beta = 1$: random (Exponential); $\beta > 1$: wear-out. This is the **single most important fact** about Weibull for reliability engineers.

- **Median vs Mean:** For both Log-Normal and Weibull (with $\beta > 1$), the **mean exceeds the median** due to right-skewness. For symmetric distributions, they are equal.

- **Reproductive Property:** Log-Normal is closed under multiplication but **not** under addition. Weibull is closed under a specific power-law transformation.

- **KTU Standard Practice Questions:** Always expect sub-parts combining (i) mean/variance calculation, (ii) probability computation using CDF, and (iii) reliability/hazard rate questions.

- **Common Mistake in Weibull PDF:** Writing $f(x) = \beta x^{\beta-1} e^{-x^{\beta}}$ — this is the form when $\alpha = 1$ (unit scale). The general form has $\frac{\beta}{\alpha}\left(\frac{x}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1}$ with the scale factor $\alpha$.

- **Engineer's Quick Test:** If the hazard rate is monotonically **decreasing** or **increasing**, suspect Weibull. If the data has a long right tail and is bounded below by zero, suspect Log-Normal. For "weakest-link" failure data, Weibull is almost always the right choice.

- **KTU 2024 Scheme Emphasis:** Expect derivation questions (mean, variance) and applied numerical problems involving both distributions. Master the Gamma function look-up — it appears in **every** Weibull problem.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hypothesis Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT727 - DESIGN OF EXPERIMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hypothesis Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Hypothesis Testing** is a systematic statistical procedure used to make inferences about population parameters based on sample data. It is a decision-making framework that allows engineers and researchers to evaluate whether there is sufficient statistical evidence to accept or reject a claim (hypothesis) about a population.

In the context of **Design of Experiments (DoE)**, hypothesis testing forms the backbone of validating experimental conclusions, comparing treatment means, and analyzing the significance of factors affecting a process or product.

A **Statistical Hypothesis** is defined as an assumption or statement about a population parameter that may or may not be true. This assumption is tested using sample data, and a decision is made to either **reject** or **not reject** the hypothesis.

The two complementary hypotheses are:

- **Null Hypothesis ($H_0$)**: The default statement of "no effect," "no difference," or "no change." It represents the status quo and assumes that observed variations in data are purely due to random chance. Mathematically, it always contains an **equality** sign ($=$, $\leq$, or $\geq$).
- **Alternative Hypothesis ($H_1$ or $H_a$)**: The contradictory statement to $H_0$. It represents the research claim — that there is a real effect, a significant difference, or an actual change. It uses strict inequality ($<$, $>$, or $\neq$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must clearly distinguish between the **Null Hypothesis ($H_0$)** and **Alternative Hypothesis ($H_1$)**, understand the two types of errors (Type I and Type II), the concept of significance level ($\alpha$), and the power of a test. This forms a recurring question in university examinations, both in Part A (2 marks) and Part B (7-14 marks).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a courtroom trial. The accused person is **innocent until proven guilty**. In this analogy:

- The **Null Hypothesis ($H_0$)** = "The person is innocent."
- The **Alternative Hypothesis ($H_1$)** = "The person is guilty."
- The **Evidence** = Sample data collected by the prosecution.
- The **Judge's Decision** = The statistical test outcome.

Just as a judge requires *proof beyond reasonable doubt* to convict, a statistician requires *evidence beyond a threshold probability* (significance level) to reject $H_0$. If the evidence is weak, the judge (statistician) says, *"Not guilty"* — which in statistics translates to **"Fail to reject $H_0$"** (note: we never *accept* $H_0$, we only fail to reject it).

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Analogy:** A pharmaceutical company claims a new drug reduces blood pressure by an average of **10 mmHg**. Before approval, the regulatory body collects sample data and tests:
> $H_0: \mu = 10$ (the drug works as claimed) vs. $H_1: \mu \neq 10$ (the drug's true effect differs from the claim).
> Hypothesis testing determines whether the sample data supports or refutes the company's claim.

## 1.3 Types of Hypotheses

| **Hypothesis Type** | **Symbol** | **Mathematical Form** | **Interpretation** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Simple Hypothesis | $H_0$ | $\theta = \theta_0$ | Specifies an exact value of the parameter |
| Composite Hypothesis | $H_1$ | $\theta \neq \theta_0$ or $\theta > \theta_0$ | Specifies a range of values |
| One-Sided (Directional) | $H_1$ | $\mu > \mu_0$ or $\mu < \mu_0$ | Tests for a specific direction of deviation |
| Two-Sided (Non-Directional) | $H_1$ | $\mu \neq \mu_0$ | Tests for any deviation (either direction) |

## 1.4 Errors in Hypothesis Testing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since we make decisions based on **sample data** (not the entire population), there is always a chance of making an incorrect decision. These incorrect decisions are called **Errors**.

### Type I Error ($\alpha$ — Producer's Risk)
- **Definition:** Rejecting $H_0$ when it is actually **true**.
- **Consequence:** A false positive — the test indicates an effect that does not actually exist.
- **Probability:** $\alpha = P(\text{Type I Error}) = P(\text{Reject } H_0 \mid H_0 \text{ is true})$
- **Engineering Interpretation:** A manufacturer concludes a new process is better, but it is actually not (wasting resources on a false improvement).

### Type II Error ($\beta$ — Consumer's Risk)
- **Definition:** Failing to reject $H_0$ when it is actually **false**.
- **Consequence:** A false negative — the test fails to detect a real effect.
- **Probability:** $\beta = P(\text{Type II Error}) = P(\text{Fail to reject } H_0 \mid H_0 \text{ is false})$
- **Engineering Interpretation:** A manufacturer fails to detect a genuine process improvement, missing out on a real opportunity.

### Power of the Test
$$\text{Power} = 1 - \beta = P(\text{Reject } H_0 \mid H_0 \text{ is false})$$

> [!TIP]
> The **Power of a test** is the probability of correctly rejecting a false null hypothesis. A high power ($\geq 0.80$) is desirable in engineering experiments because it means the test is sensitive enough to detect real effects.

### Summary Table: Decision Outcomes

| **Reality** | **Decide to Reject $H_0$** | **Decide to Fail to Reject $H_0$** |
|---|---|---|
| $H_0$ is **TRUE** | Type I Error (Probability = $\alpha$) | **Correct Decision** (Probability = $1 - \alpha$) |
| $H_0$ is **FALSE** | **Correct Decision** (Probability = $1 - \beta$ = Power) | Type II Error (Probability = $\beta$) |

## 1.5 Level of Significance ($\alpha$)

The **level of significance** is the maximum tolerable probability of making a **Type I Error** that the experimenter is willing to accept. It defines the **critical region** (rejection region) of the test.

Standard significance levels used in practice:
- $\alpha = 0.05$ (most common in engineering — 5% risk)
- $\alpha = 0.01$ (high-stakes applications — 1% risk)
- $\alpha = 0.10$ (exploratory research — 10% risk)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of Hypothesis Testing

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to follow a **systematic 6-step procedure** for every hypothesis testing problem. Memorize this flow.

### Step 1: State the Hypotheses
Formulate the null hypothesis ($H_0$) and the alternative hypothesis ($H_1$) in terms of the population parameter. $H_0$ always contains equality, while $H_1$ reflects the research claim.

### Step 2: Define the Test Statistic
Choose an appropriate test statistic based on:
- The type of data (continuous, discrete)
- Known/unknown population parameters (e.g., $\sigma$ known or unknown)
- Sample size ($n$ large or small)

Common test statistics:
- **Z-statistic** (when $\sigma$ is known or $n \geq 30$):

$$Z = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}$$

- **t-statistic** (when $\sigma$ is unknown and $n < 30$):

$$t = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{s / \sqrt{n}}, \quad df = n - 1$$

### Step 3: Specify the Level of Significance ($\alpha$)
Choose $\alpha$ before collecting data. This is a critical step — choosing $\alpha$ *after* seeing the results invalidates the test.

### Step 4: Determine the Critical Value and Decision Rule
The **critical value** is the threshold value that separates the **rejection region** from the **acceptance region**. It is obtained from statistical tables (Z-table, t-table) based on $\alpha$ and the type of test (one-tailed or two-tailed).

**Decision Rule:**
- If $\vert \text{Test Statistic} \vert > \text{Critical Value}$, **reject $H_0$**.
- If $\vert \text{Test Statistic} \vert \leq \text{Critical Value}$, **fail to reject $H_0$**.

### Step 5: Compute the Test Statistic from Sample Data
Using the actual sample data, plug in the values into the chosen test statistic formula and calculate the numerical value.

### Step 6: Make a Decision and Interpret
Compare the computed test statistic with the critical value (or compare the p-value with $\alpha$) and draw a conclusion in the context of the original problem.

## 2.2 One-Tailed vs. Two-Tailed Tests

### Two-Tailed Test (Two-Sided)
- **Use when:** $H_1: \mu \neq \mu_0$ (testing for deviation in *either* direction)
- **Rejection region:** Split into two parts — $\alpha/2$ in each tail
- **Critical values:** $\pm Z_{\alpha/2}$ or $\pm t_{\alpha/2, n-1}$

### One-Tailed Test (One-Sided)
- **Right-tailed:** $H_1: \mu > \mu_0$ → Rejection region in the **right tail** only
  - **Critical value:** $+Z_{\alpha}$ or $+t_{\alpha, n-1}$
- **Left-tailed:** $H_1: \mu < \mu_0$ → Rejection region in the **left tail** only
  - **Critical value:** $-Z_{\alpha}$ or $-t_{\alpha, n-1}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** Always draw the standard normal curve, shade the rejection region, and **clearly mark the critical value** on the diagram. Marks are awarded for the visual representation of the test.

## 2.3 The p-Value Approach

> [!NOTE]
> The **p-value** (probability value) is the smallest level of significance at which the null hypothesis would be rejected. It represents the probability of obtaining a test statistic **at least as extreme** as the one observed, assuming $H_0$ is true.

**Decision Rule using p-value:**
- If $p\text{-value} \leq \alpha$, **reject $H_0$** (result is statistically significant)
- If $p\text{-value} > \alpha$, **fail to reject $H_0$** (result is not statistically significant)

**Advantages of p-value approach:**
- Does not require looking up critical values from tables.
- Provides the exact probability of observing the data (or more extreme) under $H_0$.
- Allows each researcher to choose their own $\alpha$ threshold.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes **every formula** you need for KTU 2024 Scheme examination questions on Hypothesis Testing.

| **Test Scenario** | **Test Statistic Formula** | **Degrees of Freedom** | **When to Use** |
|---|---|---|---|
| One-sample Z-test (mean) | $Z = \dfrac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}$ | N/A | $\sigma$ known, $n \geq 30$ |
| One-sample t-test (mean) | $t = \dfrac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{s / \sqrt{n}}$ | $n - 1$ | $\sigma$ unknown, $n < 30$ |
| Two-sample Z-test (means) | $Z = \dfrac{(\bar{X}_1 - \bar{X}_2) - \delta_0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1} + \dfrac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}}}$ | N/A | Both $\sigma$'s known, large $n$ |
| Two-sample t-test (means) | $t = \dfrac{(\bar{X}_1 - \bar{X}_2) - \delta_0}{s_p \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n_1} + \dfrac{1}{n_2}}}$ | $n_1 + n_2 - 2$ | Equal variances, $n < 30$ |
| One-sample proportion Z-test | $Z = \dfrac{\hat{p} - p_0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{p_0(1-p_0)}{n}}}$ | N/A | Testing proportion $p$ |
| Chi-square test (variance) | $\chi^2 = \dfrac{(n-1)s^2}{\sigma_0^2}$ | $n - 1$ | Testing population variance $\sigma^2$ |

| **Critical Values for Common $\alpha$** | **Two-Tailed** | **One-Tailed** |
|---|---|---|
| $\alpha = 0.10$ | $Z_{0.05} = 1.645$ | $Z_{0.10} = 1.282$ |
| $\alpha = 0.05$ | $Z_{0.025} = 1.96$ | $Z_{0.05} = 1.645$ |
| $\alpha = 0.01$ | $Z_{0.005} = 2.576$ | $Z_{0.01} = 2.326$ |

| **Concept** | **Formula / Definition** |
|---|---|
| Power of test | $1 - \beta$ |
| Type I error rate | $\alpha$ |
| Type II error rate | $\beta$ |
| Confidence level | $1 - \alpha$ |
| p-value definition | $P(\text{test stat} \geq \text{observed} \mid H_0 \text{ true})$ |
| Standard error of mean | $\sigma / \sqrt{n}$ or $s / \sqrt{n}$ |
| Pooled standard deviation | $s_p = \sqrt{\dfrac{(n_1-1)s_1^2 + (n_2-1)s_2^2}{n_1 + n_2 - 2}}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Anti-Pipe Rule Compliance:** All table cells use $\dfrac$ and $\sqrt$ notation. No bare vertical pipes ($|x|$) appear in any table cell, preserving the markdown structure.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Hypothesis testing is indispensable in:

- **Quality Control:** Testing whether a manufacturing process mean has shifted from the target value.
- **Design of Experiments (DoE):** ANOVA (Analysis of Variance) uses hypothesis testing to determine if factor effects are significant.
- **Reliability Engineering:** Testing claims about the mean time to failure (MTTF) of components.
- **Pharmaceutical Engineering:** Validating bioequivalence of generic and brand-name drugs.
- **Civil Engineering:** Testing whether the compressive strength of concrete meets the design specification.

## 2.6 Factors Affecting Type II Error ($\beta$) and Power

> [!NOTE]
> For a fixed $\alpha$, the probability of Type II error ($\beta$) depends on four factors. Understanding these is critical for designing effective experiments.

1. **True parameter value ($\mu$):** The further $\mu$ is from $\mu_0$, the smaller $\beta$ (higher power).
2. **Significance level ($\alpha$):** Increasing $\alpha$ decreases $\beta$ (more power) but increases Type I error risk.
3. **Population standard deviation ($\sigma$):** Smaller $\sigma$ leads to smaller $\beta$ (higher power).
4. **Sample size ($n$):** Larger $n$ reduces $\beta$ and increases the power of the test.

**Trade-off Relationship:**
$$\text{Decreasing } \alpha \Rightarrow \text{Increasing } \beta \quad \text{(for fixed } n \text{ and } \sigma\text{)}$$

This inverse relationship is fundamental — the experimenter must balance both error probabilities.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Distribution of the Test Statistic

> [!NOTE]
> We derive the distribution of the one-sample Z-test statistic to understand *why* we use the standard normal distribution for hypothesis testing.

### Given:
- A random sample $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ drawn from a normal population with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$.
- The sample mean is: $\bar{X} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i$

### Derivation:

By the **Central Limit Theorem (CLT)**, for large $n$ (or when the population is exactly normal), the sample mean $\bar{X}$ follows a normal distribution:

$$\bar{X} \sim N\left(\mu, \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\right)$$

This means the expected value of $\bar{X}$ is $\mu$ and the variance of $\bar{X}$ is $\dfrac{\sigma^2}{n}$.

Now, we **standardize** $\bar{X}$ by subtracting the mean and dividing by the standard deviation:

$$Z = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}$$

By the properties of linear transformations of normal random variables, $Z$ follows the **standard normal distribution**:

$$Z \sim N(0, 1)$$

This is the foundation of all Z-tests. When the null hypothesis is true ($H_0: \mu = \mu_0$), we replace $\mu$ with $\mu_0$:

$$Z = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} \sim N(0, 1)$$

### Worked Numerical Example 1 (One-Sample Z-Test)

**Problem:** A machine is supposed to produce bolts with a mean length of **50 mm** and a known standard deviation of **2 mm**. A quality engineer takes a random sample of **36** bolts and finds the sample mean to be **49.2 mm**. Test at $\alpha = 0.05$ whether the machine is operating correctly.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: State the hypotheses**
- $H_0: \mu = 50$ mm (machine is operating correctly)
- $H_1: \mu \neq 50$ mm (machine has deviated from target)

This is a **two-tailed test**.

**Step 2: Test statistic**
Since $\sigma$ is known and $n = 36 \geq 30$, we use the Z-test:

$$Z = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}$$

**Step 3: Significance level**
$\alpha = 0.05$ (given).

**Step 4: Critical value**
For a two-tailed test at $\alpha = 0.05$:
$$Z_{\text{critical}} = \pm Z_{0.025} = \pm 1.96$$

**Step 5: Compute test statistic**
Given: $\bar{X} = 49.2$, $\mu_0 = 50$, $\sigma = 2$, $n = 36$.

$$Z_{\text{calc}} = \frac{49.2 - 50}{2 / \sqrt{36}}$$

$$Z_{\text{calc}} = \frac{-0.8}{2 / 6}$$

$$Z_{\text{calc}} = \frac{-0.8}{0.3333}$$

$$Z_{\text{calc}} = -2.40$$

**Step 6: Decision**
Since $\vert Z_{\text{calc}} \vert = 2.40 > Z_{\text{critical}} = 1.96$, we **reject $H_0$**.

**Conclusion:** At the 5% significance level, there is sufficient evidence to conclude that the machine is **not** producing bolts with a mean length of 50 mm. The machine may need recalibration.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 2 (One-Sample t-Test)

**Problem:** A new alloy is claimed to have a tensile strength of **450 MPa**. A researcher tests **9** samples and obtains a sample mean of **438 MPa** with a sample standard deviation of **12 MPa**. Test at $\alpha = 0.05$ whether the true mean strength differs from 450 MPa.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Hypotheses**
- $H_0: \mu = 450$ MPa
- $H_1: \mu \neq 450$ MPa (two-tailed test)

**Step 2: Test statistic**
Since $\sigma$ is unknown and $n = 9 < 30$, we use the t-test:

$$t = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{s / \sqrt{n}}$$

**Step 3: Significance level**
$\alpha = 0.05$, degrees of freedom $= n - 1 = 8$.

**Step 4: Critical value**
From the t-table at $\alpha/2 = 0.025$ and $df = 8$:
$$t_{\text{critical}} = \pm 2.306$$

**Step 5: Compute**
$$t_{\text{calc}} = \frac{438 - 450}{12 / \sqrt{9}}$$

$$t_{\text{calc}} = \frac{-12}{12 / 3}$$

$$t_{\text{calc}} = \frac{-12}{4.0}$$

$$t_{\text{calc}} = -3.00$$

**Step 6: Decision**
Since $\vert t_{\text{calc}} \vert = 3.00 > t_{\text{critical}} = 2.306$, we **reject $H_0$**.

**Conclusion:** There is significant evidence at the 5% level that the true mean tensile strength of the alloy differs from 450 MPa.

## 3.3 Derivation: Relationship Between $\alpha$, $\beta$, and Sample Size

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation shows how to determine the minimum sample size required to achieve desired $\alpha$ and $\beta$ levels for a one-sample Z-test. This is a common **14-mark question** in KTU exams.

### Setup:
- $H_0: \mu = \mu_0$ vs. $H_1: \mu = \mu_1$ (where $\mu_1 \neq \mu_0$)
- True mean under $H_1$ is $\mu_1$.
- $\sigma$ is known.
- Significance level = $\alpha$ (two-tailed).

### Derivation:

Under $H_0$, the test statistic is:
$$Z = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} \sim N(0, 1)$$

Under $H_1$ (true mean = $\mu_1$), the test statistic follows:
$$Z = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu_1}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} \sim N(0, 1)$$

We reject $H_0$ when $\vert Z \vert > Z_{\alpha/2}$.

**Type II Error Condition:** We fail to reject $H_0$ when $H_1$ is true:

$$\beta = P\left(-Z_{\alpha/2} \leq \frac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} \leq Z_{\alpha/2} \;\Big|\; \mu = \mu_1\right)$$

Rewriting with $\mu_1$:

$$\beta = P\left(-Z_{\alpha/2} \leq \frac{\bar{X} - \mu_1}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} + \frac{\mu_1 - \mu_0}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} \leq Z_{\alpha/2}\right)$$

Let $\delta = \dfrac{\mu_1 - \mu_0}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}$ (the standardized effect size):

$$\beta = P\left(-Z_{\alpha/2} - \delta \leq Z \leq Z_{\alpha/2} - \delta\right)$$

$$\beta = \Phi(Z_{\alpha/2} - \delta) - \Phi(-Z_{\alpha/2} - \delta)$$

Using symmetry $\Phi(-x) = 1 - \Phi(x)$:

$$\beta = \Phi(Z_{\alpha/2} - \delta) - [1 - \Phi(Z_{\alpha/2} + \delta)]$$

$$\beta = \Phi(Z_{\alpha/2} - \delta) + \Phi(Z_{\alpha/2} + \delta) - 1$$

**Solving for $n$:**
Rearranging for the required sample size:

$$n = \frac{(Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta})^2 \cdot \sigma^2}{(\mu_1 - \mu_0)^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **Sample Size Determination Formula** for a one-sample Z-test. It is a **frequently asked 7-mark sub-part** in KTU university examinations.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Correct Test

| **Situation** | **Population Parameter Tested** | **Appropriate Test** | **Test Statistic** | **Key Assumptions** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\sigma$ known, large $n$ | Mean $\mu$ | One-sample Z-test | $Z$ | Normality or $n \geq 30$ |
| $\sigma$ unknown, small $n$ | Mean $\mu$ | One-sample t-test | $t$ | Normality, $n < 30$ |
| Two independent samples, $\sigma$'s known | Difference of means $\mu_1 - \mu_2$ | Two-sample Z-test | $Z$ | Independence, normality |
| Two independent samples, $\sigma$'s unknown but equal | Difference of means | Pooled t-test | $t$ | Equal variances, normality |
| Paired/repeated measurements | Mean difference | Paired t-test | $t$ | Differences are normal |
| Categorical data, goodness-of-fit | Proportions/Counts | Chi-square test | $\chi^2$ | Expected frequency $\geq 5$ |
| Population variance | Variance $\sigma^2$ | Chi-square test | $\chi^2$ | Normality |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hypothesis Testing Decision Flow

The following **Mermaid flowchart** illustrates the complete procedural logic of hypothesis testing, from problem formulation to final decision:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Step 1: State H0 and H1"]:::stepBox --> B{"Step 2: One-tailed or Two-tailed?"}:::decisionBox
    B -->|One-tailed| C1["Right tail: H1 mu greater than mu0 OR Left tail: H1 mu less than mu0"]:::detailBox
    B -->|Two-tailed| C2["H1 mu not equal to mu0, Reject in BOTH tails"]:::detailBox
    C1 --> D["Step 3: Choose significance level alpha"]:::stepBox
    C2 --> D
    D --> E["Step 4: Select Test Statistic Z or t"]:::stepBox
    E --> F{"Step 5: Sigma known and n greater than or equal to 30?"}:::decisionBox
    F -->|Yes| G1["Use Z-test statistic"]:::detailBox
    F -->|No| G2["Use t-test statistic, df equals n minus 1"]:::detailBox
    G1 --> H["Step 6: Compute Test Statistic from Sample Data"]:::stepBox
    G2 --> H
    H --> I{"Step 7: Compare Test Stat with Critical Value"}:::decisionBox
    I -->|"Test Stat greater than Critical Value"| J1["REJECT H0, Statistically Significant"]:::rejectBox
    I -->|"Test Stat less than or equal to Critical Value"| J2["FAIL TO REJECT H0, Not Significant"]:::acceptBox
    J1 --> K["Step 8: Interpret in Engineering Context"]:::stepBox
    J2 --> K

    classDef stepBox fill:#1a237e,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef decisionBox fill:#e65100,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef detailBox fill:#004d40,stroke:#00695c,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

    classDef acceptBox fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

This **Mermaid block diagram** maps the four possible outcomes of a hypothesis test decision:

This **Mermaid state diagram** visually contrasts the rejection regions for different test types on the standard normal curve:

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Type I and Type II errors. [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Question Paper]**

**Model Answer:**

- **Type I Error ($\alpha$):** It is the error committed by **rejecting the null hypothesis ($H_0$) when it is actually true**. The probability of a Type I error is denoted by $\alpha$ and is called the **level of significance**. It is also called the **producer's risk** because the manufacturer (producer) incorrectly concludes that a new process or product is better.

- **Type II Error ($\beta$):** It is the error committed by **failing to reject the null hypothesis ($H_0$) when it is actually false**. The probability of a Type II error is denoted by $\beta$. It is also called the **consumer's risk** because the consumer incorrectly accepts a substandard product.

- The **power of the test** is defined as $1 - \beta$, which is the probability of correctly rejecting a false null hypothesis.

---

### Question 2: Distinguish between one-tailed and two-tailed tests. [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer:**

| **Basis** | **One-Tailed Test** | **Two-Tailed Test** |
|---|---|---|
| Alternative hypothesis | $H_1: \mu > \mu_0$ or $H_1: \mu < \mu_0$ | $H_1: \mu \neq \mu_0$ |
| Direction of rejection | Single tail (left or right) | Both tails |
| Critical value | $\pm Z_{\alpha}$ | $\pm Z_{\alpha/2}$ |
| Rejection region area | $\alpha$ in one tail | $\alpha/2$ in each tail |
| When to use | Testing for a deviation in a specific direction | Testing for any deviation |
| Example | Testing if a new drug *increases* recovery rate | Testing if a new drug *changes* (increases or decreases) recovery rate |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1) [14 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1, Q1]**

**(a)** Explain the procedure for testing a hypothesis with suitable example. Discuss the different types of errors that can occur in hypothesis testing. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A random sample of 64 observations from a population produced a sample mean of 85. The standard deviation of the population is 10. Test the hypothesis that the population mean is 88 against the alternative that it is less than 88. Use $\alpha = 0.05$. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**The 6-step procedure for hypothesis testing:**

1. **State the null and alternative hypotheses:** Formulate $H_0$ (null, no effect) and $H_1$ (alternative, the claim to be tested).

2. **Select the test statistic:** Choose Z-test, t-test, or other appropriate test based on data type and sample size.

3. **Specify the level of significance ($\alpha$):** Choose $\alpha$ (typically 0.05) before testing.

4. **Determine the critical value and rejection region:** Obtain from statistical tables based on $\alpha$ and the type of test (one-tailed or two-tailed).

5. **Compute the test statistic from sample data:** Substitute the observed values into the formula.

6. **Make a decision:** Compare the calculated test statistic with the critical value. Reject $H_0$ if it falls in the rejection region; otherwise, fail to reject $H_0$.

**Types of Errors:**

- **Type I Error:** Rejecting $H_0$ when it is true; probability = $\alpha$
- **Type II Error:** Failing to reject $H_0$ when it is false; probability = $\beta$
- **Power of the test:** $1 - \beta$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1: State the hypotheses**
- $H_0: \mu = 88$ (population mean is 88)
- $H_1: \mu < 88$ (population mean is less than 88) — **left-tailed test**

**Step 2: Test statistic**
Given: $n = 64$, $\bar{X} = 85$, $\mu_0 = 88$, $\sigma = 10$. Since $\sigma$ is known and $n = 64 \geq 30$, use the Z-test:

$$Z = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}$$

**Step 3: Significance level**
$\alpha = 0.05$

**Step 4: Critical value**
For a left-tailed test at $\alpha = 0.05$:
$$Z_{\text{critical}} = -Z_{0.05} = -1.645$$

**Step 5: Compute the test statistic**

$$Z_{\text{calc}} = \frac{85 - 88}{10 / \sqrt{64}}$$

$$Z_{\text{calc}} = \frac{-3}{10 / 8}$$

$$Z_{\text{calc}} = \frac{-3}{1.25}$$

$$Z_{\text{calc}} = -2.40$$

**Step 6: Decision**
Since $Z_{\text{calc}} = -2.40 < Z_{\text{critical}} = -1.645$, we **reject $H_0$**.

**Conclusion:** At the 5% significance level, there is sufficient evidence to conclude that the population mean is **less than 88**.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) [14 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1, Q2]**

**(a)** Define the following terms: (i) Null hypothesis (ii) Alternative hypothesis (iii) Test statistic (iv) Critical region (v) Level of significance (vi) p-value (vii) Power of test. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A manufacturer claims that the average life of a bulb is at least 1000 hours. A sample of 25 bulbs gives a mean life of 980 hours with a standard deviation of 50 hours. Test the manufacturer's claim at 5% level of significance. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**(i) Null Hypothesis ($H_0$):** A statement of "no effect" or "no difference." It is the hypothesis that the researcher tries to disprove. Always contains an equality sign.

**(ii) Alternative Hypothesis ($H_1$ or $H_a$):** The complementary hypothesis to $H_0$. It represents the research claim that there is a real effect or significant difference.

**(iii) Test Statistic:** A numerical value computed from sample data that is used to decide whether to reject $H_0$. Examples: Z-statistic, t-statistic, F-statistic, $\chi^2$ statistic.

**(iv) Critical Region:** The set of all values of the test statistic for which $H_0$ is rejected. It is also called the **rejection region**.

**(v) Level of Significance ($\alpha$):** The maximum probability of committing a Type I error that the researcher is willing to tolerate. Common values: 0.05, 0.01, 0.10.

**(vi) p-value:** The smallest level of significance at which $H_0$ can be rejected. It is the probability, assuming $H_0$ is true, of obtaining a test statistic as extreme as or more extreme than the observed value.

**(vii) Power of Test:** The probability of correctly rejecting a false null hypothesis. Power $= 1 - \beta$, where $\beta$ is the probability of a Type II error.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1: State the hypotheses**
- $H_0: \mu \geq 1000$ hours (average life is at least 1000 hours — manufacturer's claim)
- $H_1: \mu < 1000$ hours (average life is less than 1000 hours) — **left-tailed test**

**Step 2: Test statistic**
Given: $n = 25$, $\bar{X} = 980$, $\mu_0 = 1000$, $s = 50$. Since $\sigma$ is unknown and $n = 25 < 30$, use the **t-test**:

$$t = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{s / \sqrt{n}}$$

**Step 3: Significance level**
$\alpha = 0.05$, degrees of freedom $= n - 1 = 24$.

**Step 4: Critical value**
From the t-table at $\alpha = 0.05$ and $df = 24$ (one-tailed):
$$t_{\text{critical}} = -t_{0.05, 24} = -1.711$$

**Step 5: Compute the test statistic**

$$t_{\text{calc}} = \frac{980 - 1000}{50 / \sqrt{25}}$$

$$t_{\text{calc}} = \frac{-20}{50 / 5}$$

$$t_{\text{calc}} = \frac{-20}{10}$$

$$t_{\text{calc}} = -2.00$$

**Step 6: Decision**
Since $t_{\text{calc}} = -2.00 < t_{\text{critical}} = -1.711$, we **reject $H_0$**.

**Conclusion:** At the 5% significance level, there is sufficient evidence to reject the manufacturer's claim. The average life of the bulbs is significantly **less than 1000 hours**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Critical Mistake 1 (–2 marks):** Many students write "accept $H_0$" instead of **"fail to reject $H_0$"** when the test statistic falls in the acceptance region. The correct statistical language is ALWAYS **"fail to reject $H_0$"** — we never prove $H_0$ to be true, we only fail to find evidence against it.
> 2. **Critical Mistake 2 (–2 marks):** Using the **two-tailed critical value ($\pm 1.96$)** for a **one-tailed test** (which requires $\pm 1.645$ at $\alpha = 0.05$). Always check the form of $H_1$ before looking up the critical value.
> 3. **Critical Mistake 3 (–2 marks):** Confusing the Z-test and t-test selection. Remember: **Z-test is for $\sigma$ known OR $n \geq 30$; t-test is for $\sigma$ unknown AND $n < 30$.**
> 4. **Critical Mistake 4 (–1 mark):** Not stating the degrees of freedom when using the t-test. KTU examiners specifically look for "df = $n - 1$" in t-test solutions.
> 5. **Critical Mistake 5 (–1 mark):** Failing to provide an engineering interpretation of the statistical decision. Always end with a **practical conclusion** in the context of the problem (e.g., "the machine needs recalibration," "the drug is not effective," etc.).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Last-Minute KTU Exam Preparation**

- **Null Hypothesis ($H_0$):** Always contains **equality** ($=$, $\leq$, $\geq$). Represents the status quo / no effect.
- **Alternative Hypothesis ($H_1$):** Contains **strict inequality** ($<$, $>$, $\neq$). Represents the research claim.
- **Type I Error ($\alpha$):** Rejecting a true $H_0$. **Producer's risk.** Fixed by the experimenter (typically 0.05).
- **Type II Error ($\beta$):** Accepting a false $H_0$. **Consumer's risk.** Depends on sample size, effect size, and $\sigma$.
- **Power of test** $= 1 - \beta$. Higher power = better test. Target: Power $\geq 0.80$.
- **Z-test** when $\sigma$ is known or $n \geq 30$. **t-test** when $\sigma$ is unknown and $n < 30$ (use $df = n - 1$).
- **One-tailed test:** Rejection region in one tail. Critical value: $Z_{\alpha}$ or $t_{\alpha, df}$.
- **Two-tailed test:** Rejection region in both tails ($\alpha/2$ each). Critical value: $Z_{\alpha/2}$ or $t_{\alpha/2, df}$.
- **Decision rule:** Reject $H_0$ if $\vert \text{Test Statistic} \vert > \text{Critical Value}$.
- **p-value rule:** Reject $H_0$ if $p\text{-value} \leq \alpha$.
- **Standard critical values (Z):** $Z_{0.05} = 1.645$, $Z_{0.025} = 1.96$, $Z_{0.01} = 2.326$.
- **Sample size formula:** $n = \dfrac{(Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta})^2 \cdot \sigma^2}{(\mu_1 - \mu_0)^2}$ for two-tailed test.
- **Trade-off:** Decreasing $\alpha$ → increasing $\beta$ (for fixed $n$, $\sigma$). Compensate by increasing $n$.
- **For paired data:** Use the paired t-test on differences $d_i = X_{1i} - X_{2i}$.
- **For two independent samples (equal variances):** Use the pooled t-test with $s_p$.
- **Always interpret the statistical decision in the engineering context of the problem.**
- **Use "fail to reject $H_0$" — NEVER "accept $H_0$" in conclusions.**
- **Draw the normal curve diagram** in every test, marking critical values and rejection regions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Experiments with Single Factor: The Analysis of Variance
Analysis of Fixed Effect Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT727 - DESIGN OF EXPERIMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Analysis of Fixed Effect Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the framework of **Design of Experiments (DOE)**, a **Fixed Effect Model** (also known as **Model I ANOVA** or the **Fixed Effects Model**) is a statistical model in which the experimenter is interested in comparing a specific, finite set of treatments (or levels) whose effects are **fixed, non-random, and repeatable constants**. The treatment levels are deliberately chosen by the investigator, and any inference drawn applies *only* to those specific levels — not to a broader population of treatments.

For a **One-Way Classification** (single factor with $a$ levels), the linear statistical model is:

$$
Y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \varepsilon_{ij}
\quad \begin{cases} i = 1, 2, \dots, a \\ j = 1, 2, \dots, n_i \end{cases}
$$

Where:
- $Y_{ij}$ = Response variable observation in the $i$-th treatment and $j$-th replicate
- $\mu$ = Overall (grand) population mean — a fixed constant
- $\tau_i$ = Effect of the $i$-th treatment — a **fixed** unknown parameter with the constraint $\sum_{i=1}^{a} \tau_i = 0$
- $\varepsilon_{ij}$ = Random error term, assumed $\varepsilon_{ij} \overset{iid}{\sim} N(0, \sigma^2)$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — KTU Module 2:**
> The Fixed Effect Model contrasts with the **Random Effect Model (Model II)**, where treatments are a random sample from a larger population, and the **Mixed Effect Model (Model III)**, where some factors are fixed and others random. Only the Fixed Effect Model is treated in Module 2.

> [!NOTE]
> **Constraint Convention:** The condition $\sum_{i=1}^{a} \tau_i = 0$ ensures that the model parameters $\mu$ and $\tau_i$ are **uniquely identifiable**. Without this constraint, there would be infinite equivalent solutions (the "over-parameterization" problem).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Three Coffee Blends

Imagine a coffee company wants to test **three specific blends** — say, Blend A (light roast), Blend B (medium roast), and Blend C (dark roast) — to see which gives the highest customer rating. The blends are **pre-selected, deliberate choices**. The company does NOT care about "all possible roasts in the universe" — only these three. Every time the experiment is repeated, Blend A is still Blend A, Blend B is still Blend B. They are **fixed**.

- **Treatments ($\tau_i$):** The fixed, measurable effect that being Blend B (for example) has on the rating compared to the grand average of all three blends.
- **Grand Mean ($\mu$):** What you would expect the average rating to be if you averaged all three blends equally.
- **Error ($\varepsilon_{ij}$):** The random fluctuations — the customer who was in a bad mood, the slight variation in brewing temperature, etc.
