**(b) [7 Marks]** State and explain the **Gauss-Markov theorem**. Under what conditions does OLS fail to be BLUE?

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a): Complete Derivation (See Section 3.1)

The candidate must:
1. Set up $S(\beta_0, \beta_1) = \sum(y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i)^2$.
2. Take partial derivatives and set to zero to obtain the **two normal equations**.
3. Solve simultaneously for $\hat{\beta}_1$ and $\hat{\beta}_0$ to obtain:

$$\hat{\beta}_1 = \frac{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2} = \frac{S_{xy}}{S_{xx}}, \qquad \hat{\beta}_0 = \bar{y} - \hat{\beta}_1 \bar{x}$$

4. (Bonus) Show the equivalent form $\hat{\beta}_1 = [n\sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i]/[n\sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2]$.

**Verification check:** Plug estimators back into the partial derivative — should give zero.

#### Part (b): Gauss-Markov Theorem

**Statement:** Under the classical linear model assumptions:
- (i) $E(\epsilon_i) = 0$
- (ii) $\text{Var}(\epsilon_i) = \sigma^2$ (constant)
- (iii) $\text{Cov}(\epsilon_i, \epsilon_j) = 0$ for $i \neq j$
- (iv) The $X$ values are non-stochastic (fixed in repeated samples)

the OLS estimators $\hat{\beta}_0$ and $\hat{\beta}_1$ are **BLUE**: **B**est (minimum variance) **L**inear (linear functions of $Y$) **U**nbiased ($E(\hat{\beta}) = \beta$) **E**stimators.

**When OLS fails to be BLUE (violations):**
1. **Heteroscedasticity:** $\text{Var}(\epsilon_i) \neq \text{Var}(\epsilon_j)$ — use **Weighted Least Squares (WLS)**.
2. **Autocorrelation:** $\text{Cov}(\epsilon_i, \epsilon_j) \neq 0$ — use **Generalized Least Squares (GLS)**.
3. **Endogeneity:** $X$ correlated with $\epsilon$ — use **Instrumental Variables (IV)**.
4. **Multicollinearity (in multiple regression):** Inflated variances, estimators still unbiased but no longer minimum-variance in practical sense.
5. **Non-linearity:** Wrong functional form — use transformations or non-linear regression.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** When asked about Gauss-Markov, many students write "OLS is the best estimator" — this is **incomplete**. You must specify "best *linear unbiased* estimator" and list at least **three classical assumptions**. Failure to qualify "linear" and "unbiased" will lose **2 to 3 marks** out of 7.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Estimation of Parameters in Linear Regression Models**

- ✅ **Model:** $Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_i + \epsilon_i$ with $\epsilon_i \overset{iid}{\sim} N(0, \sigma^2)$.
- ✅ **OLS objective:** Minimize $SSE = \sum(y_i - \hat{\beta}_0 - \hat{\beta}_1 x_i)^2$.
- ✅ **Slope estimator:** $\hat{\beta}_1 = S_{xy}/S_{xx}$.
- ✅ **Intercept estimator:** $\hat{\beta}_0 = \bar{y} - \hat{\beta}_1 \bar{x}$.
- ✅ **Variance estimator:** $\hat{\sigma}^2 = SSE/(n-2)$ — **never** $SSE/n$.
- ✅ **Slope SE:** $\sqrt{\hat{\sigma}^2 / S_{xx}}$.
- ✅ **Intercept SE:** $\sqrt{\hat{\sigma}^2(1/n + \bar{x}^2/S_{xx})}$.
- ✅ **$R^2$:** $1 - SSE/SST$ — measures proportion of variation explained.
- ✅ **Test statistic:** $t_0 = \hat{\beta}_1 / \text{SE}(\hat{\beta}_1)$ with $n-2$ degrees of freedom.
- ✅ **Gauss-Markov:** OLS = **BLUE** under classical assumptions.
- ✅ **Spreading $X$ values reduces** $\text{Var}(\hat{\beta}_1)$ — design implication.
- ✅ **ANOVA identity:** $SST = SSR + SSE$ — always verify.
- ✅ **Degrees of freedom:** Total $= n-1$, Regression $= 1$, Error $= n-2$.
- ✅ **Multiple regression extension:** $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hypothesis Testing in Multiple Regression
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBBT727 - DESIGN OF EXPERIMENTS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Hypothesis Testing in Multiple Regression ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition** > **Hypothesis Testing in Multiple Regression** refers to the systematic statistical procedure used to determine whether the estimated regression coefficients, the overall regression model, or subsets of predictors are statistically significant, by formulating and testing null hypotheses against alternative hypotheses using distributions such as the **t-distribution** and **F-distribution**, with a specified level of significance ($\alpha$). In a general **Multiple Linear Regression (MLR)** model with $k$ independent variables: $$Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_{1i} + \beta_2 X_{2i} + \dots + \beta_k X_{ki} + \varepsilon_i$$ where $\varepsilon_i \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$, the objective of hypothesis testing is to validate whether the proposed model meaningfully explains the variation in the response variable $Y$. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] **Intuitive Real-World Analogy — "The Cooking Recipe Test"** > Imagine a chef who claims that adding **Salt (X₁)**, **Pepper (X₂)**, and **Turmeric (X₃)** improves the taste score (Y) of a dish. Hypothesis testing in multiple regression is analogous to a **blind taste-test panel** evaluating each ingredient's contribution. > - **Overall F-test** = Asking: *"Does the whole recipe (the model) actually work?"* > - **Individual t-tests** = Asking: *"Does adding Salt alone significantly improve the taste, holding Pepper and Turmeric constant?"* > - **Partial F-test** = Asking: *"Do Salt and Pepper together add anything beyond what Turmeric alone provides?"* > > Just as a chef would not want to keep an unnecessary ingredient, a statistician would not want a redundant predictor. ### 1.3 Types of Hypothesis Tests in MLR | Test Type | Purpose | Statistic Used | |---|---|---| | **Overall F-test** | Significance of entire model | F-distribution | | **Individual t-test** | Significance of each $\beta_j$ | t-distribution | | **Partial F-test** | Significance of a subset of $\beta_j$'s | F-distribution | | **Confidence Intervals** | Range estimation for $\beta_j$ | t-distribution | | **Test on R²** | Goodness of fit assessment | F-distribution | > [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Board Highlight** > Under the **KTU 2024 NEP-aligned OBE framework**, students are expected to demonstrate the ability to **formulate hypotheses, compute test statistics, compare with critical values, and draw engineering conclusions** — these form the core **Course Outcome (CO) mapping** for Module 4. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Levels of Hypothesis Testing in MLR Multiple regression hypothesis testing operates at **three hierarchical levels**, each answering a progressively refined question. #### **Level 1: Overall Model Significance (Global Test)** This is the **first test** any engineer must perform before interpreting individual coefficients. **Hypotheses:** $$H_0: \beta_1 = \beta_2 = \dots = \beta_k = 0 \quad \text{(Model is useless — none of the predictors matter)}$$ $$H_1: \text{At least one } \beta_j \neq 0 \quad \text{(At least one predictor is useful)}$$ **Test Statistic (F-statistic):** $$F_0 = \frac{MS_R}{MS_E} = \frac{SS_R / k}{SS_E / (n-k-1)}$$ **Decision Rule:** - If $F_0 > F_{\alpha, k, n-k-1}$ → **Reject $H_0$** (Model is significant) - If $F_0 \leq F_{\alpha, k, n-k-1}$ → **Fail to Reject $H_0$** (Model is not significant) > [!NOTE] **Engineering Interpretation** > A non-significant overall F-test means the model has **no explanatory power** — stop and revise your model before drawing any further conclusions. --- #### **Level 2: Individual Coefficient Significance (Marginal Tests)** After confirming the model is globally significant, examine **each predictor** independently. **Hypotheses for coefficient $\beta_j$:** $$H_0: \beta_j = 0 \quad \text{(Predictor } X_j \text{ contributes nothing, given others)}$$ $$H_1: \beta_j \neq 0$$ **Test Statistic (t-statistic):** $$t_0 = \frac{\hat{\beta}_j}{SE(\hat{\beta}_j)} = \frac{\hat{\beta}_j}{\sqrt{\hat{\sigma}^2 \cdot C_{jj}}}$$ where $C_{jj}$ is the $(j+1)$-th diagonal element of the matrix $(X^TX)^{-1}$, and $\hat{\sigma}^2 = MS_E = \frac{SS_E}{n-k-1}$. **Decision Rule:** - If $\vert t_0 \vert > t_{\alpha/2, n-k-1}$ → **Reject $H_0$** (Predictor $X_j$ is significant) --- #### **Level 3: Partial F-test (Subset Significance)** Tests whether a **subset of $r$ coefficients** is simultaneously zero. This is essential when comparing nested models. **Hypotheses:** $$H_0: \beta_{k-r+1} = \beta_{k-r+2} = \dots = \beta_k = 0$$ $$H_1: \text{At least one of these } \beta_j \neq 0$$ **Test Statistic:** $$F_0 = \frac{(SS_R(\text{full}) - SS_R(\text{reduced})) / r}{SS_E(\text{full}) / (n-k-1)}$$ **Decision Rule:** - If $F_0 > F_{\alpha, r, n-k-1}$ → **Reject $H_0$** (The subset of predictors significantly improves the model) --- ### 2.2 Confidence Intervals for Regression Coefficients A $(1-\alpha) \times 100\%$ confidence interval for $\beta_j$ is: $$\hat{\beta}_j - t_{\alpha/2, n-k-1} \cdot SE(\hat{\beta}_j) \leq \beta_j \leq \hat{\beta}_j + t_{\alpha/2, n-k-1} \cdot SE(\hat{\beta}_j)$$ ### 2.3 Relationship Between F-test and t-test > [!IMPORTANT] **Key KTU Result** > For a single coefficient ($r = 1$), the **Partial F-test** is mathematically equivalent to the **squared t-test**: > $$F_0 = t_0^2$$ > This is a **high-yield fact** frequently tested in KTU university examinations. ### 2.4 Coefficient of Multiple Determination ($R^2$) and Adjusted $R^2$ $$R^2 = \frac{SS_R}{SS_T} = 1 - \frac{SS_E}{SS_T}$$ $$\bar{R}^2 = 1 - \frac{SS_E / (n-k-1)}{SS_T / (n-1)} = 1 - (1-R^2)\frac{n-1}{n-k-1}$$ > [!WARNING] **Common Pitfall** > $R^2$ **always increases** when variables are added, even if they are useless. Therefore, **adjusted $R^2$** is preferred for model comparison in multiple regression. ### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | Symbol | Formula | Description | Distribution | |---|---|---|---| | $F_0$ (Overall) | $\dfrac{MS_R}{MS_E}$ | Mean Square Regression / Error | $F_{k, n-k-1}$ | | $t_0$ (Individual) | $\dfrac{\hat{\beta}_j}{SE(\hat{\beta}_j)}$ | Coefficient / Std. Error | $t_{n-k-1}$ | | $F_0$ (Partial) | $\dfrac{(SS_{R,\text{full}} - SS_{R,\text{red}})/r}{SS_{E,\text{full}}/(n-k-1)}$ | Extra SS due to $r$ variables | $F_{r, n-k-1}$ | | $SE(\hat{\beta}_j)$ | $\sqrt{\hat{\sigma}^2 \cdot C_{jj}}$ | Standard error of $\hat{\beta}_j$ | — | | $\hat{\sigma}^2$ | $MS_E = \dfrac{SS_E}{n-k-1}$ | Estimate of $\sigma^2$ | $\chi^2_{n-k-1}$ scaled | | $CI(\beta_j)$ | $\hat{\beta}_j \pm t_{\alpha/2, n-k-1} \cdot SE(\hat{\beta}_j)$ | Confidence interval | — | | $R^2$ | $1 - \dfrac{SS_E}{SS_T}$ | Coefficient of determination | — | | $\bar{R}^2$ | $1 - (1-R^2)\dfrac{n-1}{n-k-1}$ | Adjusted R² | — | ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > - **Civil Engineering:** Testing whether soil type, moisture, and load all jointly affect concrete compressive strength. > - **Mechanical Engineering:** Evaluating whether temperature, pressure, and RPM simultaneously influence engine efficiency. > - **Industrial/Quality Engineering:** Determining which process variables (out of many candidates) significantly affect yield. > - **Biomedical Engineering:** Assessing the combined effect of drug dose, age, and weight on patient recovery. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Overall F-test Statistic Starting from the **ANOVA decomposition** of total sum of squares: $$SS_T = SS_R + SS_E$$ with degrees of freedom: $$n-1 = k + (n-k-1)$$ Under $H_0: \beta_1 = \beta_2 = \dots = \beta_k = 0$, we have the expected mean squares: $$E[MS_E] = \sigma^2$$ $$E[MS_R] = \sigma^2 + \sum_{j=1}^{k} \beta_j^2 \cdot \frac{S_{jj}}{k}$$ where $S_{jj}$ relates to the variability of $X_j$. Therefore, the ratio: $$F_0 = \frac{MS_R}{MS_E}$$ is approximately **1** under $H_0$ and **greater than 1** when $H_0$ is false. **Formal derivation:** $$\begin{aligned} F_0 &= \frac{SS_R / k}{SS_E / (n-k-1)} \\[6pt] &= \frac{\hat{\beta}^T X^T Y - n\bar{Y}^2 / k}{SS_E / (n-k-1)} \\[6pt] &= \frac{(SS_T - SS_E) / k}{SS_E / (n-k-1)} \\[6pt] &= \frac{MS_R}{MS_E} \sim F_{k, n-k-1} \text{ under } H_0 \end{aligned}$$ **Decision Rule:** - If $F_0 > F_{\alpha, k, n-k-1}$, reject $H_0$. - Otherwise, fail to reject $H_0$. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of the t-test Statistic for $\beta_j$ Since $\hat{\beta}_j$ is an unbiased estimator of $\beta_j$: $$E[\hat{\beta}_j] = \beta_j$$ The variance of $\hat{\beta}_j$ in the general MLR setting is: $$\text{Var}(\hat{\beta}_j) = \sigma^2 (X^TX)^{-1}_{jj} = \sigma^2 C_{jj}$$ Using $\hat{\sigma}^2 = MS_E$ as an unbiased estimator of $\sigma^2$: $$SE(\hat{\beta}_j) = \sqrt{\hat{\sigma}^2 C_{jj}}$$ Under $H_0: \beta_j = 0$: $$\begin{aligned} t_0 &= \frac{\hat{\beta}_j - 0}{\sqrt{\hat{\sigma}^2 C_{jj}}} \\[6pt] &= \frac{\hat{\beta}_j}{SE(\hat{\beta}_j)} \sim t_{n-k-1} \end{aligned}$$ **Decision Rule:** - If $\vert t_0 \vert > t_{\alpha/2, n-k-1}$, reject $H_0$. --- ### 3.3 Derivation of the Partial F-test (Reduced vs Full Model) Let the **full model** be: $$Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \dots + \beta_k X_k + \varepsilon$$ Let the **reduced model** be (with $r$ coefficients set to zero): $$Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \dots + \beta_{k-r} X_{k-r} + \varepsilon$$ Let: - $SS_R(\text{full})$ = Regression sum of squares for the full model - $SS_R(\text{reduced})$ = Regression sum of squares for the reduced model - $SS_E(\text{full})$ = Error sum of squares for the full model - $df_R = k$ for full, $df_R = k-r$ for reduced - $df_E = n-k-1$ for both The **extra regression sum of squares** due to the $r$ added variables is: $$SS_{\text{extra}} = SS_R(\text{full}) - SS_R(\text{reduced})$$ The partial F-test statistic becomes: $$\begin{aligned} F_0 &= \frac{SS_{\text{extra}} / r}{SS_E(\text{full}) / (n-k-1)} \\[6pt] &= \frac{[SS_R(\text{full}) - SS_R(\text{reduced})] / r}{SS_E(\text{full}) / (n-k-1)} \\[6pt] &\sim F_{r, n-k-1} \text{ under } H_0 \end{aligned}$$ **Decision Rule:** - If $F_0 > F_{\alpha, r, n-k-1}$, reject $H_0$ — the extra $r$ variables significantly improve the model. --- ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical Pattern) **Problem Statement:** A study investigates the effect of **Temperature ($X_1$)** and **Pressure ($X_2$)** on **Yield ($Y$)**. Data from $n = 10$ runs yields: - $SS_T = 1450$, $SS_R = 1180$, $SS_E = 270$ - $k = 2$ predictors, $\hat{\beta}_1 = 2.45$, $\hat{\beta}_2 = -1.32$ - $SE(\hat{\beta}_1) = 0.82$, $SE(\hat{\beta}_2) = 0.61$ - $C_{jj}$ already incorporated into $SE$ values - Significance level: $\alpha = 0.05$ #### **Step 1: Overall F-test** $$\begin{aligned} df_R &= k = 2 \\ df_E &= n - k - 1 = 10 - 2 - 1 = 7 \\ MS_R &= \frac{SS_R}{df_R} = \frac{1180}{2} = 590 \\ MS_E &= \frac{SS_E}{df_E} = \frac{270}{7} \approx 38.571 \\ F_0 &= \frac{MS_R}{MS_E} = \frac{590}{38.571} \approx 15.297 \end{aligned}$$ Critical value: $F_{0.05, 2, 7} = 4.74$ Since $F_0 = 15.297 > 4.74$ → **Reject $H_0$** at $\alpha = 0.05$. **Conclusion:** The overall regression model is **highly significant** — at least one of the predictors contributes meaningfully to yield. --- #### **Step 2: Individual t-tests** Degrees of freedom: $n - k - 1 = 7$ Critical value: $t_{0.025, 7} = 2.365$ **For $\beta_1$ (Temperature):** $$\begin{aligned} t_0 &= \frac{\hat{\beta}_1}{SE(\hat{\beta}_1)} = \frac{2.45}{0.82} \approx 2.988 \end{aligned}$$ Since $\vert t_0 \vert = 2.988 > 2.365$ → **Reject $H_0: \beta_1 = 0$**. **For $\beta_2$ (Pressure):** $$\begin{aligned} t_0 &= \frac{\hat{\beta}_2}{SE(\hat{\beta}_2)} = \frac{-1.32}{0.61} \approx -2.164 \end{aligned}$$ Since $\vert t_0 \vert = 2.164 < 2.365$ → **Fail to reject $H_0: \beta_2 = 0$**. **Conclusion:** Temperature is significant, but Pressure is **not statistically significant** at the 5% level. --- #### **Step 3: Coefficient of Determination** $$R^2 = \frac{SS_R}{SS_T} = \frac{1180}{1450} \approx 0.8138 \quad (81.38\%)$$ $$\bar{R}^2 = 1 - (1 - 0.8138)\frac{10-1}{10-2-1} = 1 - 0.1862 \times \frac{9}{7} \approx 1 - 0.2394 \approx 0.7606$$ --- #### **Step 4: 95% Confidence Interval for $\beta_1$** $$\begin{aligned} CI &= \hat{\beta}_1 \pm t_{0.025, 7} \cdot SE(\hat{\beta}_1) \\ &= 2.45 \pm 2.365 \times 0.82 \\ &= 2.45 \pm 1.939 \\ &= (0.511, \; 4.389) \end{aligned}$$ Since the interval **does not contain zero**, we confirm $\beta_1 \neq 0$ at 95% confidence — consistent with the t-test result. --- ### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix | Result of F-test | Result of t-tests | Engineering Action | |---|---|---| | Reject $H_0$ | Most $\beta_j$ significant | Model is good; retain significant predictors | | Reject $H_0$ | Some $\beta_j$ not significant | Use **stepwise regression** to remove weak predictors | | Fail to reject $H_0$ | Most $\beta_j$ not significant | **Model is useless** — revisit variable selection | | Fail to reject $H_0$ | One $\beta_j$ significant (rare) | Investigate multicollinearity / data issues | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Hypothesis Testing Hierarchy in Multiple Regression ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Start: Fit Multiple Regression Model"] --> B["Step 1: Overall F-Test"] B --> B1{"H0: All βj = 0?"} B1 -->|Reject H0| C["Step 2: Individual t-Tests"] B1 -->|Fail to Reject| Z["STOP: Model is not significant. Revise."] C --> C1{"β1 significant?"} C1 -->|Yes| D1["Retain X1"] C1 -->|No| D2["Drop X1"] D2 --> E["Step 3: Partial F-Test"] D1 --> E E --> E1{"Subset of predictors useful?"} E1 -->|Reject H0| F["Keep additional predictors"] E1 -->|Fail to Reject| G["Drop non-significant subset"] F --> H["Step 4: Confidence Intervals"] G --> H H --> I["Step 5: R-squared and Adjusted R-squared Assessment"] I --> J["Final Validated Model"] ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram: Statistical Decision Flow ```mermaid graph LR A["Sample Data: n observations, k predictors"] --> B["Compute SS_T, SS_R, SS_E"] B --> C["Compute F0 = MS_R / MS_E"] C --> D{"Compare F0 with F(α, k, n-k-1)"} D -->|F0 > F_critical| E["Model is significant"] D -->|F0 ≤ F_critical| F["Model not significant"] E --> G["Compute t0 = βj_hat / SE(βj_hat) for each j"] G --> H{"Compare |t0| with t(α/2, n-k-1)"} H -->|t0 > t_critical| I["Predictor Xj is significant"] H -->|t0 ≤ t_critical| J["Predictor Xj is not significant"] I --> K["Construct Confidence Interval for βj"] J --> K K --> L["Final Model with Significant Predictors Only"] ``` ### 4.3 Conceptual Schematic: Sources of Variation Decomposition ```mermaid graph TD SST["SS_T: Total Sum of Squares
df = n-1"] --> SSR["SS_R: Regression Sum of Squares
Explained by Model
df = k"] SST --> SSE["SS_E: Error Sum of Squares
Unexplained Variation
df = n-k-1"] SSR --> MSR["MS_R = SS_R / k"] SSE --> MSE["MS_E = SS_E / (n-k-1)"] MSR --> FRatio["F0 = MS_R / MS_E"] MSE --> FRatio FRatio --> Decision{"Compare with F_critical"} Decision -->|Reject H0| Sig["Model Significant"] Decision -->|Fail to Reject| NotSig["Model Not Significant"] ``` ### 4.4 Visual Mapping of Three Tests ```mermaid graph TD Test1["Test 1: Overall F-Test
Tests ALL βj simultaneously
Uses F-distribution"] Test2["Test 2: Individual t-Test
Tests SINGLE βj
Uses t-distribution"] Test3["Test 3: Partial F-Test
Tests SUBSET of r coefficients
Uses F-distribution"] Test1 --> Link1["Relation: For r=1, F = t²"] Test2 --> Link1 Test3 --> Link1 Test1 --> Outcome1["Is the model worth using?"] Test2 --> Outcome2["Which predictors matter?"] Test3 --> Outcome3["Is this group of predictors useful?"] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### **Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)** > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper Style]** **Q1. State the null and alternative hypotheses for testing the overall significance of a multiple regression model with $k$ predictors.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The null and alternative hypotheses for the overall F-test are: $$H_0: \beta_1 = \beta_2 = \beta_3 = \dots = \beta_k = 0$$ $$H_1: \text{At least one } \beta_j \neq 0 \quad (j = 1, 2, \dots, k)$$ $H_0$ implies that **none of the predictor variables** has a linear relationship with the response variable $Y$. The test statistic follows an **F-distribution** with $k$ and $n-k-1$ degrees of freedom. --- **Q2. What is the relationship between the t-test and the F-test when testing a single coefficient in multiple regression?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** For a single coefficient $\beta_j$, the **square of the t-test statistic equals the partial F-test statistic**: $$t_0^2 = F_0 \quad \text{when } r = 1$$ The t-test follows $t_{n-k-1}$ and the F-test follows $F_{1, n-k-1}$, and the squared t-statistic has the same distribution. Both tests yield the **same conclusion** regarding $H_0: \beta_j = 0$. --- ### **Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)** > **Note:** KTU 2024 Scheme mandates an internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]** **(a)** Define the following terms with reference to hypothesis testing in multiple regression: **(7 Marks)** - (i) Overall F-test - (ii) Partial F-test - (iii) Coefficient of multiple determination $R^2$ **(b)** A multiple regression model with $k = 3$ predictors is fit using $n = 20$ observations. The following ANOVA results are obtained: - $SS_R = 850$, $SS_E = 150$, $SS_T = 1000$ - $\hat{\beta}_1 = 4.2, \; SE(\hat{\beta}_1) = 1.5$ - $\hat{\beta}_2 = 2.1, \; SE(\hat{\beta}_2) = 0.9$ - $\hat{\beta}_3 = 0.5, \; SE(\hat{\beta}_3) = 0.4$ Test the overall significance of the model and the significance of each regression coefficient at $\alpha = 0.05$. Comment on the usefulness of the model. **(7 Marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Question A** #### **Part (a) — Definitions (7 Marks)** **(i) Overall F-test (2 Marks):** The Overall F-test is used to test the joint significance of all regression coefficients simultaneously. The hypotheses are: $$H_0: \beta_1 = \beta_2 = \dots = \beta_k = 0$$ $$H_1: \text{At least one } \beta_j \neq 0$$ The test statistic is: $$F_0 = \frac{MS_R}{MS_E} = \frac{SS_R / k}{SS_E / (n-k-1)}$$ It follows the F-distribution with $(k, n-k-1)$ degrees of freedom under $H_0$. **(ii) Partial F-test (2.5 Marks):** The Partial F-test evaluates whether a **subset of $r$ regression coefficients** is simultaneously equal to zero, by comparing the **full model** (with all $k$ predictors) against the **reduced model** (with $k-r$ predictors). $$F_0 = \frac{(SS_R^{\text{full}} - SS_R^{\text{red}}) / r}{SS_E^{\text{full}} / (n-k-1)} \sim F_{r, n-k-1}$$ **(iii) Coefficient of Multiple Determination $R^2$ (2.5 Marks):** $R^2$ measures the **proportion of total variation** in the response variable that is explained by the regression model: $$R^2 = \frac{SS_R}{SS_T} = 1 - \frac{SS_E}{SS_T}$$ It ranges from **0 to 1**, with higher values indicating better fit. However, it always increases when predictors are added, so the **adjusted R²** is preferred for model comparison. --- #### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)** **Given:** $n = 20$, $k = 3$, $\alpha = 0.05$, $SS_R = 850$, $SS_E = 150$ **Step 1: Overall F-test (3 Marks)** $$\begin{aligned} df_R &= k = 3 \\ df_E &= n - k - 1 = 20 - 3 - 1 = 16 \\ MS_R &= \frac{SS_R}{df_R} = \frac{850}{3} \approx 283.333 \\ MS_E &= \frac{SS_E}{df_E} = \frac{150}{16} = 9.375 \\ F_0 &= \frac{MS_R}{MS_E} = \frac{283.333}{9.375} \approx 30.222 \end{aligned}$$ Critical value: $F_{0.05, 3, 16} = 3.24$ Since $F_0 = 30.222 > 3.24$ → **Reject $H_0$** at $\alpha = 0.05$. **Conclusion:** The regression model is **highly significant** overall. **Step 2: Individual t-tests (3 Marks)** Degrees of freedom: $n - k - 1 = 16$ Critical value: $t_{0.025, 16} = 2.120$ For $\beta_1$: $$t_0 = \frac{4.2}{1.5} = 2.80 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \vert 2.80 \vert > 2.120 \Rightarrow \textbf{Significant}$$ For $\beta_2$: $$t_0 = \frac{2.1}{0.9} \approx 2.33 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \vert 2.33 \vert > 2.120 \Rightarrow \textbf{Significant}$$ For $\beta_3$: $$t_0 = \frac{0.5}{0.4} = 1.25 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \vert 1.25 \vert < 2.120 \Rightarrow \textbf{Not significant}$$ **Step 3: Coefficient of Determination (1 Mark)** $$R^2 = \frac{850}{1000} = 0.85 \quad (85\%)$$ **Final Comment:** The model explains **85% of the variability** in the response. However, $X_3$ is not a significant predictor and may be **dropped** from the model. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]** **(a)** Explain the difference between the **overall F-test** and the **partial F-test** in multiple regression. When is each test most appropriately used? **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A study examines the effect of **Humidity ($X_1$)**, **Temperature ($X_2$)**, and **Wind Speed ($X_3$)** on **Power Output ($Y$)** of a wind turbine. The following data are obtained: | Source | df | SS | |---|---|---| | Regression | 3 | 1240 | | Error | 16 | 360 | | Total | 19 | 1600 | Test the overall significance of the model at $\alpha = 0.01$. If the model is significant, compute $R^2$ and $\bar{R}^2$ and interpret. **(7 Marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Question B** #### **Part (a) — Conceptual Explanation (7 Marks)** **Overall F-test (3.5 Marks):** - Tests whether **all** $k$ regression coefficients are simultaneously zero. - Hypotheses: $H_0: \beta_1 = \beta_2 = \dots = \beta_k = 0$ - Used as a **preliminary test** before interpreting individual coefficients. - A single F-statistic ($F_0 = MS_R / MS_E$) determines the global usefulness. **Partial F-test (3.5 Marks):** - Tests whether a **specific subset of $r$ coefficients** (where $r < k$) are simultaneously zero. - Hypotheses: $H_0: \beta_{k-r+1} = \dots = \beta_k = 0$ - Compares a **full model** vs a **reduced model**. - Most appropriate when deciding whether to add or remove a **group** of variables from an existing model. **Key Difference:** The Overall F-test examines the **entire model** in one go, while the Partial F-test examines a **specific group** of variables by comparing nested models. --- #### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)** **Given:** $df_R = 3$, $df_E = 16$, $SS_R = 1240$, $SS_E = 360$, $SS_T = 1600$, $\alpha = 0.01$ **Step 1: Overall F-test (3 Marks)** $$\begin{aligned} MS_R &= \frac{1240}{3} \approx 413.333 \\ MS_E &= \frac{360}{16} = 22.5 \\ F_0 &= \frac{413.333}{22.5} \approx 18.370 \end{aligned}$$ Critical value: $F_{0.01, 3, 16} = 5.29$ Since $F_0 = 18.370 > 5.29$ → **Reject $H_0$** at $\alpha = 0.01$. **Conclusion:** The regression model is **highly significant** at the 1% level. **Step 2: Compute $R^2$ and $\bar{R}^2$ (4 Marks)** $$R^2 = \frac{SS_R}{SS_T} = \frac{1240}{1600} = 0.775 \quad (77.5\%)$$ $$\begin{aligned} \bar{R}^2 &= 1 - (1 - R^2) \cdot \frac{n-1}{n-k-1} \\ &= 1 - (1 - 0.775) \cdot \frac{20-1}{20-3-1} \\ &= 1 - 0.225 \times \frac{19}{16} \\ &= 1 - 0.2672 \\ &\approx 0.7328 \quad (73.28\%) \end{aligned}$$ **Interpretation:** - $R^2 = 0.775$ indicates that **77.5%** of the variation in power output is explained by Humidity, Temperature, and Wind Speed. - The **adjusted $R^2 = 0.7328$** is slightly lower, accounting for the number of predictors and degrees of freedom. - The relatively small gap between $R^2$ and $\bar{R}^2$ suggests that **all three predictors** are contributing meaningfully to the model. - Combined with the significant overall F-test, the model demonstrates **strong predictive power** for wind turbine power output. --- ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks** > 1. **Degrees of Freedom Error:** Students frequently write $df_E = n - k$ instead of $n - k - 1$. The intercept $\beta_0$ consumes **one additional degree of freedom**. KTU examiners explicitly deduct **1–2 marks** for this. > 2. **Using Wrong Critical Value Table:** For overall F-test, use $F_{\alpha, k, n-k-1}$. For partial F-test, use $F_{\alpha, r, n-k-1}$. Many students confuse these two — leading to wrong conclusions. > 3. **Misinterpreting $R^2$:** A high $R^2$ does **not** guarantee a good model. Always cross-check with adjusted $R^2$ and residual analysis. > 4. **Confusing Two-sided vs One-sided Tests:** All t-tests in regression are **two-sided** by default ($H_1: \beta_j \neq 0$), so use $\alpha/2$ in the critical value lookup. > 5. **Forgetting to State the Hypotheses:** KTU valuation key requires **explicit statement of $H_0$ and $H_1$** before computing the test statistic. Omitting this loses 1 mark per sub-question. --- ### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid-Revision Checklist** - ✅ **Overall F-test** answers: *"Is the model useful at all?"* - ✅ **Individual t-test** answers: *"Is this specific predictor useful?"* - ✅ **Partial F-test** answers: *"Is this group of predictors useful together?"* - ✅ For a single coefficient: **$F_0 = t_0^2$** (high-yield KTU fact) - ✅ Degrees of freedom for error: **$n - k - 1$** (NOT $n - k$) - ✅ Decision rule for F-test: **Reject $H_0$ if $F_0 > F_{\alpha, df_1, df_2}$** - ✅ Decision rule for t-test: **Reject $H_0$ if $\vert t_0 \vert > t_{\alpha/2, n-k-1}$** - ✅ Confidence interval formula: $\hat{\beta}_j \pm t_{\alpha/2, n-k-1} \cdot SE(\hat{\beta}_j)$ - ✅ $R^2$ always increases with more variables — use **adjusted $R^2$** for fair comparison - ✅ A non-significant overall F-test = **STOP** — do not interpret individual t-tests - ✅ All t-tests in regression are **two-sided** by default - ✅ $MS_R = SS_R / k$ and $MS_E = SS_E / (n - k - 1)$ - ✅ Standard error of $\hat{\beta}_j$: $SE(\hat{\beta}_j) = \sqrt{\hat{\sigma}^2 \cdot C_{jj}}$ - ✅ **Order of testing:** Always perform Overall F-test **first**, then individual t-tests - ✅ **Partial F-test** is the bridge between **Overall F-test** (global) and **individual t-tests** (marginal) - ✅ KTU 2024 emphasis: Linking statistical conclusions to **engineering decisions** is mandatory for full marks

topics.title: Confidence Intervals in Multiple Regression
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT727 - DESIGN OF EXPERIMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Confidence Intervals in Multiple Regression

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In a **multiple linear regression model** with $k$ independent (predictor) variables, the population regression function is written as:

$$Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_{i1} + \beta_2 X_{i2} + \cdots + \beta_k X_{ik} + \varepsilon_i, \quad i=1,2,\dots,n$$

where $\varepsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2)$ are i.i.d. normal errors. A **confidence interval (CI)** in this setting is a random interval, constructed from sample data using the sampling distribution of an estimator, that is designed to contain the *true unknown parameter* (a regression coefficient $\beta_j$, a mean response $E(Y \vert \mathbf{x}_0)$, or a future observation $Y_0$) with a pre-specified probability called the **confidence level** $(1-\alpha)$, typically **95%** in KTU board problems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Three distinct interval constructs must be mastered:
> 1. **CI for a regression coefficient** $\beta_j$ — measures parameter uncertainty.
> 2. **CI for the mean response** $E(Y_h)$ at a given point $\mathbf{x}_h$ — measures mean-curve uncertainty.
> 3. **Prediction Interval (PI) for a new observation** $Y_h$ — measures total variability of a *single future* value.
> Each uses the same $t$-distribution with $n - (k+1) = n - p$ degrees of freedom, but differs in its standard error.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Dart Board Metaphor"**
> Imagine you are throwing darts at a target whose **true centre is unknown**. Each dart throw is like collecting one sample of regression data.
> - **CI for a coefficient $\beta_j$** = "Where do I believe the *true centre of the target* lies?" — narrow band around the average centre.
> - **CI for the mean response** = "Where do I believe the *average* of all future throws at a chosen spot will land?" — slightly wider, because even with the centre fixed, throws scatter.
> - **Prediction Interval** = "Where will *this specific next throw* land?" — the widest of all, because individual throws wander even more than the average.
> This widening order (Coefficient CI ⊂ Mean CI ⊂ Prediction PI) is a hallmark KTU result.

### 1.3 Standard Notation Used Throughout

| Symbol | Meaning | KTU Convention |
|---|---|---|
| $n$ | Number of observations | $\ge 30$ typically |
| $k$ | Number of predictor variables | Given in statement |
| $p$ | Number of parameters ($=k+1$) | Includes intercept |
| $\mathbf{X}$ | $n \times p$ design matrix | First column = 1 |
| $\boldsymbol{\beta}$ | $p \times 1$ parameter vector | Unknown |
| $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$ | Least squares estimator | $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}=(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{Y}$ |
| $MSE$ | Mean Squared Error | $SSE/(n-p)$ — **unbiased** estimator of $\sigma^2$ |
| $t_{\alpha/2,\,n-p}$ | Critical $t$-value | Two-tailed, **bold weight** in tables |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — How a Confidence Interval is Built

A confidence interval in regression always has the structural skeleton:

$$\text{Estimator} \;\pm\; \text{Critical Value} \;\times\; \text{Standard Error of Estimator}$$

The three components are:

1. **Point Estimator** — derived from Ordinary Least Squares (OLS).
2. **Critical Value** — drawn from $t_{n-p}$ (single interval) or from $F_{p,\,n-p}$ (Working–Hotelling simultaneous family).
3. **Standard Error** — computed using $MSE$ and the $(j,j)$ diagonal element $C_{jj}$ of $(\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X})^{-1}$, denoted $c_{jj}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Why $n-p$ degrees of freedom?**
> We estimate $p = k+1$ parameters ($\beta_0, \beta_1, \dots, \beta_k$). Each estimated parameter "consumes" one degree of freedom. Starting with $n$ observations, the residual degrees of freedom is $n-p$. This is the divisor used to make $MSE$ an **unbiased** estimator of $\sigma^2$.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below contains **every formula** you will be asked to reproduce in a KTU ESE / Module quiz on this topic. Pipe characters (`|`) are replaced by `\mid` to preserve table rendering.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Conditions / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | OLS estimator | $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{Y}$ | Requires $\mathbf{X}$ full column rank |
| 2 | Fitted values | $\hat{\mathbf{Y}} = \mathbf{X}\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{Y}$ | Hat matrix $\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top$ |
| 3 | Residuals | $\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{Y}-\hat{\mathbf{Y}} = (\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{H})\mathbf{Y}$ | $\mathbf{e}^\top\mathbf{X}=\mathbf{0}$ |
| 4 | $SSE$ | $SSE = \mathbf{Y}^\top(\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{H})\mathbf{Y}$ | Sum of Squared Errors |
| 5 | $MSE$ | $MSE = \dfrac{SSE}{n-p}$ | Unbiased estimator of $\sigma^2$; $\mathbb{E}[MSE]=\sigma^2$ |
| 6 | Var-Cov of $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$ | $\operatorname{Cov}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}) = \sigma^2(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}$ | Diagonal gives $\operatorname{Var}(\hat{\beta}_j)$ |
| 7 | Standard Error of $\hat{\beta}_j$ | $SE(\hat{\beta}_j) = \sqrt{MSE \cdot c_{jj}}$ | $c_{jj}$ = jth diagonal of $(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}$ |
| 8 | CI for $\beta_j$ | $\hat{\beta}_j \pm t_{\alpha/2,\,n-p}\,SE(\hat{\beta}_j)$ | Tests significance of single predictor |
| 9 | Mean response at $\mathbf{x}_0$ | $\hat{Y}_0 = \mathbf{x}_0^\top\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$ | Point on regression surface |
| 10 | $SE(\hat{Y}_0)$ | $\sqrt{MSE \cdot \mathbf{x}_0^\top(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_0}$ | Var of fitted value |
| 11 | CI for $E(Y \vert \mathbf{x}_0)$ | $\hat{Y}_0 \pm t_{\alpha/2,\,n-p}\,\sqrt{MSE \cdot \mathbf{x}_0^\top(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_0}$ | **Mean-curve CI** |
| 12 | $SE$ for prediction of $Y_0$ | $\sqrt{MSE \cdot \bigl(1+\mathbf{x}_0^\top(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_0\bigr)}$ | Adds unit variance of $\varepsilon$ |
| 13 | PI for new $Y_0$ | $\hat{Y}_0 \pm t_{\alpha/2,\,n-p}\,\sqrt{MSE \cdot \bigl(1+\mathbf{x}_0^\top(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_0\bigr)}$ | **Always wider than Mean CI** |
| 14 | Working–Hotelling | $\hat{Y}_0 \pm \sqrt{p \cdot F_{\alpha,\,p,\,n-p}}\,\sqrt{MSE \cdot \mathbf{x}_0^\top(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_0}$ | Simultaneous band for *all* $\mathbf{x}$ |
| 15 | Bonferroni joint CI on $r$ coefs | $\hat{\beta}_j \pm t_{\alpha/(2r),\,n-p}\,SE(\hat{\beta}_j)$ | Approximate but simple |

### 2.3 Why This System Matters in Engineering

In real engineering experimentation (KTU lab-based DOOE courses), confidence intervals translate abstract sums-of-squares into **practical decision tools**:

- **CI for $\beta_j$** tells the engineer whether a factor (e.g., temperature, pressure, catalyst concentration) has a statistically meaningful linear effect on yield.
- **Mean-response CI** quantifies uncertainty in the predicted *average* performance at a chosen operating condition — vital for process control.
- **Prediction PI** is the most used in practice: "If I set my reactor to $180^\circ\text{C}$ and $2.5\,\text{atm}$, between what limits will a *single batch yield* fall?"

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A PI that crosses zero or crosses a process specification limit (e.g., purity $\ge 99\%$) immediately signals a robustness problem in Six-Sigma / DOE industrial practice.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Distribution of $\hat{\beta}_j$ and the Single-Coefficient CI

**Starting assumption.** The classical linear model:
$\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}, \quad \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\sim\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0},\sigma^2\mathbf{I}_n)$.

**Step 1.** The OLS estimator is

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{Y}.$$

**Step 2.** Because $\mathbf{Y}$ is multivariate normal, any linear transformation of $\mathbf{Y}$ is multivariate normal. Therefore $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$ is also multivariate normal.

**Step 3.** Compute its mean:

$$\mathbb{E}[\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}] = (\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{Y}] = (\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top(\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}) = (\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})\boldsymbol{\beta} = \boldsymbol{\beta}.$$

So $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$ is an **unbiased** estimator of $\boldsymbol{\beta}$.

**Step 4.** Compute its variance-covariance matrix:

$$\operatorname{Cov}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}) = (\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top \operatorname{Cov}(\mathbf{Y}) \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}.$$

Since $\operatorname{Cov}(\mathbf{Y}) = \sigma^2 \mathbf{I}_n$:

$$\operatorname{Cov}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}) = (\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top (\sigma^2 \mathbf{I}_n)\mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1} = \sigma^2(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}.$$

The middle two matrices cancel:

$$\operatorname{Cov}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}) = \sigma^2(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}.$$

Hence the variance of the $j$th coefficient is the $j$th diagonal entry of $\sigma^2(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}$:

$$\operatorname{Var}(\hat{\beta}_j) = \sigma^2 c_{jj}, \quad \text{where } c_{jj}=\bigl[(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\bigr]_{jj}.$$

**Step 5.** Replace $\sigma^2$ with its unbiased estimator $MSE = \dfrac{SSE}{n-p}$. The estimated standard error is

$$SE(\hat{\beta}_j)=\sqrt{MSE \cdot c_{jj}}.$$

**Step 6.** Form the pivotal quantity:

$$T=\dfrac{\hat{\beta}_j-\beta_j}{SE(\hat{\beta}_j)}=\dfrac{\hat{\beta}_j-\beta_j}{\sqrt{MSE\,c_{jj}}}.$$

It can be proved (using Cochran's theorem) that the numerator is normal $\mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2 c_{jj})$ and **independent** of $MSE$, with $\dfrac{(n-p)MSE}{\sigma^2}\sim\chi^2_{n-p}$. Hence

$$T\sim t_{\,n-p}.$$

**Step 7.** Impose the coverage requirement $\Pr(-t_{\alpha/2,n-p}\le T\le t_{\alpha/2,n-p})=1-\alpha$ and rearrange for $\beta_j$:

$$\Pr\!\left(\hat{\beta}_j - t_{\alpha/2,n-p}\,SE(\hat{\beta}_j)\;\le\;\beta_j\;\le\;\hat{\beta}_j + t_{\alpha/2,n-p}\,SE(\hat{\beta}_j)\right)=1-\alpha.$$

Thus the $(1-\alpha)\times 100\%$ **CI for $\beta_j$** is

$$\boxed{\;\hat{\beta}_j \;\pm\; t_{\alpha/2,\,n-p}\,SE(\hat{\beta}_j)\;}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mean Response CI at an Arbitrary Point $\mathbf{x}_0$

Let $\mathbf{x}_0 = (1, x_{01}, x_{02}, \dots, x_{0k})^\top$ be a $(p\times 1)$ vector of predictor values at which we wish to estimate the mean response.

**Step 1.** The estimator of $E(Y\vert \mathbf{x}_0) = \mathbf{x}_0^\top\boldsymbol{\beta}$ is the fitted value

$$\hat{Y}_0 = \mathbf{x}_0^\top\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}.$$

**Step 2.** Mean and variance of $\hat{Y}_0$:

$$\mathbb{E}[\hat{Y}_0] = \mathbf{x}_0^\top\boldsymbol{\beta} = E(Y\vert \mathbf{x}_0).$$

$$\operatorname{Var}(\hat{Y}_0) = \mathbf{x}_0^\top \operatorname{Cov}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}})\mathbf{x}_0 = \mathbf{x}_0^\top \sigma^2(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_0 = \sigma^2\,\mathbf{x}_0^\top(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_0.$$

**Step 3.** Estimated standard error (pivot-friendly):

$$SE(\hat{Y}_0)=\sqrt{MSE \cdot \mathbf{x}_0^\top(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_0}.$$

**Step 4.** The pivotal $t$ is

$$T=\dfrac{\hat{Y}_0-E(Y\vert \mathbf{x}_0)}{\sqrt{MSE \cdot \mathbf{x}_0^\top(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_0}} \sim t_{n-p}.$$

**Step 5.** Rearranging gives the $(1-\alpha)\times 100\%$ **CI for the mean response**:

$$\boxed{\;\hat{Y}_0 \;\pm\; t_{\alpha/2,\,n-p}\,\sqrt{MSE \cdot \mathbf{x}_0^\top(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_0}\;}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Prediction Interval for a New Observation $Y_0$

A *new* observation at $\mathbf{x}_0$ is

$$Y_0 = \mathbf{x}_0^\top\boldsymbol{\beta} + \varepsilon_0, \quad \varepsilon_0\sim\mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2),\; \varepsilon_0\perp \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}.$$

**Step 1.** Define the prediction error:

$$D = Y_0 - \hat{Y}_0 = (\mathbf{x}_0^\top\boldsymbol{\beta}+\varepsilon_0) - \mathbf{x}_0^\top\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{x}_0^\top\boldsymbol{\beta} - \mathbf{x}_0^\top\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}) + \varepsilon_0.$$

**Step 2.** Expected value:

$$\mathbb{E}[D] = \mathbf{x}_0^\top(\boldsymbol{\beta}-\boldsymbol{\beta}) + 0 = 0.$$

**Step 3.** Variance (using independence of $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$ and $\varepsilon_0$):

$$\operatorname{Var}(D) = \operatorname{Var}(\mathbf{x}_0^\top\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}) + \operatorname{Var}(\varepsilon_0) = \sigma^2 \mathbf{x}_0^\top(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_0 + \sigma^2.$$

Factor out $\sigma^2$:

$$\operatorname{Var}(D) = \sigma^2\Bigl(1+\mathbf{x}_0^\top(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_0\Bigr).$$

**Step 4.** Estimated standard error of the prediction:

$$SE_{\text{pred}}(Y_0)=\sqrt{MSE\,\bigl(1+\mathbf{x}_0^\top(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_0\bigr)}.$$

**Step 5.** Form the $t$-pivot:

$$T=\dfrac{Y_0-\hat{Y}_0}{\sqrt{MSE\bigl(1+\mathbf{x}_0^\top(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_0\bigr)}} \sim t_{n-p}.$$

**Step 6.** Solve for $Y_0$ to get the $(1-\alpha)\times 100\%$ **Prediction Interval**:

$$\boxed{\;\hat{Y}_0 \;\pm\; t_{\alpha/2,\,n-p}\,\sqrt{MSE \cdot \bigl(1+\mathbf{x}_0^\top(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_0\bigr)}\;}$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Working–Hotelling Simultaneous Confidence Band

For $m$ arbitrary predictor points $\mathbf{x}_0^{(1)}, \mathbf{x}_0^{(2)},\dots,\mathbf{x}_0^{(m)}$, we want a *simultaneous* (family-wise) band:

$$\Pr\!\Bigl(\bigcap_{h=1}^{m}\bigl\{\hat{Y}_h - W \le E(Y_h)\le \hat{Y}_h + W\bigr\}\Bigr)=1-\alpha.$$

The squared Mahalanobis-like distance follows an $F$ distribution:

$$F=\dfrac{(\hat{Y}_h - \mathbf{x}_0^{(h)\top}\boldsymbol{\beta})^2}{MSE \cdot \mathbf{x}_0^{(h)\top}(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_0^{(h)}} \sim F_{1,\,n-p}.$$

Generalising for $p$ parameters gives $\dfrac{p \cdot MSE \cdot \mathbf{x}_0^\top(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_0}{MSE}\sim p\cdot F_{p,n-p}$. The simultaneous half-width is

$$\boxed{\;W = \sqrt{p \cdot F_{\alpha,\,p,\,n-p}} \cdot \sqrt{MSE \cdot \mathbf{x}_0^\top(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_0}\;}$$

which collapses to the single-point CI when $m=1$ via the identity $t_{\alpha/2,n-p}^2 = F_{\alpha,1,n-p}$.

### 3.5 Derivations Summary Matrix

| Interval Type | Half-Width Multiplier | Extra Term Under the Square Root | Width Ordering |
|---|---|---|---|
| CI for $\beta_j$ | $t_{\alpha/2,n-p}$ | $c_{jj}$ | Narrowest (parameter-level) |
| CI for mean response | $t_{\alpha/2,n-p}$ | $\mathbf{x}_0^\top(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_0$ | Medium |
| Working–Hotelling | $\sqrt{p F_{\alpha,p,n-p}}$ | $\mathbf{x}_0^\top(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_0$ | Wider than mean CI |
| PI for new $Y_0$ | $t_{\alpha/2,n-p}$ | $1+\mathbf{x}_0^\top(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_0$ | **Widest** |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of Confidence-Interval Construction

The figure below traces the complete computational pipeline from raw data to the four interval outputs.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Data X and Y] --> B[Form Design Matrix X with intercept column]
    B --> C[Compute XtX = transpose X times X]
    C --> D[Compute inverse of XtX]
    D --> E[Compute XtY = transpose X times Y]
    E --> F[Compute beta_hat = inverse XtX times XtY]
    F --> G[Compute fitted values Y_hat = X times beta_hat]
    G --> H[Compute residuals e = Y minus Y_hat]
    H --> I[Compute SSE = sum of squared residuals]
    I --> J[Compute MSE = SSE divided by n minus p]
    J --> K[Branch A: CI for beta_j]
    J --> L[Branch B: CI for Mean Response]
    J --> M[Branch C: PI for new Y0]
    J --> N[Branch D: Working Hotelling band]

    K --> K1[Use c_jj = diagonal j of inverse XtX]
    K1 --> K2[SE beta_hat j = sqrt MSE times c_jj]
    K2 --> K3[Half width = t_alpha2 n-p times SE]

    L --> L1[Define x0 = 1 x01 x02 ... x0k]
    L1 --> L2[Compute x0 transpose inverse XtX x0]
    L2 --> L3[SE mean = sqrt MSE times scalar]
    L3 --> L4[Half width = t_alpha2 n-p times SE]

    M --> M1[Same scalar as Branch B]
    M1 --> M2[SE pred = sqrt MSE times 1 plus scalar]
    M2 --> M3[Half width = t_alpha2 n-p times SE]

    N --> N1[Half width = sqrt p F alpha p n-p times SE mean]
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Map — Width Hierarchy of Intervals

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Coefficient CI narrowest] --> Q[Mean Response CI medium]
    Q --> R[Working Hotelling Band wider]
    R --> S[Prediction PI widest]

    style P fill:#dff0d8,stroke:#3c763d
    style Q fill:#d9edf7,stroke:#31708f
    style R fill:#fcf8e3,stroke:#8a6d3b
    style S fill:#f2dede,stroke:#a94442
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree — Which Interval Should I Report?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start{What are you estimating?} --> P1[True coefficient beta_j]
    Start --> P2[Average response at x0]
    Start --> P3[Single new observation at x0]
    Start --> P4[All responses on a curve]

    P1 --> R1[Report CI for beta_j: t-based]
    P2 --> R2[Report CI for mean response: t-based]
    P3 --> R3[Report Prediction Interval: t-based plus 1 under root]
    P4 --> R4[Report Working Hotelling band: F-based]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Board-Exam Tip:** If the KTU problem says "interpret the regression coefficient $X_2$" → use *CI for $\beta_j$*. If it says "estimate the average yield when temperature = 180" → use *Mean CI*. If it says "predict the yield of the *next* batch" → use *PI*. The keyword indicators are **average** vs **single next** vs **coefficient**.

### 4.4 Schematic of Sampling Distribution Behind the $t$-Pivot

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph StandardNormal[Numerator Behaviour]
        A1[hat beta minus beta divided by sigma sqrt c] --> B1[Standard Normal Z]
    end
    subgraph ChiSquare[Denominator Behaviour]
        A2[(n-p) MSE divided by sigma squared] --> B2[Chi square with n-p df]
    end
    B1 --> C[T = Z divided by sqrt chi over df]
    B2 --> C
    C --> D[t distribution with n-p df]
    D --> E[Two tails outside plus minus t_alpha2 give alpha]
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** State the $(1-\alpha)\times 100\%$ confidence interval formula for an individual regression coefficient $\beta_j$ in a multiple linear regression model with $k$ predictors. Mention the degrees of freedom used.

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 
> $$\beta_j \;\in\; \hat{\beta}_j \pm t_{\alpha/2,\,n-(k+1)}\,SE(\hat{\beta}_j)$$
> 
> where $\;SE(\hat{\beta}_j)=\sqrt{MSE\cdot c_{jj}}\;$ and $\;c_{jj}\;$ is the $j$th diagonal element of $(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}$. The degrees of freedom for the $t$-statistic are $n-p = n-(k+1)$, where $p$ is the number of estimated parameters (intercept + $k$ slopes). **(3 marks — 1 mark formula, 1 mark SE expression, 1 mark df)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Distinguish between a *confidence interval for the mean response* and a *prediction interval for a new observation*. Why is the prediction interval always wider?

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **CI for mean response** estimates $E(Y \mid \mathbf{x}_0) = \mathbf{x}_0^\top \boldsymbol{\beta}$: half-width $t_{\alpha/2,n-p}\sqrt{MSE\cdot \mathbf{x}_0^\top(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_0}$. **(1 mark)**
> - **PI for new $Y_0$** estimates $\mathbf{x}_0^\top\boldsymbol{\beta}+\varepsilon_0$: half-width $t_{\alpha/2,n-p}\sqrt{MSE\cdot\bigl(1+\mathbf{x}_0^\top(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_0\bigr)}$. **(1 mark)**
> - The PI contains an **additional $\sigma^2$** under the square root due to the irreducible variability $\varepsilon_0$ of a single new observation, whereas the mean-response CI only reflects uncertainty in the *location* of the regression surface. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Degrees of freedom slip-up:** Examiners deduct 1 full mark if you write $t_{n-2}$ in a *multiple* regression problem. It must be $t_{n-k-1} = t_{n-p}$.
> 2. **Forgetting the intercept column:** If $\mathbf{x}_0$ does not start with a $1$ (for the intercept), your fitted value is wrong by $\beta_0$. Examiners specifically check this.
> 3. **Using $\sigma^2$ instead of $MSE$:** Always replace $\sigma^2$ with its unbiased estimator $MSE=SSE/(n-p)$.
> 4. **Mixing $t$ and $F$:** Working–Hotelling uses the $F$ distribution with $p$ and $n-p$ df. Using a $t$ multiplier is a 2-mark penalty.
> 5. **Sign convention:** Write the interval as "$\hat\beta_j \pm \text{half-width}$", not as two separate inequalities, unless the question asks for the lower and upper limits explicitly.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4 Internal Choice A]**

Consider a multiple regression model fitted to $n=25$ observations with $k=3$ predictors. The OLS estimates and key quantities are:

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}=\begin{pmatrix}12.50\\-0.80\\2.10\\1.45\end{pmatrix},\quad (\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}=\begin{pmatrix}0.45 & -0.05 & 0.02 & 0.01\\ -0.05 & 0.10 & -0.01 & 0.00\\ 0.02 & -0.01 & 0.08 & 0.02\\ 0.01 & 0.00 & 0.02 & 0.06\end{pmatrix}$$

Given $SSE = 180$.

**(a) Compute the 95% confidence interval for the regression coefficient $\beta_2$ (the coefficient of $X_2$). Comment on its significance.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) A new operating point is $\mathbf{x}_0 = (1, 5, 3, 4)^\top$. Construct the 95% confidence interval for the *mean response* and the 95% prediction interval for a *new observation* at this point. Compare their widths.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Question A:**

**Part (a) — CI for $\beta_2$**

**(i) Determine parameters and df.** Number of predictors $k=3$, parameters $p=k+1=4$. Degrees of freedom:

$$df = n-p = 25-4 = 21.$$

**(ii) Compute $MSE$.**

$$MSE = \frac{SSE}{n-p} = \frac{180}{21} = 8.5714.$$

**(iii) Identify $c_{22}$.** The diagonal element corresponding to $\beta_2$ (the third entry, index 2):

$$c_{22} = 0.08.$$

**(iv) Compute $SE(\hat{\beta}_2)$.**

$$SE(\hat{\beta}_2) = \sqrt{MSE \cdot c_{22}} = \sqrt{8.5714 \times 0.08} = \sqrt{0.6857} = 0.8281.$$

**(v) Critical $t$-value.** From tables, $t_{0.025,\,21} = 2.080$.

**(vi) Margin of error.**

$$ME = t_{0.025,\,21}\cdot SE(\hat{\beta}_2) = 2.080 \times 0.8281 = 1.7224.$$

**(vii) Construct CI.**

$$\hat{\beta}_2 \pm ME = 2.10 \pm 1.7224 \;\Rightarrow\; (0.378,\; 3.822).$$

**(viii) Significance comment.** Since the interval $(0.378,\; 3.822)$ **does not contain zero**, $\beta_2$ is statistically significant at the 5% level — $X_2$ has a meaningful linear effect on $Y$. **(1 mark comment)**

---

**Part (b) — Mean CI and Prediction PI at $\mathbf{x}_0=(1,5,3,4)^\top$**

**(i) Fitted value.**

$$\hat{Y}_0 = \mathbf{x}_0^\top \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (1)(12.50)+(5)(-0.80)+(3)(2.10)+(4)(1.45).$$

Compute term-by-term:

$$\begin{aligned}
\hat{Y}_0 &= 12.50 - 4.00 + 6.30 + 5.80 \\
&= 12.50 + 6.30 + 5.80 - 4.00 \\
&= 24.60 - 4.00 \\
&= 20.60.
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Compute the scalar $\mathbf{x}_0^\top(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_0$.**

Let $\mathbf{v}=(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_0$. Solve component-wise.

$$v_1 = (0.45)(1) + (-0.05)(5) + (0.02)(3) + (0.01)(4) = 0.45 - 0.25 + 0.06 + 0.04 = 0.30.$$

$$v_2 = (-0.05)(1) + (0.10)(5) + (-0.01)(3) + (0.00)(4) = -0.05 + 0.50 - 0.03 + 0.00 = 0.42.$$

$$v_3 = (0.02)(1) + (-0.01)(5) + (0.08)(3) + (0.02)(4) = 0.02 - 0.05 + 0.24 + 0.08 = 0.29.$$

$$v_4 = (0.01)(1) + (0.00)(5) + (0.02)(3) + (0.06)(4) = 0.01 + 0.00 + 0.06 + 0.24 = 0.31.$$

Then the scalar product:

$$\mathbf{x}_0^\top \mathbf{v} = (1)(0.30)+(5)(0.42)+(3)(0.29)+(4)(0.31).$$

Compute:

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{x}_0^\top \mathbf{v} &= 0.30 + 2.10 + 0.87 + 1.24 \\
&= 4.51.
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Mean-CI half-width.**

$$SE_{\text{mean}} = \sqrt{MSE \cdot \mathbf{x}_0^\top(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_0} = \sqrt{8.5714 \times 4.51} = \sqrt{38.6571} = 6.2175.$$

$$ME_{\text{mean}} = t_{0.025,21}\cdot SE_{\text{mean}} = 2.080 \times 6.2175 = 12.9324.$$

$$\text{95% Mean CI for } E(Y\vert\mathbf{x}_0):\; (20.60 - 12.93,\; 20.60 + 12.93) = (7.67,\; 33.53).$$

**(iv) Prediction-PI half-width.**

$$SE_{\text{pred}} = \sqrt{MSE\cdot\bigl(1+\mathbf{x}_0^\top(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_0\bigr)} = \sqrt{8.5714 \times 5.51} = \sqrt{47.2286} = 6.8723.$$

$$ME_{\text{pred}} = 2.080 \times 6.8723 = 14.2944.$$

$$\text{95% Prediction PI for } Y_0:\; (20.60 - 14.29,\; 20.60 + 14.29) = (6.31,\; 34.89).$$

**(v) Width comparison.**

$$\text{Mean CI width} = 2 \times 12.93 = 25.86.$$

$$\text{Prediction PI width} = 2 \times 14.29 = 28.58.$$

The prediction PI is wider by approximately $2.72$ units, confirming that **a single new observation carries more uncertainty than the average response at the same location.** **(1 mark for explicit comparison)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4 Internal Choice B]**

A chemical engineer fits the model $Y=\beta_0+\beta_1 X_1+\beta_2 X_2+\varepsilon$ to $n=20$ experiments and obtains:

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}=\begin{pmatrix}5.0\\0.6\\1.2\end{pmatrix},\quad (\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}=\begin{pmatrix}0.30 & 0.01 & -0.02\\ 0.01 & 0.04 & 0.00\\ -0.02 & 0.00 & 0.05\end{pmatrix},\quad SSE=48.$$

**(a) Compute the 99% confidence interval for $\beta_1$. Does $X_1$ have a significant effect at the 1% level? Show your working using the $t$-pivot.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) At the operating point $\mathbf{x}_0=(1,\,4,\,3)^\top$, build the 99% Working–Hotelling simultaneous confidence band for $E(Y\mid\mathbf{x}_0)$. Why is this band wider than the ordinary $t$-based mean-response CI?** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Question B:**

**Part (a) — 99% CI for $\beta_1$**

**(i) df.** $p=k+1=3$, so $df=n-p=20-3=17$.

**(ii) MSE.**

$$MSE = \frac{SSE}{17} = \frac{48}{17} = 2.8235.$$

**(iii) Identify $c_{11}$ for $\beta_1$.** From $(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}$, $c_{11}=0.04$.

**(iv) Standard error.**

$$SE(\hat{\beta}_1) = \sqrt{2.8235 \times 0.04} = \sqrt{0.11294} = 0.3361.$$

**(v) Critical value.** For 99% CI, $\alpha=0.01$ so $\alpha/2=0.005$. From $t$-tables, $t_{0.005,\,17}=2.898$.

**(vi) Margin of error.**

$$ME = 2.898 \times 0.3361 = 0.9740.$$

**(vii) Construct the CI.**

$$\hat{\beta}_1 \pm ME = 0.6 \pm 0.9740 \;\Rightarrow\; (-0.374,\; 1.574).$$

**(viii) Significance interpretation.** Since the interval $(-0.374,\;1.574)$ **contains zero**, we **fail to reject** $H_0:\beta_1=0$ at the 1% level. Thus $X_1$ is **not statistically significant** at $\alpha=0.01$. (Even at 5% it would be marginally non-significant.)

---

**Part (b) — 99% Working–Hotelling Band at $\mathbf{x}_0=(1,4,3)^\top$**

**(i) Fitted value.**

$$\hat{Y}_0 = (1)(5.0)+(4)(0.6)+(3)(1.2) = 5.0 + 2.4 + 3.6 = 11.0.$$

**(ii) Compute $\mathbf{x}_0^\top(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_0$.**

Compute $\mathbf{v}=(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_0$:

$$v_1=(0.30)(1)+(0.01)(4)+(-0.02)(3)=0.30+0.04-0.06=0.28.$$

$$v_2=(0.01)(1)+(0.04)(4)+(0.00)(3)=0.01+0.16+0.00=0.17.$$

$$v_3=(-0.02)(1)+(0.00)(4)+(0.05)(3)=-0.02+0.00+0.15=0.13.$$

Scalar product:

$$\mathbf{x}_0^\top\mathbf{v}=(1)(0.28)+(4)(0.17)+(3)(0.13)=0.28+0.68+0.39=1.35.$$

**(iii) Standard error of mean response.**

$$SE_{\text{mean}}=\sqrt{MSE \times 1.35}=\sqrt{2.8235\times 1.35}=\sqrt{3.8117}=1.9524.$$

**(iv) Critical $F$-value.** For 99% band, $\alpha=0.01$. With $p=3$ and $df_2=n-p=17$, from $F$-tables: $F_{0.01,\,3,\,17}=5.18$.

**(v) Working–Hotelling half-width.**

$$W=\sqrt{p\,F_{\alpha,p,n-p}}\cdot SE_{\text{mean}}=\sqrt{3\times 5.18}\times 1.9524.$$

$$\sqrt{3\times 5.18}=\sqrt{15.54}=3.9421.$$

$$W=3.9421\times 1.9524=7.6971.$$

**(vi) Simultaneous 99% confidence band.**

$$E(Y\mid\mathbf{x}_0)\;\in\;11.0\pm 7.6971 \;\Rightarrow\; (3.303,\; 18.697).$$

**(vii) Comparison to $t$-based mean CI.**

For comparison, $t_{0.005,\,17}=2.898$, so the *ordinary* 99% mean-CI half-width would be:

$$ME_t = 2.898 \times 1.9524 = 5.6581,$$

giving the narrower band $(5.342,\;16.658)$.

The Working–Hotelling band is wider because it provides **simultaneous coverage** over *all* $p=3$ parameters (and hence over the entire regression surface), whereas the simple $t$-based CI protects only the single point $\mathbf{x}_0$ at confidence level $1-\alpha$. The multiplier $\sqrt{p\,F_{\alpha,p,n-p}}$ is always $\ge t_{\alpha/2,n-p}$ when $p\ge 2$, hence the band is wider. **(2 marks for the comparison explanation)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Confidence Intervals in Multiple Regression**
> - **Model form:** $Y=\beta_0+\beta_1X_1+\cdots+\beta_kX_k+\varepsilon$, with $\varepsilon\sim\mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2)$. **(Always specify i.i.d.)**
> - **OLS estimator:** $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}=(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{Y}$ — unbiased, with $\operatorname{Cov}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}})=\sigma^2(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}$.
> - **MSE:** $MSE=SSE/(n-p)$ is the **unbiased** estimator of $\sigma^2$; do **not** divide by $n-1$ or $n-k$.
> - **Degrees of freedom:** Always $n-p=n-(k+1)$ in multiple regression — examiners check this.
> - **CI for $\beta_j$:** $\hat{\beta}_j\pm t_{\alpha/2,n-p}\sqrt{MSE\cdot c_{jj}}$. Interval excludes $0 \Rightarrow \beta_j$ significant.
> - **Mean-response CI:** $\hat{Y}_0\pm t_{\alpha/2,n-p}\sqrt{MSE\cdot \mathbf{x}_0^\top(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_0}$. Use the word **average**.
> - **Prediction PI:** $\hat{Y}_0\pm t_{\alpha/2,n-p}\sqrt{MSE\cdot(1+\mathbf{x}_0^\top(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_0)}$. Use the word **next/single/new**.
> - **Working–Hotelling band:** $\hat{Y}_0\pm\sqrt{p\,F_{\alpha,p,n-p}}\sqrt{MSE\cdot \mathbf{x}_0^\top(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_0}$. Use when *many* $\mathbf{x}_0$ points are considered simultaneously.
> - **Width ordering:** Coefficient CI $\le$ Mean CI $\le$ Working–Hotelling $\le$ Prediction PI. *(Mean CI and Working–Hotelling ordering depends on $p$.)*
> - **Bonferroni shortcut** for $r$ simultaneous CIs: replace $\alpha/2$ by $\alpha/(2r)$.
> - **Critical $t$-value table lookup:** Round df down to nearest tabulated value (e.g., df=21 → use $t_{20}$ row for conservative estimate if 21 not listed).
> - **Always include the intercept column** ($1$) as the first entry of $\mathbf{x}_0$ — common mistake costing 1 mark.
> - **Assumptions** (must be valid for any CI to hold): linearity, independence, homoscedasticity ($\operatorname{Var}(\varepsilon_i)=\sigma^2$), normality of errors.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Regression Model Diagnostics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT727 - DESIGN OF EXPERIMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Regression Model Diagnostics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Regression Model Diagnostics** refers to the systematic set of formal statistical procedures, graphical tools, and numerical tests used to **validate the adequacy, assumptions, and predictive reliability** of a fitted linear (or generalized linear) regression model. It examines whether the model satisfies the underlying Gauss–Markov assumptions, identifies outlier and influential observations, and quantifies the structural integrity of the estimated coefficients.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OEBBT727 – Design of Experiments)**, regression diagnostics are treated as the **post-fitting validation layer** that follows the estimation of model parameters using methods such as **Ordinary Least Squares (OLS)**, following the standard regression model:

$$
Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_{1i} + \beta_2 X_{2i} + \dots + \beta_k X_{ki} + \varepsilon_i
$$

The standard assumptions that diagnostics verify are:

1. **Linearity** of the relationship between response and predictors.
2. **Independence** of error terms (no autocorrelation).
3. **Homoscedasticity** (constant variance of errors).
4. **Normality** of error terms.
5. **No perfect multicollinearity** among predictors.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Trustworthy Bridge Inspector:**
> Imagine you are a civil engineer who has just constructed a suspension bridge. The cables, pylons, and deck are in place, but before opening it to traffic, an **inspector** walks along the structure checking each rivet, measuring cable tension, and looking for stress points. The bridge *looks* fine, but the inspector verifies whether the **assumed load distribution** truly holds.
>
> In the same way, after a regression model is **fitted** (the bridge built), diagnostics act as the **inspector**, examining residuals (rivet stress), checking leverage points (heavy-load trucks that bend cables), and verifying homoscedasticity (uniform traffic flow). If the bridge passes, we trust the model; if not, we **repair, redesign, or refine**.

### 1.3 Why Diagnostics Matter in DOE

In Design of Experiments, the regression model is the **bridge** between the experimental layout (factorial, RSM, mixture) and the empirical conclusions drawn about factor effects. A model that violates assumptions will produce **biased standard errors, invalid p-values, and misleading confidence intervals** — making diagnostics essential, not optional.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** A regression model is *never* considered complete after parameter estimation. Diagnostics are a **mandatory** step in any KTU board answer dealing with "model adequacy" or "validation."

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Diagnostic Architecture

Diagnostics operate on **four pillars**:

#### Pillar 1 — Residual Analysis
The residual is the difference between observed and fitted response:
$$
e_i = Y_i - \hat{Y}_i
$$

Because raw residuals have non-constant variance, we transform them into:

| Residual Type | Formula | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Standardized Residual** | $d_i = \dfrac{e_i}{\sqrt{MSE}}$ | Standardizes magnitude |
| **Studentized Residual (Internal)** | $r_i = \dfrac{e_i}{s \sqrt{1-h_{ii}}}$ | Adjusts for leverage |
| **Studentized Residual (External / Deleted)** | $t_i = \dfrac{e_i}{s_{(i)} \sqrt{1-h_{ii}}}$ | Removes point *i*'s own influence |
| **PRESS Residual** | $e_{(i)} = \dfrac{e_i}{1-h_{ii}}$ | Used for PRESS statistic |

Where:
- $s$ = $\sqrt{MSE}$ = residual standard error
- $s_{(i)}$ = residual standard error with point *i* deleted
- $h_{ii}$ = leverage (hat) value

#### Pillar 2 — Leverage (Hat Values)
The hat matrix $\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top$ encodes how each observation pulls the fitted regression line toward itself.

$$
h_{ii} = \text{the } i^{th} \text{ diagonal element of } \mathbf{H}
$$

A point is considered **high leverage** if:
$$
h_{ii} > \frac{2(p+1)}{n}
$$
where *p* is the number of predictors (excluding intercept) and *n* is the sample size.

#### Pillar 3 — Influence Measures
Influence combines **leverage** and **residual size** to detect points whose removal drastically shifts the model.

| Measure | Formula | Threshold of Concern |
|---|---|---|
| **Cook's Distance** | $D_i = \dfrac{e_i^2}{p \cdot MSE} \cdot \dfrac{h_{ii}}{(1-h_{ii})^2}$ | $D_i > 1$ (or $> 4/n$) |
| **DFFITS** | $DFFITS_i = t_i \sqrt{\dfrac{h_{ii}}{1-h_{ii}}}$ | $\vert DFFITS_i \vert > 2\sqrt{\dfrac{p+1}{n}}$ |
| **DFBETAS** | $DFBETAS_{j(i)} = \dfrac{\hat{\beta}_j - \hat{\beta}_{j(i)}}{s_{(i)}\sqrt{C_{jj}}}$ | $\vert DFBETAS_{j(i)} \vert > \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{n}}$ |

Where $C_{jj}$ is the *j*th diagonal element of $(\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X})^{-1}$.

#### Pillar 4 — Multicollinearity Diagnostics
When predictors are highly correlated, coefficient estimates become unstable.

| Diagnostic | Formula | Threshold |
|---|---|---|
| **Variance Inflation Factor (VIF)** | $VIF_j = \dfrac{1}{1 - R_j^2}$ | $VIF_j > 10$ (or $> 5$) |
| **Condition Number** | $\kappa = \dfrac{\sqrt{\lambda_{max}}}{\sqrt{\lambda_{min}}}$ | $\kappa > 30$ |

Where $R_j^2$ is the $R^2$ from regressing $X_j$ on all other predictors, and $\lambda$ denotes eigenvalues of $\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X}$.

### 2.2 Assumption Verification Tests

| Assumption | Graphical Tool | Formal Test |
|---|---|---|
| **Linearity** | Residuals vs. Fitted | Lack-of-Fit F-test |
| **Independence** | Residuals vs. Order | Durbin–Watson statistic |
| **Homoscedasticity** | Scale-Location plot | Breusch–Pagan, White's test |
| **Normality** | Normal Q-Q plot | Shapiro–Wilk, Anderson–Darling |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Durbin–Watson Statistic (key formula for KTU):**
>
> $$
> DW = \frac{\sum_{i=2}^{n}(e_i - e_{i-1})^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} e_i^2}
> $$
>
> Values near **2** indicate no autocorrelation; values near **0** suggest positive autocorrelation.

### 2.3 Model Adequacy Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> These metrics balance **fit** with **parsimony** — penalizing models that add non-contributing predictors.

| Metric | Formula | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **Coefficient of Determination** | $R^2 = 1 - \dfrac{SS_{Res}}{SS_{Tot}}$ | Proportion of variance explained |
| **Adjusted R²** | $R^2_{adj} = 1 - \dfrac{(1-R^2)(n-1)}{n-p-1}$ | Penalized for extra predictors |
| **Mallows' Cp** | $C_p = \dfrac{SS_{Res}(p)}{MSE_{full}} - (n - 2p)$ | Values near *p* indicate good model |
| **Akaike Information Criterion** | $AIC = n \ln\left(\dfrac{SS_{Res}}{n}\right) + 2(p+1)$ | Lower is better |
| **Bayesian Information Criterion** | $BIC = n \ln\left(\dfrac{SS_{Res}}{n}\right) + (p+1)\ln(n)$ | Stronger penalty than AIC |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceutical DOE**: Diagnosing curvature in response surface models ensures accurate identification of optimal drug formulation.
- **Manufacturing**: Cook's Distance identifies faulty batches that distort the predicted yield equation.
- **Aerospace**: Multicollinearity checks prevent unstable coefficient estimates in regression models linking wind-tunnel factors to drag coefficients.
- **Civil Engineering**: Residual plots validate whether the assumed linear elastic relationship truly holds in stress-strain data.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Hat Matrix and Leverage Values

We begin with the OLS estimator:
$$
\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{Y}
$$

The vector of fitted values is therefore:
$$
\hat{\mathbf{Y}} = \mathbf{X} \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{Y}
$$

Define the **hat matrix** as the operator that converts $\mathbf{Y}$ into $\hat{\mathbf{Y}}$:
$$
\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^\top
$$

Hence the *i*th fitted value is:
$$
\hat{Y}_i = h_{i1}Y_1 + h_{i2}Y_2 + \dots + h_{ii}Y_i + \dots + h_{in}Y_n
$$

The **leverage** $h_{ii}$ is the *i*th diagonal of $\mathbf{H}$, representing the weight that observation *i* assigns to itself. Two key properties follow:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} h_{ii} = \text{tr}(\mathbf{H}) = p + 1
$$

$$
0 \le h_{ii} \le 1
$$

> **Interpretation:** Average leverage is $\frac{p+1}{n}$. Values exceeding $\frac{2(p+1)}{n}$ are flagged as **high-leverage** points.

### 3.2 Derivation: Standardized & Studentized Residuals

The raw residual vector is:
$$
\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{Y} - \hat{\mathbf{Y}} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})\mathbf{Y}
$$

Its covariance matrix is:
$$
\text{Cov}(\mathbf{e}) = \sigma^2 (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})
$$

Therefore, the variance of the *i*th residual is:
$$
\text{Var}(e_i) = \sigma^2 (1 - h_{ii})
$$

Replacing $\sigma^2$ with its unbiased estimator $MSE = s^2$ yields the **standardized residual**:
$$
d_i = \frac{e_i}{s\sqrt{1 - h_{ii}}}
$$

For **externally studentized residuals**, $s^2$ is replaced by $s_{(i)}^2$, the MSE computed after removing observation *i*. Using a standard algebraic identity:
$$
s_{(i)}^2 = \frac{(n-p-1)s^2 - \frac{e_i^2}{1-h_{ii}}}{n-p-2}
$$

Hence:
$$
t_i = \frac{e_i}{s_{(i)}\sqrt{1 - h_{ii}}}
$$

> **Why two forms?** Internal studentization still uses point *i*'s information to estimate variance. External studentization "deletes" point *i*, giving a more honest measure of how *i* alone behaves — important for **outlier detection**.

### 3.3 Derivation: Cook's Distance

Cook's Distance measures the **squared standardized shift** in $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$ when point *i* is removed:

$$
D_i = \frac{(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} - \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{(i)})^\top (\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X}) (\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} - \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{(i)})}{p \cdot s^2}
$$

We use the algebraic identity for the change in $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$ upon deletion:
$$
\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} - \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{(i)} = \frac{(\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_i e_i}{1 - h_{ii}}
$$

Substituting and simplifying (a multi-step algebraic reduction that KTU students should practice on the board):

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_i &= \frac{1}{p \cdot s^2} \cdot \frac{e_i^2}{(1-h_{ii})^2} \cdot \mathbf{x}_i^\top (\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{x}_i \\
&= \frac{e_i^2}{p \cdot s^2} \cdot \frac{h_{ii}}{(1-h_{ii})^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

This **beautiful form** reveals that Cook's Distance is large when **both** the residual $e_i$ and the leverage $h_{ii}$ are large.

### 3.4 Derivation: PRESS Statistic

The **Prediction Error Sum of Squares** quantifies predictive accuracy by leave-one-out cross-validation:

$$
PRESS = \sum_{i=1}^{n} e_{(i)}^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left(\frac{e_i}{1-h_{ii}}\right)^2
$$

The relationship $e_{(i)} = \frac{e_i}{1 - h_{ii}}$ comes from:
$$
\hat{Y}_{(i)} = \hat{Y}_i - \frac{h_{ii} e_i}{1-h_{ii}}
$$

Hence:
$$
e_{(i)} = Y_i - \hat{Y}_{(i)} = e_i + \frac{h_{ii} e_i}{1 - h_{ii}} = \frac{e_i}{1 - h_{ii}}
$$

A **PRESS-related $R^2$** can be computed:
$$
R^2_{PRESS} = 1 - \frac{PRESS}{SS_{Tot}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why PRESS matters in DOE:** When validating a Response Surface Methodology (RSM) model, PRESS offers a near-cross-validation check on prediction accuracy without re-fitting the model $n$ times.

### 3.5 Derivation: Variance Inflation Factor (VIF)

Consider predictor $X_j$. Its $R^2$ from auxiliary regression on all other predictors is $R_j^2$. The variance of $\hat{\beta}_j$ in OLS is:
$$
\text{Var}(\hat{\beta}_j) = \frac{\sigma^2}{(n-1) \cdot s_{X_j}^2 \cdot (1 - R_j^2)}
$$

The factor $\frac{1}{1-R_j^2}$ is the **VIF**:
$$
VIF_j = \frac{1}{1 - R_j^2}
$$

If predictors are orthogonal, $R_j^2 = 0$ and $VIF_j = 1$. As $R_j^2 \to 1$, $VIF_j \to \infty$, indicating **perfect multicollinearity**.

### 3.6 Analytical Master Matrix

The following table is the **single most important reference for KTU problems** on this topic:

| Diagnostic | Inputs Required | Detects | Computational Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| $e_i$ | $\mathbf{Y}, \hat{\mathbf{Y}}$ | Basic misfit | Low |
| $d_i$ | $e_i, s$ | Large residuals | Low |
| $r_i$ | $e_i, s, h_{ii}$ | Outliers (internally standardized) | Low |
| $t_i$ | $e_i, s_{(i)}, h_{ii}$ | True outliers | Moderate |
| $h_{ii}$ | $\mathbf{X}$ | High-leverage points | Low |
| $D_i$ | $e_i, h_{ii}, s, p$ | Influential observations | Moderate |
| $DFFITS_i$ | $t_i, h_{ii}$ | Influence on fitted value | Moderate |
| $DFBETAS_{j(i)}$ | $\hat{\beta}_j, \hat{\beta}_{j(i)}, C_{jj}$ | Influence on each coefficient | High |
| $VIF_j$ | Auxiliary $R_j^2$ | Multicollinearity | Moderate |
| $PRESS$ | $e_i, h_{ii}$ | Predictive accuracy | Moderate |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagnostic Workflow Diagram

The following diagram outlines the canonical sequence of diagnostics performed after fitting a regression model in a DOE context.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fit Regression Model via OLS] --> B[Compute Residuals e_i]
    B --> C{Assumption Checks}
    C --> C1[Linearity: Residuals vs Fitted Plot]
    C --> C2[Normality: Q-Q Plot + Shapiro-Wilk]
    C --> C3[Homoscedasticity: Breusch-Pagan]
    C --> C4[Independence: Durbin-Watson]
    C1 --> D{All Assumptions Met?}
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    D -- No --> E[Apply Remediation]
    E --> E1[Transform Variables]
    E --> E2[Add Interaction or Polynomial Terms]
    E --> E3[Use Weighted Least Squares]
    E --> A
    D -- Yes --> F[Compute Leverage h_ii]
    F --> G[Flag High Leverage Points]
    G --> H[Compute Cook Distance D_i]
    H --> I[Compute DFFITS and DFBETAS]
    I --> J[Check Multicollinearity VIF]
    J --> K{Final Model Valid?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Accept Model for Inference]
    K -- No --> M[Remove Influential Points or Refit]
    M --> A
```

### 4.2 Influence Measure Decision Map

This diagram maps the **logical flow** a KTU student should follow when classifying an unusual observation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Observation i Identified] --> Q{Large Residual?}
    Q -- No --> R{High Leverage?}
    R -- No --> S[Routine Point: No Action]
    R -- Yes --> T[High Leverage but Low Influence: Monitor]
    Q -- Yes --> U{High Leverage?}
    U -- No --> V[Outlier but Low Influence: Investigate Y_i]
    U -- Yes --> W[Influential Point: Compute Cook D and DFFITS]
    W --> X{Cook D Greater Than 1?}
    X -- Yes --> Y[Candidate for Removal or Refit]
    X -- No --> Z[Borderline: Examine DFBETAS]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Diagnostic Engine

In applied DOE software, a diagnostics module operates as a layered functional block interacting with the regression engine.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer[Input Layer]
        I1[Design Matrix X]
        I2[Response Vector Y]
    end
    subgraph FitLayer[Regression Fit Layer]
        F1[OLS Solver]
        F2[Hat Matrix H]
        F3[Residual Vector e]
    end
    subgraph DiagnosticLayer[Diagnostic Layer]
        D1[Leverage Calculator]
        D2[Standardized Residual Engine]
        D3[Cook Distance Engine]
        D4[VIF Calculator]
        D5[Assumption Test Suite]
    end
    subgraph OutputLayer[Output Layer]
        O1[Diagnostic Tables]
        O2[Diagnostic Plots]
        O3[Influence Flags]
    end
    I1 --> F1
    I2 --> F1
    F1 --> F2
    F1 --> F3
    F2 --> D1
    F3 --> D2
    F2 --> D3
    F3 --> D3
    I1 --> D4
    F3 --> D5
    F2 --> D5
    D1 --> O1
    D2 --> O1
    D3 --> O1
    D4 --> O1
    D5 --> O1
    D1 --> O2
    D2 --> O2
    D5 --> O2
    D1 --> O3
    D3 --> O3
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: Define Leverage in Regression Diagnostics. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
Leverage, denoted $h_{ii}$, is the *i*th diagonal element of the hat matrix $\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top$. It quantifies how far an observation's predictor values are from the centroid of the predictor space. A point is considered high-leverage if $h_{ii} > \dfrac{2(p+1)}{n}$. High-leverage points exert strong pull on the fitted regression line, even if their residuals are small. **(3 Marks)**

#### Question 2: What is Cook's Distance and What Threshold Indicates an Influential Point? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
Cook's Distance $D_i$ measures the squared standardized change in the regression coefficients when observation *i* is removed. The formula is:

$$
D_i = \frac{e_i^2}{p \cdot s^2} \cdot \frac{h_{ii}}{(1-h_{ii})^2}
$$

A point is flagged as influential when $D_i > 1$ (or alternatively $D_i > \dfrac{4}{n}$). Values exceeding these thresholds suggest that the observation substantially alters the fitted model. **(3 Marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - Students frequently **forget to specify the threshold** when defining leverage or Cook's Distance — always state the cut-off.
> - In derivation questions, marks are awarded for **each algebraic step**, not just the final formula. Skipping intermediate algebra can cost **3 to 4 marks**.
> - Many students confuse **standardized** and **studentized** residuals. Remember: standardized uses $s$, studentized (external) uses $s_{(i)}$.
> - VIF interpretation must include both the **formula** and the **threshold (VIF > 10)** to secure full credit.

---

#### Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the four key assumptions of a linear regression model. For each assumption, mention one diagnostic plot or formal test used to verify it. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The four key assumptions of the linear regression model are:

1. **Linearity:** The relationship between the response and predictors is linear. This is checked using a **Residuals vs. Fitted Values plot** — a random scatter around zero confirms linearity. A systematic U-shape or curve indicates nonlinearity. The formal test is the **Lack-of-Fit F-test**, which compares the pure-error variance to the lack-of-fit variance.

2. **Independence:** Error terms are uncorrelated. This is verified using a **Residuals vs. Order plot** (no trend) or the **Durbin–Watson statistic**, where values near 2 indicate no autocorrelation.
