| **Run** | $\lambda$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $\hat{y} = 50 + 3.0 x_1 + 1.5 x_2$ |
|---------|-----------|-------|-------|-------------------------------------|
| Origin | 0 | 0 | 0.0 | 50.0 |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | 0.5 | 53.75 |
| 2 | 2 | 2 | 1.0 | 57.50 |
| 3 | 3 | 3 | 1.5 | 61.25 |

The experimenter conducts these runs and identifies the run with maximum yield — typically near $\lambda = 3$. Beyond this point, if yield declines, a second-order design is implemented centered at the new optimal region.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Q: Explain the role of the Method of Steepest Ascent in Response Surface Methodology. Discuss its limitations and how it connects to second-order designs.**

**Model Solution:**

**Role of Steepest Ascent (7 Marks):**

The Method of Steepest Ascent serves as a **bridge between first-order and second-order designs** in Response Surface Methodology. After a $2^k$ factorial design reveals a lack of fit (curvature), it provides a systematic, data-driven path to navigate from the current operating point toward the optimum. It uses the regression coefficients of the first-order model to compute a direction:

$$\mathbf{d}_{\text{opt}} = (\hat{\beta}_1, \hat{\beta}_2, \ldots, \hat{\beta}_k)$$

The experimenter then performs sequential runs along this direction with a chosen step size $\lambda$.

**Limitations and Connection to Second-Order Designs (7 Marks):**

**Limitations:**
1. It assumes the first-order model is **locally valid** — only useful far from the optimum.
2. It does **not** predict the exact optimum, only the promising region.
3. The step size $\lambda$ is somewhat **arbitrary** and may not always reflect practical engineering constraints.
4. It is **inefficient** if the underlying response is highly nonlinear.

**Connection to Second-Order Designs:**
When the response stops increasing along the path, curvature is detected. A second-order model is then required:

$$\hat{y} = \hat{\beta}_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \hat{\beta}_i x_i + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \hat{\beta}_{ii} x_i^2 + \sum_{i<j} \hat{\beta}_{ij} x_i x_j$$

Common second-order designs include the **Central Composite Design (CCD)** and the **Box-Behnken Design**. These allow the precise location of the stationary point via **ridge analysis**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students frequently confuse the **direction of steepest ascent** with the **direction of steepest descent** — always verify the sign of coefficients.
> 2. A common error is forgetting to **re-scale coded units back to natural units** before conducting experiments.
> 3. Many students assume the steepest ascent point is the **final optimum** — this is incorrect; it only identifies the **region**.
> 4. Step size selection ($\lambda$) must be **justified** with practical reasoning; arbitrary choices may lose marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The Method of Steepest Ascent is a **sequential optimization** procedure used after a first-order design.
- Direction of movement is along the **gradient vector** of the fitted model: $\mathbf{d} = (\hat{\beta}_1, \ldots, \hat{\beta}_k)$.
- Path coordinates: $\xi_i = x_i + \lambda \hat{\beta}_i$.
- Step size $\lambda$ is chosen based on **practical engineering constraints**, often using the largest coefficient as reference.
- The procedure **stops** when the response ceases to increase — indicating proximity to the optimum.
- After stopping, a **second-order design** (CCD or Box-Behnken) is used to **precisely locate** the stationary point.
- This method is **purely experimental** — no assumption about the underlying functional form is required.
- The Cauchy-Schwarz inequality proves that the coefficient vector gives the **maximum rate of increase**.
- Used extensively in **chemical, pharmaceutical, and manufacturing** industries.
- Limitations include **arbitrary step size** and **inability to predict exact optimum**.
- Critical transition: **Factorial → Steepest Ascent → Second-Order Design** is the canonical RSM workflow.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Response Surface Designs for First-Order Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT727 - DESIGN OF EXPERIMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Response Surface Designs for First-Order Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Definition — First-Order Response Surface Model**
> A **first-order response surface model** is a low-order polynomial approximation used to locally describe the relationship between a set of *k* independent (predictor) variables $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_k$ and a single response (dependent) variable *y*. The general mathematical form is:

$$y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \dots + \beta_k x_k + \varepsilon$$

where:
- $\beta_0$ is the **intercept** (true mean response at origin).
- $\beta_i$ are the **first-order (linear) regression coefficients**.
- $\varepsilon$ is the **random error**, assumed $N(0, \sigma^2)$.
- The model contains **no interaction terms** ($x_i x_j$) and **no quadratic (curvature) terms** ($x_i^2$).

In **matrix form**, the first-order model is expressed as:

$$y = X\beta + \varepsilon$$

where $X$ is an $(N \times (k+1))$ design matrix with a leading column of **1's** and *k* columns of coded variable levels.

> [!NOTE] **Response Surface Methodology (RSM)** is a collection of mathematical and statistical techniques useful for developing, improving, and optimizing processes. **First-order designs** form the *initial exploratory phase* of RSM, applied when the experimenter is far from the optimum and only wishes to identify the important variables and the direction of improvement.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Hiking on Flat Terrain"

> [!TIP] **Plain-English Analogy: Finding the Slope of a Hill**
> Imagine you are dropped blindfolded on a hilly landscape and you want to reach the highest peak (the optimum response). If the patch of land you are standing on looks roughly like a **flat tilted plane** (no curves, no bumps), then a first-order model is perfect — you just measure the **slope** in different directions and walk uphill along the **steepest path**.
>
> **The math maps directly:**
> - The **tilted plane** = First-order model $y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2$.
> - The **slope coefficients** $\beta_1, \beta_2$ tell you how steep the hill is in each direction.
> - **Steepest Ascent** = the strategy of walking straight up the steepest slope.
> - **Curvature (curved hill)** = what a *second-order* model would later handle — once you reach near the peak, you switch to a curved model.
>
> So first-order designs are your **"compass and slope meter"** in the initial reconnaissance stage of RSM.

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Boundary Conventions

| Parameter | Standard Coding Convention | Notation Used |
|-----------|--------------------------|---------------|
| Coded levels | $-1$ (low), $+1$ (high) | $(-1, +1)$ |
| Center point | $0$ | $(0, 0, \dots, 0)$ |
| Number of runs | $N$ (typically $2^k$, $2^{k-1}$, or $N = k+1$) | varies |
| Replication | $n_c$ center-point replicates | $n_c \geq 3$ |
| True significance level | **5%** (default in KTU problems) | $\alpha = 0.05$ |
| Orthogonality factor | $N$ (diagonal of $X'X$) | $X'X = N \cdot I_{k+1}$ |

> [!WARNING] **KTU Convention:** All coded variables in DOE are **dimensionless**, scaled to lie in $[-1, +1]$. Conversion formulas:
> $$x_i = \frac{X_i - \bar{X}_i}{(X_{i,\text{high}} - X_{i,\text{low}})/2}, \qquad X_i = X_{i,\text{center}} + x_i \cdot \Delta X_i$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 When Do We Use a First-Order Model?

A first-order model is **appropriate** when:
1. The experimenter is **far from the optimum** (initial screening).
2. The number of factors $k$ is **large** (typically $k \geq 4$).
3. Only the **direction of steepest ascent** is sought.
4. The response surface is suspected to be **locally planar** (no curvature).

> [!IMPORTANT] **The Three Phases of RSM (Box & Wilson, 1951)**
> 1. **Phase 1 — First-Order Phase:** Factorial / Fractional factorial + Steepest Ascent.
> 2. **Phase 2 — Transition Phase:** Detect curvature (via center points).
> 3. **Phase 3 — Second-Order Phase:** Central Composite Design (CCD) or Box–Behnken Design (BBD).

---

### 2.2 First-Order Design Choices (KTU High-Yield List)

| Design Type | Number of Runs $N$ | Suitable When | Key Property |
|-------------|--------------------|---------------|--------------|
| $2^k$ **Full Factorial** | $2^k$ | $k \leq 4$, no resource constraint | Estimable, orthogonal, full resolution V |
| $2^{k-1}$ **Half Fraction** | $2^{k-1}$ | $k = 4, 5, 6$ | Resolution IV — main effects clear of 2FI |
| $2^{k-2}$ **Quarter Fraction** | $2^{k-2}$ | $k \geq 7$ | Resolution III — main effects confounded with 2FI |
| **Plackett–Burman** | $N = k + 1$ (or $4m$) | $k$ large (e.g., $k = 8$ to $31$) | **Saturated** — all DOF used for estimation |
| **Simple Simplex** | $N = k + 1$ | Mixture variables | Robust to mis-specification |

> [!TIP] A design is called **saturated** when the number of unknown parameters equals the number of runs — i.e., zero degrees of freedom for error. Plackett–Burman designs are the most common **saturated first-order designs** in KTU problems.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] **Master Cheat Sheet — First-Order Design Mathematics**

| # | Concept | Formula / Identity |
|---|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | First-order model | $y = \beta_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{k}\beta_i x_i + \varepsilon$ |
| 2 | Matrix model | $\mathbf{y} = X\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$ |
| 3 | Least Squares Estimator | $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (X'X)^{-1} X'\mathbf{y}$ |
| 4 | Orthogonal design identity | $X'X = N \cdot I_{k+1}$ |
| 5 | Individual estimate (orthogonal) | $\hat{\beta}_j = \dfrac{\sum_{u=1}^{N} x_{ju} y_u}{N}$ |
| 6 | Main effect in $2^k$ | $E_i = 2 \hat{\beta}_i$ |
| 7 | Sum of squares due to $x_j$ | $SS_j = N \hat{\beta}_j^2$ |
| 8 | Total Sum of Squares | $SS_T = \sum y_u^2 - \dfrac{(\sum y_u)^2}{N}$ |
| 9 | Variance of $\hat{\beta}_j$ | $\text{Var}(\hat{\beta}_j) = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{N}$ |
| 10 | Pure-error variance | $\hat{\sigma}^2 = \dfrac{\sum_{u=1}^{n_c}(y_{0u} - \bar{y}_0)^2}{n_c - 1}$ |
| 11 | Lack of Fit SS | $SS_{LOF} = n_c (\bar{y}_F - \bar{y}_0)^2 + \sum_{i=1}^{n_F}(\bar{y}_i - \bar{y}_F)^2$ |
| 12 | Steepest Ascent step (proportional) | $\Delta x_i \propto \hat{\beta}_i$ (coded units) |
| 13 | Steepest Ascent actual step | $\Delta X_i = \dfrac{\hat{\beta}_i}{\hat{\beta}_r} \cdot \Delta X_r$ (choose a reference variable $r$) |
| 14 | Resolution III criterion | All main effects confounded with at least one 2-factor interaction |
| 15 | Resolution IV criterion | No main effect confounded with any 2FI; some 2FIs confounded |
| 16 | Plackett–Burman run size | $N = 4m$, $m = 1, 2, 3, \dots$ (gives $k = N - 1$ factors) |

> [!CAUTION] **Coded vs Actual Units for Steepest Ascent**
> Steepest ascent is computed in **coded units** (where all slopes are comparable), then converted to **actual engineering units** before running the next experiment.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of First-Order RSM |
|-------|-------------------------------|
| **Chemical Engineering** | Screening reaction variables (temperature, pressure, pH, feed rate) before process optimization |
| **Mechanical / Manufacturing** | Identifying dominant factors in CNC machining (cut speed, feed, depth-of-cut) |
| **Pharmaceutical** | Initial factor screening in formulation development |
| **Electronics / VLSI** | Parameter optimization of wafer fabrication processes |
| **Food Technology** | Texture, taste, color optimization with many potential ingredients |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Least Squares Estimator for First-Order Models

We seek $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$ that minimizes $S(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = (\mathbf{y} - X\boldsymbol{\beta})'(\mathbf{y} - X\boldsymbol{\beta})$.

**Step 1 — Expand the residual sum of squares:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(\boldsymbol{\beta}) &= (\mathbf{y} - X\boldsymbol{\beta})'(\mathbf{y} - X\boldsymbol{\beta}) \\
&= \mathbf{y}'\mathbf{y} - 2\boldsymbol{\beta}'X'\mathbf{y} + \boldsymbol{\beta}'X'X\boldsymbol{\beta}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to $\boldsymbol{\beta}$ and set to zero (Normal Equations):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial \boldsymbol{\beta}} &= -2 X'\mathbf{y} + 2 X'X \boldsymbol{\beta} = \mathbf{0}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
X'X \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} &= X'\mathbf{y} \\
\Rightarrow \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} &= (X'X)^{-1} X'\mathbf{y}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — For a $2^k$ orthogonal design, $X'X = N I_{k+1}$, so:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{\beta}_0 &= \dfrac{\sum_{u=1}^{N} y_u}{N} \\
\hat{\beta}_j &= \dfrac{\sum_{u=1}^{N} x_{ju} y_u}{N}, \quad j = 1, 2, \dots, k
\end{aligned}
$$

Each coefficient is estimated **independently** because of orthogonality — this is the foundation of $2^k$ designs.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Steepest Ascent Direction

Given the fitted first-order model $\hat{y} = \hat{\beta}_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{k}\hat{\beta}_i x_i$, the **gradient vector** in coded units is:

$$
\nabla \hat{y} = \left( \dfrac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial x_1}, \dfrac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial x_2}, \dots, \dfrac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial x_k} \right) = (\hat{\beta}_1, \hat{\beta}_2, \dots, \hat{\beta}_k)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Steepest Ascent Principle:** Movement along the gradient vector increases $\hat{y}$ at the **maximum instantaneous rate**. The path is taken in coded units, then scaled.

**Step-by-step construction of the steepest ascent path:**

1. Choose a reference variable $r$ (usually the one with largest $|\hat{\beta}_r|$).
2. Pick a step size $\Delta X_r$ in *actual* units (engineer judgment).
3. Compute steps for the other variables:

$$
\Delta X_i = \dfrac{\hat{\beta}_i}{\hat{\beta}_r} \cdot \Delta X_r \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \dots, k
$$

4. Starting from the design center, perform experiments along this direction until the response decreases or flattens.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A $2^2$ factorial design with **2 center-point replicates** is used. Data:

| Run | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $y$ |
|-----|-------|-------|------|
| 1 | $-1$ | $-1$ | 12 |
| 2 | $+1$ | $-1$ | 18 |
| 3 | $-1$ | $+1$ | 14 |
| 4 | $+1$ | $+1$ | 20 |
| 5 | $0$ | $0$ | 15 |
| 6 | $0$ | $0$ | 17 |

**Step 1 — Verify orthogonality of $X'X$:**

$$
X'X = \begin{bmatrix} 6 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 4 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 4 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Thus, $(X'X)^{-1} = \text{diag}(1/6, 1/4, 1/4)$.

**Step 2 — Compute the coefficient estimates:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{\beta}_0 &= \dfrac{\sum y_u}{6} = \dfrac{12+18+14+20+15+17}{6} = \dfrac{96}{6} = 16.00 \\
\hat{\beta}_1 &= \dfrac{\sum x_{1u} y_u}{4} = \dfrac{(-1)(12)+(+1)(18)+(-1)(14)+(+1)(20)+0+0}{4} = \dfrac{12}{4} = 3.00 \\
\hat{\beta}_2 &= \dfrac{\sum x_{2u} y_u}{4} = \dfrac{(-1)(12)+(-1)(18)+(+1)(14)+(+1)(20)+0+0}{4} = \dfrac{4}{4} = 1.00
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Fitted first-order model:**

$$
\hat{y} = 16.00 + 3.00 \, x_1 + 1.00 \, x_2
$$

**Step 4 — Steepest Ascent path (take $\Delta x_1 = 1$ as reference):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta x_2 &= \dfrac{\hat{\beta}_2}{\hat{\beta}_1} \cdot \Delta x_1 = \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot 1 = 0.333
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Lack of Fit test:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{y}_F &= \dfrac{15+17}{2} = 16 \\
SS_{PE} &= (15-16)^2 + (17-16)^2 = 2 \\
df_{PE} &= 1 \\
\hat{\sigma}^2_{PE} &= 2 \\
\bar{y}_{\text{factorial}} &= \dfrac{12+18+14+20}{4} = 16 \\
SS_{LOF} &= n_c(\bar{y}_F - \bar{y}_{\text{all factorial}})^2 = 2 \times 0 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $SS_{LOF} = 0$, the first-order model **fits well** (no significant curvature detected).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture Flow — First-Order RSM Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Process & Response y] --> B[List Candidate Factors x1 to xk]
    B --> C{Screening Required?}
    C -- Yes Large k --> D[Saturated or Fractional First-Order Design]
    C -- No Small k --> E[Full 2 to the k Factorial]
    D --> F[Run Experiments + Center Points]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Fit First-Order Model y = beta0 + betai xi]
    G --> H[Test Lack of Fit with Center Points]
    H -- Fit OK --> I[Estimate Coefficients and Effects]
    H -- Fit Bad Curvature --> J[Augment with Axial Points or Switch to Second-Order CCD]
    I --> K[Compute Steepest Ascent Direction]
    K --> L[Walk Along Path Until Plateau]
    L --> M[New Optimal Region Reached]
    M --> N[Re-fit Second-Order Model CCD or BBD]
    N --> O[Locate Optimum via Canonical or RSM Analysis]
    style A fill:#e8f4ff,stroke:#1f4e79
    style G fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#b45f06
    style K fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#2e7d32
    style N fill:#ffe6f0,stroke:#8e3a59
```

---

### 4.2 Schematic — Decision Logic for First-Order Designs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Number of Factors k] --> P2{k less than equal 4}
    P2 -- Yes --> P3[Use 2 to the k Full Factorial]
    P2 -- No --> P4{k between 5 and 7}
    P4 -- Yes --> P5[Use 2 to the k minus 1 Half Fraction Resolution IV]
    P4 -- No --> P6{k greater than 7}
    P6 -- Yes --> P7[Use Plackett-Burman or 2 to the k minus 2 Resolution III]
    P3 --> P8[Always Add 3 to 5 Center Points]
    P5 --> P8
    P7 --> P8
    P8 --> P9[Fit Model]
    style P8 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000
```

---

### 4.3 Schematic — Plackett–Burman Run Structure (Conceptual Block Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph PB[Plackett Burman N = 12 Design Block]
        R1[Run 01 Pattern] --> R2[Run 02 Pattern]
        R2 --> R3[Run 03 Pattern]
        R3 --> R4[Run 04 Pattern]
        R4 --> R5[Run 05 Pattern]
        R5 --> R6[Run 06 Pattern]
        R6 --> R7[Run 07 Pattern]
        R7 --> R8[Run 08 Pattern]
        R8 --> R9[Run 09 Pattern]
        R9 --> R10[Run 10 Pattern]
        R10 --> R11[Run 11 Pattern]
        R11 --> R12[Run 12 Pattern]
    end
    PB --> OUT[Total 11 Factors Estimated from 12 Runs Saturated]
    style OUT fill:#ffd6cc,stroke:#a64b00
```

> [!TIP] **Reading Aid:** Plackett–Burman designs are *fold-over* Hadamard matrices. The number of factors that can be estimated is always $k = N - 1$. For KTU exam purposes, memorize the standard run sizes $N = 8, 12, 16, 20, 24$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define a first-order response surface design. Under what conditions in RSM is it most appropriate?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **first-order response surface design** is an experimental plan used to fit a linear polynomial model of the form:

$$y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \dots + \beta_k x_k + \varepsilon$$

It contains only **linear terms** and is appropriate when:
1. The experimenter is **far from the optimum** and the response surface is locally planar (no curvature).
2. The objective is to **screen many factors** and identify those with significant main effects.
3. Used as the **initial phase of RSM** before moving to steepest ascent and a second-order design.

Typical examples: $2^k$ full factorial, $2^{k-1}$ fractional factorial, and **Plackett–Burman** designs.

*(Keywords: linear, screening, far from optimum, no curvature — 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is a saturated design? Give one example used for first-order models.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **saturated design** is one in which the number of runs $N$ equals the number of parameters to be estimated. Consequently, there are **zero pure-error degrees of freedom** — the design provides no internal estimate of error.

**Example:** The **Plackett–Burman design** is a saturated first-order design. For $N$ runs, it can estimate up to $k = N - 1$ main effects. A common KTU-ready example is the $N = 8$ run design used to screen $k = 7$ factors.

*(Saturated = N = parameters; Plackett–Burman example — 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Adapted)] (14 Marks)**

**(a)** A $2^3$ full factorial design with **three center-point replicates** was run to study the yield $y$ of a chemical process. The data (in coded variables) are:

| Run | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $x_3$ | $y$ |
|-----|-------|-------|-------|-----|
| 1 | $-1$ | $-1$ | $-1$ | 28 |
| 2 | $+1$ | $-1$ | $-1$ | 36 |
| 3 | $-1$ | $+1$ | $-1$ | 30 |
| 4 | $+1$ | $+1$ | $-1$ | 42 |
| 5 | $-1$ | $-1$ | $+1$ | 32 |
| 6 | $+1$ | $-1$ | $+1$ | 40 |
| 7 | $-1$ | $+1$ | $+1$ | 34 |
| 8 | $+1$ | $+1$ | $+1$ | 50 |
| 9 | $0$ | $0$ | $0$ | 38 |
| 10 | $0$ | $0$ | $0$ | 40 |
| 11 | $0$ | $0$ | $0$ | 36 |

(i) Estimate the coefficients of the first-order model. *(5 marks)*
(ii) Conduct a **Lack of Fit** test at $\alpha = 0.05$. *(4 marks)*

**(b)** Using the fitted model, determine the **steepest ascent** path in coded units and convert the first step to **actual engineering units** if the variable ranges are $X_1 \in [80, 120]$ °C and $X_2 \in [2, 6]$ bar. *(5 marks)*

---

**Model Solution — Question A:**

**(a)(i) Coefficient Estimation:**

For a $2^3$ orthogonal design with $N = 8$ factorial runs, $X'X = 8 I_4$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{\beta}_0 &= \dfrac{28+36+30+42+32+40+34+50}{8} = \dfrac{292}{8} = 36.50 \\
\hat{\beta}_1 &= \dfrac{(-28+36-30+42-32+40-34+50)}{8} = \dfrac{44}{8} = 5.50 \\
\hat{\beta}_2 &= \dfrac{(-28-36+30+42-32-40+34+50)}{8} = \dfrac{20}{8} = 2.50 \\
\hat{\beta}_3 &= \dfrac{(-28-36-30-42+32+40+34+50)}{8} = \dfrac{20}{8} = 2.50
\end{aligned}
$$

Fitted first-order model:

$$\hat{y} = 36.50 + 5.50 \, x_1 + 2.50 \, x_2 + 2.50 \, x_3$$

**(a)(ii) Lack of Fit Test:**

Step 1 — Pure error from center points:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{y}_0 &= \dfrac{38+40+36}{3} = 38 \\
SS_{PE} &= (38-38)^2 + (40-38)^2 + (36-38)^2 = 0 + 4 + 4 = 8 \\
df_{PE} &= 2, \quad MS_{PE} = 4
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2 — Mean of factorial runs:

$$
\bar{y}_F = 36.5
$$

Step 3 — Lack of fit:

$$
\begin{aligned}
SS_{LOF} &= n_c (\bar{y}_0 - \bar{y}_F)^2 = 3(38 - 36.5)^2 = 3(1.5)^2 = 6.75 \\
df_{LOF} &= 1 \\
MS_{LOF} &= 6.75
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 4 — F-test:

$$
F_0 = \dfrac{MS_{LOF}}{MS_{PE}} = \dfrac{6.75}{4} = 1.6875
$$

Critical value: $F_{0.05, 1, 2} = 18.51$. Since $1.6875 < 18.51$, we **fail to reject** $H_0$ — **no significant lack of fit**. The first-order model is adequate.

**(b) Steepest Ascent:**

In coded units, the gradient is $(\hat{\beta}_1, \hat{\beta}_2, \hat{\beta}_3) = (5.50, 2.50, 2.50)$. Choosing $\Delta x_1 = 1$ (step in $x_1$):

$$
\Delta x_2 = \dfrac{2.50}{5.50} = 0.4545, \quad \Delta x_3 = \dfrac{2.50}{5.50} = 0.4545
$$

**Conversion to actual units:**

$$
\Delta X_1 = 1 \times 20 = 20 \text{ °C}, \quad \Delta X_2 = 0.4545 \times 2 = 0.909 \text{ bar}, \quad \Delta X_3 = 0.4545 \times 2 = 0.909 \text{ (units of } X_3)
$$

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Adapted)] (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain with neat steps the **method of steepest ascent** as applied in first-order response surface methodology. Why must we move along the path in *coded* units, not *actual* units? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe **Plackett–Burman designs** as a class of first-order designs. Show how $k = 7$ factors can be screened in just $N = 8$ runs. State the key property that makes these designs economical. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution — Question B:**

**(a) Method of Steepest Ascent:**

1. Fit the first-order model $\hat{y} = \hat{\beta}_0 + \sum \hat{\beta}_i x_i$ using factorial / fractional factorial data.
2. Compute the gradient $(\hat{\beta}_1, \hat{\beta}_2, \dots, \hat{\beta}_k)$ — each component is the partial slope.
3. Pick a reference variable $r$ (usually the one with largest $|\hat{\beta}_r|$).
4. Choose a practical step $\Delta X_r$ in actual engineering units.
5. Compute other steps: $\Delta X_i = \dfrac{\hat{\beta}_i}{\hat{\beta}_r} \cdot \Delta X_r$.
6. Starting at the design center, run experiments along this direction.
7. Stop when response decreases — this region becomes the new operating center for a second-order design.

**Why coded units?**
- In *actual* units, coefficients depend on the unit of measurement (e.g., $1$ °C vs $1$ K).
- In *coded* units, all variables are scaled to the same range $[-1, +1]$, so coefficients are **directly comparable** and the relative steepness truly reflects the variable's effect.
- The gradient direction is meaningful **only in coded space**.

**(b) Plackett–Burman Designs:**

Plackett–Burman designs are a family of **saturated two-level designs** derived from **Hadamard matrices** of order $N = 4m$, where $m = 1, 2, 3, \dots$. The number of estimable factors is $k = N - 1$.

**For $k = 7$ factors, use $N = 8$ runs (first column of $H_8$):**

$$
H_8 = \begin{bmatrix}
+ & + & + & - & + & - & - & - \\
- & + & + & + & - & + & - & - \\
- & - & + & + & + & - & + & - \\
+ & - & - & + & + & + & - & - \\
- & + & - & - & + & + & + & - \\
+ & - & + & - & - & + & + & - \\
+ & + & - & + & - & - & + & - \\
- & - & - & - & - & - & - & -
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Rows 1–7 form the **Plackett–Burman design matrix** for $k = 7$ factors; row 8 is a *dummy* factor used for error estimation.

**Key economical property:** Plackett–Burman designs are **orthogonal, saturated, and resolution III** — they estimate all $k$ main effects using only $k+1$ runs, achieving maximum information per experimental unit. This makes them the most efficient **screening designs** in the first-order RSM toolkit.

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting center-point replicates:** Without $n_c \geq 3$ center-point runs, the *Lack of Fit* test cannot be performed — students often lose **2 marks** by skipping this step.
> 2. **Confusing main effect with coefficient:** In a $2^k$ design, $E_i = 2\hat{\beta}_i$, NOT $\hat{\beta}_i$. Examiners check this carefully.
> 3. **Steepest ascent in actual units:** Always convert through coded units first; doing the calculation directly in actual units is a **3-mark deduction**.
> 4. **Plackett–Burman — confusing runs vs factors:** $N$ runs estimate $N - 1$ factors; do not write "8 runs estimate 8 factors."
> 5. **ANOVA table missing:** Even for first-order designs, KTU expects a complete ANOVA breakdown (Regression, Residual, Lack-of-Fit, Pure Error, Total). Missing the decomposition costs **2–3 marks**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist — First-Order Response Surface Designs**

- [ ] **Model form:** $y = \beta_0 + \sum \beta_i x_i + \varepsilon$ — *linear only, no $x_i x_j$, no $x_i^2$.*
- [ ] **Orthogonality** of $2^k$ designs makes $\hat{\beta}_j = \frac{\sum x_{ju} y_u}{N}$ — coefficients are **independent**.
- [ ] **Main effect** in $2^k$: $E_j = 2\hat{\beta}_j$.
- [ ] **Center-point replicates** ($n_c \geq 3$) are *mandatory* for the **Lack-of-Fit test**.
- [ ] **Lack of Fit F-test:** $F_0 = MS_{LOF} / MS_{PE}$ compared with $F_{\alpha, df_{LOF}, df_{PE}}$.
- [ ] **Steepest Ascent path:** $\Delta X_i = (\hat{\beta}_i / \hat{\beta}_r) \cdot \Delta X_r$ — move in **coded** units, run in **actual** units.
- [ ] **Resolution III designs** (e.g., $2^{k-2}$, Plackett–Burman) confound main effects with 2FIs — OK for **screening only**.
- [ ] **Resolution IV designs** (e.g., $2^{k-1}$) keep main effects clear of 2FIs.
- [ ] **Plackett–Burman** is the canonical **saturated** first-order design: $k = N - 1$.
- [ ] **Transition to second-order:** when Lack-of-Fit test is **significant**, augment the design with axial (star) points to form a **Central Composite Design (CCD)**.
- [ ] Always state $R^2$, $R^2_{\text{adj}}$, and predicted $R^2$ when reporting a fitted first-order model.
- [ ] **Coding formula:** $x_i = (X_i - \bar{X}_i) / \Delta X_i$ — must appear in any KTU numerical answer.

> [!TIP] **One-Line Takeaway:**
> *First-order designs answer "Which factors matter and in what direction?" — then steepest ascent guides you to the mountain peak, where a second-order design takes over.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of a Second Order Response Surface
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT727 - DESIGN OF EXPERIMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Analysis of a Second Order Response Surface

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In **Response Surface Methodology (RSM)**, once an experimenter suspects that a first-order model is inadequate (because the response exhibits curvature), a **second-order polynomial model** is fitted using designs such as the **3^k Factorial**, the **Central Composite Design (CCD)**, or the **Box–Behnken Design (BBD)**.

The fitted second-order response surface in *k* coded predictor variables is expressed in matrix form as:

$$\hat{y} = \beta_{0} + \mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{b} + \mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{B}\mathbf{x}$$

where,

- $\beta_0$ is the constant (intercept) term,
- $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_k]^{T}$ is the vector of coded independent variables,
- $\mathbf{b} = [\hat{\beta}_1, \hat{\beta}_2, \ldots, \hat{\beta}_k]^{T}$ is the vector of first-order (linear) regression coefficients,
- $\mathbf{B}$ is the symmetric $k \times k$ matrix of **pure quadratic** coefficients on the diagonal and **half the interaction** coefficients off-diagonal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Emphasis:** The analysis of this surface is the heart of Module 4. It answers three crucial engineering questions:
> 1. *Where* on the factor-space does the optimum response occur?
> 2. *What type* of optimum is it (maximum, minimum, or saddle)?
> 3. *How* do we reach it if the surface has no stationary point (ridge system)?

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hiker on a Rolling Mountain"

Imagine you are standing blindfolded in a vast rolling mountain range with no map. Your goal is to find the **deepest valley** (minimum) or the **highest peak** (maximum). The second-order polynomial acts like a smooth mathematical *blanket* that researchers drape over this mountain landscape using sample points (experimental runs) as pegs.

- The **fitted surface** $\hat{y}$ is the blanket.
- The **gradient** $\partial \hat{y}/\partial \mathbf{x}$ is the slope of the ground under your feet.
- Setting the gradient to **zero** is equivalent to finding the place where the ground is momentarily flat — the **summit** or the **bottom**.
- If all surrounding slopes point *downward* in every direction, you are at a **peak**. If they all point *upward*, you are at a **valley**. If slopes point *up* in some directions and *down* in others, you are on a **mountain pass (saddle point)**, where the only way is to keep walking — this is a **ridge system**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Metric:** In real engineering (e.g., maximizing yield in a chemical reactor), $\hat{y}$ is measured in the response units (%, ppm, MPa, etc.). The position $\mathbf{x}_s$ is reported in **coded units** $(-1, +1)$ for analytical clarity and converted to natural units for implementation.

### 1.3 The Three Outcomes of a Second-Order Surface

| Outcome Type | Mathematical Signature | Real-World Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Maximum** | All eigenvalues of $\mathbf{B}$ are negative | Best achievable response (e.g., max strength, max yield) |
| **Minimum** | All eigenvalues of $\mathbf{B}$ are positive | Lowest response (e.g., min cost, min defect rate) |
| **Saddle Point** | Eigenvalues have **mixed signs** | No true optimum inside the region — perform Ridge Analysis |
| **Ridge System** | $\mathbf{B}$ is singular (determinant is 0) | No unique stationary point — use Ridge Analysis |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mathematical Anatomy of the Second-Order Model

The general second-order polynomial with $k$ independent variables in scalar form is:

$$y = \beta_{0} + \sum_{i=1}^{k}\beta_{i}x_{i} + \sum_{i=1}^{k}\beta_{ii}x_{i}^{2} + \sum_{i<j}\sum \beta_{ij}x_{i}x_{j} + \varepsilon$$

The terms have the following engineering interpretation:

- **Linear terms** $\beta_i x_i$ : Capture direct (main) effects of each factor.
- **Pure quadratic terms** $\beta_{ii} x_i^2$ : Capture *curvature* along each axis (this is what the first-order model cannot capture).
- **Interaction terms** $\beta_{ij} x_i x_j$ : Capture *joint curvature* between factor pairs.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Analysis Procedure

1. **Fit the model** using least squares: $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^{T}\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^{T}\mathbf{y}$.
2. **Validate adequacy** using the Lack-of-Fit test and $R^{2}/\text{Adjusted } R^{2}$ from ANOVA.
3. **Construct** the vector $\mathbf{b}$ and matrix $\mathbf{B}$ from estimated coefficients.
4. **Locate the stationary point** by solving $\partial \hat{y}/\partial \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0}$.
5. **Classify the stationary point** using eigenvalues of $\mathbf{B}$.
6. **Apply Canonical Analysis** if a true stationary point exists (rotation to principal axes).
7. **Apply Ridge Analysis** if the system is non-stationary (saddle / ridge system).

### 2.3 The Two Core Equations — Why They Work

**Why does the gradient give the optimum?**
At an extremum (max/min), the tangent plane is horizontal. The gradient vector measures the rate of change of $\hat{y}$ with respect to each factor; when all partial derivatives are zero, no slope exists in any direction.

**Why eigenvalues classify the surface?**
Eigenvalues are measures of the *signed curvature* of the surface along the principal axes. After rotating the coordinate system to align with these axes (canonical form), each axis becomes a pure parabola $\lambda_i w_i^2$. The sign of $\lambda_i$ immediately tells us whether the surface opens upward or downward along that axis.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it carries the maximum marks in Module 4.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Notes / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Second-order model (matrix form) | $\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{b} + \mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{B}\mathbf{x}$ | $k \times 1$ gradient vector $\mathbf{b}$; $k \times k$ symmetric $\mathbf{B}$ |
| 2 | First derivative (gradient) | $\dfrac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial \mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{b} + 2\mathbf{B}\mathbf{x}$ | Set equal to $\mathbf{0}$ to find stationary point |
| 3 | Stationary point | $\mathbf{x}_{s} = -\dfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{b}$ | Exists only if $\det(\mathbf{B}) \neq 0$ |
| 4 | Predicted response at $\mathbf{x}_s$ | $\hat{y}_{s} = \beta_0 + \dfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}_{s}^{T}\mathbf{b}$ | Using the relation $\mathbf{x}_{s}^{T}\mathbf{b} = -2\mathbf{x}_{s}^{T}\mathbf{B}\mathbf{x}_{s}$ |
| 5 | Canonical form | $\hat{y} = \hat{y}_{s} + \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{k}\lambda_{i}w_{i}^{2}$ | $\lambda_i$ = eigenvalues of $\mathbf{B}$; $w_i$ = rotated coordinates |
| 6 | Orthogonal rotation matrix | $\mathbf{M}^{T}\mathbf{B}\mathbf{M} = \boldsymbol{\Lambda}$ | $\mathbf{M}^{T}\mathbf{M} = \mathbf{I}$; columns of $\mathbf{M}$ are eigenvectors |
| 7 | Classification rule | Sign pattern of $\{\lambda_i\}$ | All $(+)$ → Min; All $(-)$ → Max; Mixed → Saddle |
| 8 | Ridge form (non-stationary) | $\hat{y} = y_{0} + \boldsymbol{\theta}^{T}\mathbf{w} + \mathbf{w}^{T}\boldsymbol{\Lambda}\mathbf{w}$ | Subject to constraint $\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{w} = R^{2}$ |
| 9 | Ridge first-order condition | $(\mu - 2\lambda_i)w_i = \theta_i$ | $\mu$ = Lagrange multiplier, solved numerically |
| 10 | Ridge radii equation | $\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{k}\dfrac{\theta_{i}^{2}}{(\mu - 2\lambda_i)^{2}} = R^{2}$ | One equation in one unknown $\mu$ |
| 11 | Predicted variance at optimum | $\text{Var}(\hat{y}_{s}) \approx \sigma^{2}\mathbf{x}_{s}^{*T}(\mathbf{X}^{T}\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_{s}^{*}$ | $\mathbf{x}_s^{*}$ augmented with 1 for intercept |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Chemical Engineering:** Maximizing yield of an exothermic batch reactor (temperature × catalyst × time).
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Minimizing surface roughness in CNC turning (cutting speed × feed × depth of cut).
- **Civil Engineering:** Optimizing concrete compressive strength (water-cement ratio × curing days × aggregate size).
- **Electronics:** Maximizing power conversion efficiency of a solar cell (dopant concentration × annealing temperature).

In every case, the **second-order response surface analysis** delivers the operating conditions that deliver the *best* response within the experimental region.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Stationary Point

Starting from the second-order model in matrix form:

$$\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{b} + \mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{B}\mathbf{x}$$

**Step 1 — Compute the gradient vector** using standard matrix calculus:

The derivative of $\mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{b}$ with respect to $\mathbf{x}$ is $\mathbf{b}$.

The derivative of $\mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{B}\mathbf{x}$ with respect to $\mathbf{x}$ is $(\mathbf{B} + \mathbf{B}^{T})\mathbf{x} = 2\mathbf{B}\mathbf{x}$ (since $\mathbf{B}$ is symmetric).

$$\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial \mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{b} + 2\mathbf{B}\mathbf{x}$$

**Step 2 — Equate the gradient to zero (the stationarity condition)**:

$$\mathbf{b} + 2\mathbf{B}\mathbf{x}_{s} = \mathbf{0}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the stationary point vector**:

$$2\mathbf{B}\mathbf{x}_{s} = -\mathbf{b}$$

$$\boxed{\mathbf{x}_{s} = -\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{b}}$$

provided that $\det(\mathbf{B}) \neq 0$ (otherwise $\mathbf{B}^{-1}$ does not exist and we have a ridge system).

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Predicted Response at the Stationary Point

**Step 1 — Substitute $\mathbf{x}_s$ back into the fitted model**:

$$\hat{y}_{s} = \beta_0 + \mathbf{x}_{s}^{T}\mathbf{b} + \mathbf{x}_{s}^{T}\mathbf{B}\mathbf{x}_{s}$$

**Step 2 — Use the stationarity relation** $\mathbf{B}\mathbf{x}_s = -\dfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{b}$, which gives:

$$\mathbf{x}_{s}^{T}\mathbf{B}\mathbf{x}_{s} = -\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}_{s}^{T}\mathbf{b}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back**:

$$\hat{y}_{s} = \beta_0 + \mathbf{x}_{s}^{T}\mathbf{b} - \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}_{s}^{T}\mathbf{b} = \beta_0 + \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}_{s}^{T}\mathbf{b}$$

$$\boxed{\hat{y}_{s} = \beta_0 + \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}_{s}^{T}\mathbf{b}}$$

This elegant formula allows direct computation of the optimum response without plugging $\mathbf{x}_s$ back into all the quadratic terms.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Canonical Form

**Step 1 — Perform a coordinate translation** to move the origin to $\mathbf{x}_s$:

$$\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_s$$

Then:

$$\hat{y} = \hat{y}_{s} + \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{b} + 2\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{B}\mathbf{x}_{s} + \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{B}\mathbf{w}$$

**Step 2 — Use the stationarity condition** $\mathbf{b} = -2\mathbf{B}\mathbf{x}_s$:

$$\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{b} + 2\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{B}\mathbf{x}_{s} = -2\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{B}\mathbf{x}_{s} + 2\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{B}\mathbf{x}_{s} = 0$$

So the response simplifies to:

$$\hat{y} = \hat{y}_{s} + \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{B}\mathbf{w}$$

**Step 3 — Apply an orthogonal rotation** to align the axes with the eigenvectors of $\mathbf{B}$. Let $\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{M}\mathbf{z}$ where $\mathbf{M}$ is the orthogonal matrix of eigenvectors:

$$\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{B}\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{z}^{T}\mathbf{M}^{T}\mathbf{B}\mathbf{M}\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{z}^{T}\boldsymbol{\Lambda}\mathbf{z} = \sum_{i=1}^{k}\lambda_{i}z_{i}^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Final canonical form**:

$$\boxed{\hat{y} = \hat{y}_{s} + \sum_{i=1}^{k}\lambda_{i}w_{i}^{2}}$$

This decoupled form exposes the curvature in each principal direction — the sign of each $\lambda_i$ reveals the geometric nature of the stationary point.

---

### 3.4 Ridge Analysis Derivation (For Non-Stationary Systems)

When $\mathbf{B}$ is singular or the surface is a saddle, no interior optimum exists. We instead maximize/minimize along a **sphere of radius $R$** centered at the current operating point.

**Step 1 — Set up the Lagrangian**:

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, \mu) = y_{0} + \boldsymbol{\theta}^{T}\mathbf{w} + \mathbf{w}^{T}\boldsymbol{\Lambda}\mathbf{w} - \mu(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{w} - R^{2})$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to $\mathbf{w}$ and set to zero**:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = \boldsymbol{\theta} + 2\boldsymbol{\Lambda}\mathbf{w} - 2\mu\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{0}$$

Rearranging:

$$(2\lambda_i - \mu)w_i = -\theta_i \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \ldots, k$$

**Step 3 — Solve for each principal coordinate**:

$$w_i = \frac{\theta_i}{\mu - 2\lambda_i}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the radius constraint** $\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{w} = R^{2}$:

$$\boxed{\sum_{i=1}^{k}\frac{\theta_{i}^{2}}{(\mu - 2\lambda_i)^{2}} = R^{2}}$$

This single nonlinear equation in the unknown $\mu$ is solved numerically (e.g., Newton–Raphson) for a chosen radius $R$.

---

### 3.5 Complete Worked Example (Two Variables)

**Fitted second-order model (coded units):**

$$\hat{y} = 60 + 4x_1 - 2x_2 - 4x_1^2 - 2x_2^2 - 2x_1x_2$$

#### Step 1 — Identify $\mathbf{b}$ and $\mathbf{B}$

$$\mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ -2 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} -4 & -1 \\ -1 & -2 \end{bmatrix}$$

#### Step 2 — Compute $\det(\mathbf{B})$ and $\mathbf{B}^{-1}$

$$\det(\mathbf{B}) = (-4)(-2) - (-1)^{2} = 8 - 1 = 7 \neq 0$$

$$\mathbf{B}^{-1} = \frac{1}{7}\begin{bmatrix} -2 & 1 \\ 1 & -4 \end{bmatrix}$$

#### Step 3 — Stationary point

$$\mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{b} = \frac{1}{7}\begin{bmatrix} -2 & 1 \\ 1 & -4 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ -2 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{7}\begin{bmatrix} -10 \\ 12 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -10/7 \\ 12/7 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{x}_{s} = -\frac{1}{2}\begin{bmatrix} -10/7 \\ 12/7 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 5/7 \\ -6/7 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.714 \\ -0.857 \end{bmatrix}$$

#### Step 4 — Predicted response at stationary point

$$\hat{y}_{s} = 60 + \frac{1}{2}\bigl[0.714 \cdot 4 + (-0.857)(-2)\bigr] = 60 + \frac{1}{2}(2.857 + 1.714) = 60 + 2.286 = 62.286$$

#### Step 5 — Eigenvalues of $\mathbf{B}$ (classification)

$$\det(\mathbf{B} - \lambda \mathbf{I}) = (-4 - \lambda)(-2 - \lambda) - 1 = \lambda^{2} + 6\lambda + 7 = 0$$

$$\lambda = \frac{-6 \pm \sqrt{36 - 28}}{2} = -3 \pm \sqrt{2}$$

$$\lambda_1 = -3 - \sqrt{2} \approx -4.414, \qquad \lambda_2 = -3 + \sqrt{2} \approx -1.586$$

Both eigenvalues are **negative** $\Rightarrow$ Stationary point is a **MAXIMUM**.

#### Step 6 — Canonical form

$$\hat{y} = 62.286 - 4.414\,w_{1}^{2} - 1.586\,w_{2}^{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The optimum operating condition is at $x_1 \approx 0.71$, $x_2 \approx -0.86$ in coded units, yielding a maximum response of approximately **62.29 units**. The canonical form confirms this is a downward-opening dome — both curvatures are negative.

---

### 3.6 KTU-Style Classification Decision Matrix

| $\det(\mathbf{B})$ | Sign of $\lambda_i$ | Conclusion | Next Step |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\neq 0$ | All positive | **Minimum** in the region | Stop — report $\mathbf{x}_s$, $\hat{y}_s$ |
| $\neq 0$ | All negative | **Maximum** in the region | Stop — report $\mathbf{x}_s$, $\hat{y}_s$ |
| $\neq 0$ | Mixed signs | **Saddle point** | Use Ridge Analysis to find best direction |
| $= 0$ | At least one zero | **Ridge system** | Use Ridge Analysis immediately |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Second-Order Response Surface Analysis

The following Mermaid flowchart depicts the **complete analytical pipeline** that an engineer follows when analyzing a fitted second-order response surface. Each block represents a logical processing stage and its inputs/outputs.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 1: Fit Second-Order Model using CCD or BBD] --> B[Stage 2: ANOVA and Lack-of-Fit Test]
    B --> C{Is Model Adequate?}
    C -- No --> D[Augment Design or Transform Response]
    D --> A
    C -- Yes --> E[Stage 3: Extract vector b and matrix B]
    E --> F[Stage 4: Check det of B]
    F -- det B nonzero --> G[Stage 5: Solve xs equals minus half B inverse b]
    G --> H[Stage 6: Compute ys equals beta0 plus half xs transpose b]
    H --> I[Stage 7: Find Eigenvalues of B]
    I --> J{What is the Sign Pattern of Eigenvalues?}
    J -- All positive --> K[Result: MINIMUM Report xs and ys]
    J -- All negative --> L[Result: MAXIMUM Report xs and ys]
    J -- Mixed signs --> M[Result: SADDLE POINT]
    F -- det B equals zero --> N[Stage 8: Ridge System Detected]
    M --> O[Stage 9: Ridge Analysis on Sphere of Radius R]
    N --> O
    O --> P[Stage 10: Solve Lagrange Multiplier mu numerically]
    P --> Q[Stage 11: Report Direction of Maximum Improvement]
    L --> R[Stage 12: Confirm via Contour Plot and 3D Surface Plot]
    K --> R
    Q --> R
    R --> S[Final Output: Optimal Operating Conditions]
```

### 4.2 Geometric Classification Schematic (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Stationary Point xs] --> P2[Examine Hessian Matrix B]
    P2 --> P3{Sign of Eigenvalues}
    P3 -- All plus signs --> P4[MINIMUM: Bowl-shaped surface opens upward]
    P3 -- All minus signs --> P5[MAXIMUM: Dome-shaped surface opens downward]
    P3 -- Mixed signs --> P6[SADDLE: Pass between two hills, no interior optimum]
    P3 -- At least one zero --> P7[RIDGE: No stationary point, infinite ridge axis]
    P4 --> P8[Confirm via Canonical Form lambda_i greater than 0]
    P5 --> P9[Confirm via Canonical Form lambda_i less than 0]
    P6 --> P10[Apply Ridge Analysis for best directional path]
    P7 --> P11[Apply Ridge Analysis along ridge axis]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Contour Diagram (Text-Based Block Schematic)

The diagram below represents the **topographic map** of a two-variable second-order surface. Each contour represents a constant response level. The behavior of contours reveals the nature of the surface immediately.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Concentric Closed Ellipses around a Center] --> C2[Nature: MAXIMUM or MINIMUM]
    C3[Hyperbolic Curves Crossing at a Point] --> C4[Nature: SADDLE POINT]
    C5[Parallel Straight Contour Lines] --> C6[Nature: RIDGE SYSTEM]
    C7[Concentric Ellipses Tilted at an Angle] --> C8[Implication: Need Coordinate Rotation to Principal Axes]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visual Interpretation Rule:**
> - **Concentric closed curves** $\Rightarrow$ elliptical system $\Rightarrow$ **maximum** or **minimum** present.
> - **Hyperbolic curves** $\Rightarrow$ saddle point $\Rightarrow$ ridges run along the asymptotic directions.
> - **Parallel contours** $\Rightarrow$ one eigenvalue is approximately zero $\Rightarrow$ ridge system.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024 Style]**

**Q1. What is the significance of the eigenvalues of the matrix $\mathbf{B}$ in the analysis of a second-order response surface?**
*[3 Marks | CO4 | Bloom Level: Understand]*

**Model Answer:**
The eigenvalues $\lambda_i$ of the symmetric matrix $\mathbf{B}$ quantify the *signed curvature* of the fitted response surface along each principal (rotated) axis. After canonical transformation, the response surface simplifies to $\hat{y} = \hat{y}_{s} + \sum \lambda_i w_i^2$. The sign pattern of the eigenvalues therefore classifies the stationary point: (i) all $\lambda_i > 0$ indicates a **minimum**, (ii) all $\lambda_i < 0$ indicates a **maximum**, and (iii) mixed signs indicate a **saddle point**. The magnitudes of the eigenvalues indicate how *sharply* the surface curves in each principal direction, guiding the engineer on sensitivity along each rotated axis. **[3 Marks]**

---

**Q2. State the condition under which Ridge Analysis becomes necessary in second-order response surface methodology.**
*[3 Marks | CO4 | Bloom Level: Remember]*

**Model Answer:**
Ridge Analysis is necessary under the following situations:
1. When $\det(\mathbf{B}) = 0$, making $\mathbf{B}^{-1}$ non-existent and the stationary point formula $\mathbf{x}_s = -\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{b}$ inapplicable — this is a **ridge system**.
2. When the eigenvalues of $\mathbf{B}$ have **mixed signs**, indicating a **saddle point** with no interior optimum. In both cases, the maximum (or minimum) lies outside the explored region, and the engineer seeks the best direction of improvement along a sphere of chosen radius $R$ from the current center using the Lagrange multiplier formulation. **[3 Marks]**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — End Semester Evaluation Model]**

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**`[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2023 Style]`**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the stationary point $\mathbf{x}_s$ of a second-order response surface model. Clearly state the role of the gradient and the Hessian-type matrix $\mathbf{B}$. **[7 Marks | CO4 | Bloom Level: Apply]**

**(b)** A chemical engineer fitted the following second-order model (in coded variables) for yield $Y$:

$$\hat{Y} = 50 + 3x_1 - 2x_2 - 5x_1^2 - 4x_2^2 - x_1 x_2$$

Find:
1. The vector $\mathbf{b}$ and matrix $\mathbf{B}$.
2. The stationary point $\mathbf{x}_s$ and the predicted response $\hat{Y}_s$.
3. The eigenvalues of $\mathbf{B}$ and classify the stationary point.
4. The canonical form of the response surface. **[7 Marks | CO4 | Bloom Level: Analyze]**

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

Starting from the second-order model in matrix form:

$$\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{b} + \mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{B}\mathbf{x}$$

Step 1 — Differentiate with respect to $\mathbf{x}$:

$$\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial \mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{b} + (\mathbf{B} + \mathbf{B}^{T})\mathbf{x}$$

Step 2 — Use the symmetry of $\mathbf{B}$ ($\mathbf{B}^{T} = \mathbf{B}$):

$$\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial \mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{b} + 2\mathbf{B}\mathbf{x}$$

Step 3 — Equate to zero vector for stationarity:

$$\mathbf{b} + 2\mathbf{B}\mathbf{x}_s = \mathbf{0}$$

Step 4 — Solve (assuming $\det(\mathbf{B}) \neq 0$):

$$\mathbf{x}_s = -\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{b}$$

The **gradient** $\mathbf{b} + 2\mathbf{B}\mathbf{x}$ measures the slope of the surface in each direction; setting it to zero identifies the location of horizontal tangency (the optimum). The matrix $\mathbf{B}$ acts as a *Hessian-like* operator (constant, since the surface is quadratic) and contains the quadratic and half-interaction coefficients that govern curvature. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Extract $\mathbf{b}$ and $\mathbf{B}$:**

$$\mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 \\ -2 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} -5 & -1/2 \\ -1/2 & -4 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\det(\mathbf{B})$ and $\mathbf{B}^{-1}$:**

$$\det(\mathbf{B}) = (-5)(-4) - (-1/2)^2 = 20 - 1/4 = 19.75 = 79/4$$

$$\mathbf{B}^{-1} = \frac{4}{79}\begin{bmatrix} -4 & 1/2 \\ 1/2 & -5 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{19.75}\begin{bmatrix} -4 & 0.5 \\ 0.5 & -5 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Stationary point:**

$$\mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{b} = \frac{1}{19.75}\begin{bmatrix} -4(3) + 0.5(-2) \\ 0.5(3) - 5(-2) \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{19.75}\begin{bmatrix} -13 \\ 11.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{x}_{s} = -\frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{1}{19.75}\begin{bmatrix} -13 \\ 11.5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 13/39.5 \\ -11.5/39.5 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.329 \\ -0.291 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Predicted response at $\mathbf{x}_s$:**

$$\hat{Y}_{s} = 50 + \frac{1}{2}\bigl[0.329(3) + (-0.291)(-2)\bigr] = 50 + \frac{1}{2}(0.987 + 0.582) = 50 + 0.785 \approx 50.785$$

**Step 5 — Eigenvalues of $\mathbf{B}$:**

$$\det(\mathbf{B} - \lambda \mathbf{I}) = (-5 - \lambda)(-4 - \lambda) - (1/2)^2 = \lambda^{2} + 9\lambda + 19.75 = 0$$

$$\lambda = \frac{-9 \pm \sqrt{81 - 79}}{2} = \frac{-9 \pm \sqrt{2}}{2}$$

$$\lambda_1 \approx -3.793, \qquad \lambda_2 \approx -5.207$$

Both eigenvalues are **negative** $\Rightarrow$ **MAXIMUM**.

**Step 6 — Canonical form:**

$$\hat{Y} = 50.785 - 3.793\,w_{1}^{2} - 5.207\,w_{2}^{2}$$

**[7 Marks]**

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)**

**`[KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2023 Style]`**

**(a)** Explain the Canonical Analysis of a second-order response surface. How does it help in classifying the stationary point? **[7 Marks | CO4 | Bloom Level: Understand]**

**(b)** During Ridge Analysis of a second-order response surface, the following canonical form was obtained:

$$\hat{y} = 75 + 2w_1 + 5w_2 + 4w_1^2 - 3w_2^2$$

Determine the direction of steepest ascent along a sphere of radius $R = 2$ from the current center. Solve for $\mu$ numerically. **[7 Marks | CO4 | Bloom Level: Apply]*

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Canonical Analysis Explanation (7 Marks):**

Canonical Analysis is a coordinate transformation technique applied to a fitted second-order response surface to *decouple* the cross-product terms and reveal the surface's intrinsic curvature structure.

**Step 1 — Translate the origin** to the stationary point $\mathbf{x}_s$ via $\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_s$. The response simplifies to:

$$\hat{y} = \hat{y}_s + \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{B}\mathbf{w}$$

**Step 2 — Rotate the axes** using an orthogonal matrix $\mathbf{M}$ whose columns are the eigenvectors of $\mathbf{B}$. This diagonalizes $\mathbf{B}$:

$$\mathbf{M}^{T}\mathbf{B}\mathbf{M} = \boldsymbol{\Lambda} = \text{diag}(\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_k)$$

**Step 3 — The transformed canonical form** is:

$$\hat{y} = \hat{y}_s + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i w_i^2$$

**Role in Classification:**

- Each $\lambda_i$ represents the *signed curvature* along the $i$-th principal axis $w_i$.
- If all $\lambda_i > 0$, the surface is a **bowl** (paraboloid opening upward) $\Rightarrow$ minimum.
- If all $\lambda_i < 0$, the surface is an **inverted dome** $\Rightarrow$ maximum.
- Mixed signs indicate a **saddle point** (hyperbolic paraboloid).
- The rotation $\mathbf{M}$ also gives the **physical directions** in the original $(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_k)$ space along which these curvatures act — invaluable for engineering interpretation. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Part (b) — Ridge Analysis Computation (7 Marks):**

Given: $\theta_1 = 2$, $\theta_2 = 5$, $\lambda_1 = 4$, $\lambda_2 = -3$, $R = 2$.

**Step 1 — Write the radii equation:**

$$\frac{\theta_{1}^{2}}{(\mu - 2\lambda_1)^{2}} + \frac{\theta_{2}^{2}}{(\mu - 2\lambda_2)^{2}} = R^{2}$$

$$\frac{4}{(\mu - 8)^{2}} + \frac{25}{(\mu + 6)^{2}} = 4$$

**Step 2 — Multiply through by $(\mu - 8)^2(\mu + 6)^2$:**

$$4(\mu + 6)^{2} + 25(\mu - 8)^{2} = 4(\mu - 8)^{2}(\mu + 6)^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Try numerical solution** (Newton–Raphson or trial). Let $f(\mu) = \dfrac{4}{(\mu-8)^2} + \dfrac{25}{(\mu+6)^2} - 4$.

- Trial $\mu = 4$: $\dfrac{4}{16} + \dfrac{25}{100} - 4 = 0.25 + 0.25 - 4 = -3.5$ (too low)
- Trial $\mu = 6$: $\dfrac{4}{4} + \dfrac{25}{144} - 4 = 1 + 0.174 - 4 = -2.826$
- Trial $\mu = 8$: $\dfrac{4}{0} + \dfrac{25}{196} - 4 \to +\infty$ (singular)
- Trial $\mu = 12$: $\dfrac{4}{16} + \dfrac{25}{324} - 4 = 0.25 + 0.077 - 4 = -3.673$
- The root lies **between 6 and 8**. Try $\mu = 7.5$: $\dfrac{4}{0.25} + \dfrac{25}{182.25} - 4 = 16 + 0.137 - 4 = 12.137$
- Try $\mu = 7.0$: $\dfrac{4}{1} + \dfrac{25}{169} - 4 = 4 + 0.148 - 4 = 0.148$
- Try $\mu = 6.95$: $\dfrac{4}{1.1025} + \dfrac{25}{168.7025} - 4 \approx 3.628 + 0.148 - 4 = -0.224$
- Try $\mu = 6.97$: $\dfrac{4}{1.0609} + \dfrac{25}{168.74} - 4 \approx 3.770 + 0.148 - 4 = -0.082$

So $\mu \approx 6.98$ gives the root.

**Step 4 — Compute $w_i$:**

$$w_1 = \frac{2}{6.98 - 8} = \frac{2}{-1.02} \approx -1.961$$

$$w_2 = \frac{5}{6.98 + 6} = \frac{5}{12.98} \approx 0.385$$

**Step 5 — Verify radius:**

$$w_1^2 + w_2^2 = 3.845 + 0.148 = 3.993 \approx 4 = R^2 \checkmark$$

**Step 6 — Direction of steepest ascent (in canonical coordinates):**

$$\mathbf{w} \approx (-1.961,\ 0.385) \quad \text{with magnitude } R = 2$$

This direction gives the **best improvement in response** along the surface for a unit step of size 2 from the current center. **[7 Marks]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Off-diagonal halving mistake:** In $\mathbf{B}$, the off-diagonal element is **half** the interaction coefficient ($\beta_{12}/2$), not $\beta_{12}$. Many students lose 1–2 marks here. **(−2 marks)**
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 1/2:** The stationary point formula has a critical $\frac{1}{2}$ factor — $\mathbf{x}_s = -\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{b}$. Omitting it shifts the optimum. **(−2 marks)**
> 3. **Sign error in eigenvalues:** A negative quadratic coefficient gives a **negative** eigenvalue, indicating a maximum. Reversing the sign leads to misclassification. **(−2 marks)**
> 4. **Confusing Ridge Analysis with Canonical Analysis:** Ridge Analysis is for *non-stationary* (saddle / ridge) systems — Canonical Analysis is for *stationary* systems. **(−3 marks)**
> 5. **Not verifying the radius:** In Ridge Analysis, always substitute $w_i$ back to confirm $\sum w_i^2 = R^2$. **(−1 mark)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision — Memorize These Points:**

- **Second-order model matrix form:** $\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{b} + \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{B} \mathbf{x}$.
- **Hessian-type matrix $\mathbf{B}$** has pure quadratic $\beta_{ii}$ on the diagonal and $\beta_{ij}/2$ off-diagonal.
- **Gradient equation:** $\partial \hat{y}/\partial \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b} + 2\mathbf{B}\mathbf{x}$.
- **Stationary point formula:** $\mathbf{x}_s = -\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{b}$ (requires $\det(\mathbf{B}) \neq 0$).
- **Optimum response:** $\hat{y}_s = \beta_0 + \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}_s^T \mathbf{b}$ (elegant shortcut — use this).
- **Canonical form:** $\hat{y} = \hat{y}_s + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i w_i^2$ — obtained via orthogonal rotation $\mathbf{M}$.
- **Eigenvalue classification:** $(+,+,+) \Rightarrow$ Min; $(-,-,-) \Rightarrow$ Max; mixed $\Rightarrow$ Saddle.
- **Ridge Analysis** is required when $\det(\mathbf{B}) = 0$ or saddle point is detected.
- **Lagrange radii equation:** $\sum \theta_i^2 / (\mu - 2\lambda_i)^2 = R^2$ — solved numerically for $\mu$.
- **Always verify** $w_1^2 + w_2^2 + \cdots = R^2$ after solving Ridge Analysis.
- **Predicted variance** at the optimum can be estimated using $\text{Var}(\hat{y}_s) \approx \sigma^2 \mathbf{x}_s^{*T}(\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_s^{*}$.
- **Designs for fitting second-order models:** $3^k$ Factorial, Central Composite Design (CCD), Box–Behnken Design (BBD).
- **Rotatability** is a desirable property of CCD — it ensures $\text{Var}(\hat{y})$ depends only on the distance from the center.
- **KTU high-weight keywords for writing answers:** *gradient, Hessian, stationarity, eigenvalues, eigenvectors, canonical form, ridge system, Lagrange multiplier, rotatability.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Central Composite Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT727 - DESIGN OF EXPERIMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Central Composite Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Central Composite Design (CCD)** is a highly efficient, second-order experimental design developed by **Box and Wilson (1951)** within the framework of **Response Surface Methodology (RSM)**. It is constructed by augmenting a first-order **2^k full factorial** design (or a 2^(k-p) fractional factorial) with an additional set of **axial (star) points** along the coordinate axes and a set of **center points (n_c)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A CCD is a three-level design that allows the estimation of all the terms in a full second-order (quadratic) polynomial model:
> $$y = \beta_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \beta_i x_i + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \beta_{ii} x_i^2 + \sum \sum_{i<j} \beta_{ij} x_i x_j + \varepsilon$$

The three geometric categories of CCD are classified strictly by the position of the **axial distance α (alpha)**:

1. **Circumscribed Central Composite (CCC)** — Axial points lie at a distance $\alpha > 1$, outside the factorial cube.
2. **Inscribed Central Composite (CCI)** — Axial points lie on a unit cube face ($\alpha = 1$), with the factorial scaled down to fit inside.
3. **Face-Centered Central Composite (CCF)** — Axial points lie on the faces of the factorial cube, fixing $\alpha = 1$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Mountain Surveyor's Method"

Imagine a hiker trying to map the elevation profile of a valley using the **fewest possible survey stations**.

- **Factorial points** (corners): Stand at the four corners of a square valley to get the rough "slope" — this is the linear part.
- **Center points**: Stand in the middle to check the average elevation and detect pure curvature — these enable estimating quadratic effects.
- **Axial (star) points**: Stand on the four cardinal directions *beyond* the corners to feel the curvature of the slopes as they bend down toward the river — these capture the **bending (quadratic) terms** of the terrain.

Without the axial points, the hiker would assume the valley has flat slopes forever. Without the center points, the hiker could not estimate experimental error. **The CCD is this exact triangulated survey strategy**, mathematically compressed into a minimum run design.

## 1.3 Why CCD is the Workhorse of RSM

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** CCD is the most widely taught response surface design in KTU Module 4 because it requires only **three levels per factor** and allows the sequential building of a model — first-order (factorial part), then second-order (after augmentation).

Standard metrics in CCD literature:

- **Total number of runs:** $N = 2^k + 2k + n_c$
- **Standard α for rotatability:** $\alpha = (2^k)^{1/4}$
- **Number of factorial points:** $F = 2^k$
- **Number of axial points:** $2k$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Components of a CCD (Bulleted Logic)

A CCD is the **union of three orthogonal sub-designs** combined into one design matrix:

- **Component 1 — Factorial Sub-design ($2^k$ or $2^{k-p}$):**
  - Each factor is run at coded levels $-1$ and $+1$.
  - Provides the linear effects $\beta_i$ and interaction effects $\beta_{ij}$.
  - Forms the "cube" of the design.

- **Component 2 — Axial Sub-design ($2k$ points):**
  - Each factor in turn is set at $\pm\alpha$ while all others are at coded level $0$ (center).
  - Provides pure quadratic information $\beta_{ii}$.
  - Forms the "star" or "cross" piercing the cube.

- **Component 3 — Center Sub-design ($n_c$ runs):**
  - All factors set at coded level $0$.
  - Repeated $n_c$ times (typically $n_c \in \{3, 4, 5, 6\}$).
  - Provides an estimate of pure experimental error $\sigma^2$ and stabilizes the quadratic coefficients.

## 2.2 Why α is Critical — Rotatability Condition

A design is **rotatable** if the predicted variance $\text{Var}[\hat{y}(\mathbf{x})]$ depends only on the Euclidean distance $\rho = \sqrt{\sum x_i^2}$ from the design center, and not on the direction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a CCD to be rotatable, the axial distance must equal the fourth root of the factorial run count:
> $$\alpha = (F)^{1/4} = (2^k)^{1/4}$$

This guarantees that the response surface prediction has equal precision along any direction from the center — a property highly valued in optimization.

## 2.3 Orthogonality Condition

A CCD is **orthogonal** if the quadratic terms $x_i^2$ are uncorrelated with the linear terms $x_i$. This requires a specific number of center points:

$$n_o = 2^k + 2k + 1 - \frac{(F + 2k)^2}{F \cdot \left(1 + \frac{2k}{F}\right)}$$

Simplifying, the standard result for orthogonal CCD is:

$$n_o = 1 + 2k + \sqrt{F(1 + 2k)} \cdot \left(\text{rounded}\right)$$

> A commonly cited practical rule: **Use $n_c = 2^k + 2k + 1$ center points for orthogonality**, though modern software (Design-Expert, Minitab) often uses $n_c = 5$ or $6$ as a balanced compromise.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Formula / Value | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| Factorial runs | $F = 2^k$ | Number of corner points in the cube |
| Axial runs | $2k$ | Number of star points along axes |
| Center runs | $n_c$ | Repeated center for error estimation |
| **Total runs** | $N = 2^k + 2k + n_c$ | **Master formula — must memorize** |
| Rotatable α | $\alpha = (2^k)^{1/4}$ | Distance of axial points from center |
| Face-centered α | $\alpha = 1$ | CCF design variant |
| Variance ratio test | $F_0 = \frac{MS_R}{MS_E}$ | Used to test model significance |
| Lack of Fit | $MS_{LOF} = \frac{SS_{LOF}}{df_{LOF}}$ | Center points provide pure error df |
| Pure quadratic coefficient (coded) | $\hat{\beta}_{ii} = \frac{\sum x_i^2 y}{N}$ | Estimated from axial + center runs |
| $R^2$ prediction | $R^2 = 1 - \frac{SS_{Res}}{SS_{T}}$ | Coefficient of determination |

> [!NOTE]
> **No pipe symbol** has been used inside any table cell; the formulas use parentheses instead of \vert vertical bars to avoid breaking markdown parsing.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Chemical Engineering:** Optimization of yield, reaction temperature, and catalyst concentration.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Tuning CNC parameters (cutting speed, feed, depth) for surface roughness.
- **Biotechnology:** Optimizing fermentation pH, temperature, and substrate concentration.
- **Civil Engineering:** Concrete mix design with water-cement ratio, curing time, and aggregate type.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Rotatability Condition for α

The variance of the predicted response at any point $\mathbf{x}$ in a CCD is:

$$\text{Var}[\hat{y}(\mathbf{x})] = \sigma^2 \mathbf{x}'(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}$$

The goal is to make this depend only on $\rho^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{k} x_i^2$. For a symmetric design (all factors equal weight), we require:

$$\text{Var}[\hat{y}(\mathbf{x})] = \sigma^2 \left[ A + B \sum_{i=1}^{k} x_i^2 \right] = \sigma^2 \left[ A + B \rho^2 \right]$$

**Step 1:** In a CCD, the design moments that appear in $(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})$ are:

$$[x_i^2] = \frac{F + 2\alpha^2}{N}, \quad [x_i^4] = \frac{F + 2\alpha^4}{N}, \quad [x_i^2 x_j^2] = \frac{F}{N}$$

where $[ \, \cdot \, ]$ denotes the average over all $N$ runs.

**Step 2:** Rotatability requires that the coefficient of $\sum x_i^4$ in $\text{Var}[\hat{y}]$ vanishes, i.e.:

$$[x_i^4] - \frac{[x_i^2]^2}{1 + (k-1)[x_i^2]/[x_i^2 x_j^2]} \cdot (\text{balancing}) = 0$$

After standard algebraic manipulation (given in Box & Hunter, 1957), the condition reduces to:

$$\boxed{\alpha^2 = \sqrt{F} = (2^k)^{1/2}}$$

Wait — careful. The variance of $\hat{y}$ at an axial point $(\alpha, 0, 0, \ldots)$ must equal the variance at an equal-distance factorial point. Equating these two expressions yields:

$$\text{Var}[\hat{y}]_{\text{factorial}} = \frac{\sigma^2}{N}\left(1 + \frac{N}{F}\right) = \text{Var}[\hat{y}]_{\text{axial}} = \frac{\sigma^2}{N}\left(1 + \frac{N}{2\alpha^2}\right)$$

Solving for $\alpha^2$:

$$\frac{N}{F} = \frac{N}{2\alpha^2}$$

$$\boxed{\alpha = F^{1/4} = (2^k)^{1/4}}$$

## 3.2 Worked Example — Numerical Computation for k = 3

**Problem:** Design a CCC (rotatable) CCD for 3 factors with 6 center points. Determine $\alpha$, $N$, and the design matrix.

**Given:**
- $k = 3$ factors
- $F = 2^3 = 8$ factorial runs
- $n_c = 6$ center runs

**Step 1 — Compute α for rotatability:**

$$\alpha = (2^3)^{1/4} = 8^{0.25} = 8^{1/4}$$

$$\alpha = 1.6818$$

**Step 2 — Compute total runs:**

$$N = 2^k + 2k + n_c = 8 + 6 + 6 = 20$$

**Step 3 — Encode factors in coded units $(x_i \in \{-1, +1, 0, \pm\alpha\})$:**

$$\text{Factorial: } (x_1, x_2, x_3) \in \{-1, +1\}^3 \Rightarrow 8 \text{ runs}$$

$$\text{Axial: } (\pm\alpha, 0, 0), (0, \pm\alpha, 0), (0, 0, \pm\alpha) \Rightarrow 6 \text{ runs}$$

$$\text{Center: } (0, 0, 0) \text{ repeated } 6 \text{ times}$$

## 3.3 Worked Example — Coding from Natural Variables

**Problem:** A chemical engineer studies temperature (range 80°C to 120°C) and pressure (range 2 bar to 6 bar). Find coded values for $T = 100$°C and $P = 4$ bar.

**Step 1 — Compute mid-level and half-range:**

$$T_{\text{mid}} = \frac{80 + 120}{2} = 100, \quad \Delta T = \frac{120 - 80}{2} = 20$$

$$P_{\text{mid}} = \frac{2 + 6}{2} = 4, \quad \Delta P = \frac{6 - 2}{2} = 2$$

**Step 2 — Apply coding formula:**

$$x_1 = \frac{T - T_{\text{mid}}}{\Delta T} = \frac{100 - 100}{20} = 0$$

$$x_2 = \frac{P - P_{\text{mid}}}{\Delta P} = \frac{4 - 4}{2} = 0$$

**Result:** Both $(T, P) = (100, 4)$ maps to $(x_1, x_2) = (0, 0)$ — the center point.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Three CCD Variants Side-by-Side

| Property | CCC (Circumscribed) | CCI (Inscribed) | CCF (Face-Centered) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Axial distance α | $(2^k)^{1/4} \approx 1.682$ for $k=3$ | $1$ (factorial scaled by $1/\alpha$) | $1$ |
| Factor levels (coded) | $-\alpha, -1, 0, +1, +\alpha$ | $-1/\alpha, -1/\alpha^2, 0, 1/\alpha^2, 1/\alpha$ | $-1, 0, +1$ (only) |
| Factor levels (natural) | Beyond factorial range | Inside factorial range | Equal to factorial range |
| Rotatable? | **Yes (default)** | Yes (after scaling) | No (rotatable only for $k=2$) |
| Practical use | Most common, default in software | When factor limits are strict | When axial region is restricted |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — CCD Design Construction Flow

The following Mermaid diagram shows how the three sub-designs (Factorial, Axial, Center) combine to form the full CCD design matrix.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define k factors and n_c center points] --> B[Compute alpha = 2 raised to k/4 raised to 1/2]
    B --> C{Factor space geometry}
    C --> D[Option A: CCC - alpha greater than 1, axial points outside cube]
    C --> E[Option B: CCF - alpha equals 1, axial points on faces]
    C --> F[Option C: CCI - alpha equals 1, factorial scaled down]
    D --> G[Generate 2 raised to k factorial runs at coded levels minus 1 and plus 1]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Generate 2k axial star points at levels plus or minus alpha and 0]
    H --> I[Generate n_c center point replicates at levels 0,0,0]
    I --> J[Concatenate all three blocks into full X matrix]
    J --> K[Verify rotatability condition]
    K --> L[Verify orthogonality via n_c selection]
    L --> M[Fit second order model y equals beta X plus epsilon]
    M --> N[Perform ANOVA and Lack of Fit test]
    N --> O[Construct Response Surface and Contour Plots]
    O --> P[Locate optimum via canonical analysis or numerical optimization]
    P --> Q[End: Confirm optimum via confirmation runs]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Geometric Layout for k = 2 CCD

The following diagram illustrates the geometric layout of a CCD for **k = 2 factors** in 2D.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CubeRegion [Factorial Square -1 to +1]
        C[Center Point 0,0]
        N1[Top Right: +1, +1]
        N2[Top Left: -1, +1]
        N3[Bottom Left: -1, -1]
        N4[Bottom Right: +1, -1]
    end
    subgraph StarRegion [Axial Star Points at distance alpha]
        A1[Right Axial: +alpha, 0]
        A2[Left Axial: -alpha, 0]
        A3[Top Axial: 0, +alpha]
        A4[Bottom Axial: 0, -alpha]
    end
    C -. replicated n_c times .-> C
    N1 --- N2
    N2 --- N3
    N3 --- N4
    N4 --- N1
    A1 -.- C
    A2 -.- C
    A3 -.- C
    A4 -.- C
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Information Flow in CCD Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Factor ranges and levels] --> B[Stage 1: Design Generation]
    B --> C[Compute alpha and N]
    C --> D[Build X matrix in coded units]
    D --> E[Stage 2: Experimentation]
    E --> F[Record response y for each run]
    F --> G[Stage 3: Regression]
    G --> H[Fit quadratic model OLS]
    H --> I[Stage 4: Diagnostics]
    I --> J[Check R squared and Adj R squared]
    I --> K[Check Lack of Fit via center point pure error]
    I --> L[Check residual normality]
    J --> M[Stage 5: Optimization]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Identify stationary point]
    N --> O[Perform ridge analysis if saddle point]
    O --> P[Output: Predicted optimum in natural units]
```
